> 4. **Not Defining $z$ explicitly (–1 mark):** Always state the assumed service level (e.g., 95% → $z = 1.65$) and the holding rate $h$ in numerical answers.
> 5. **Ignoring Units in Final Answer (–1 mark):** Always write "₹/year" or "units" explicitly in the final answer box.
> 6. **Writing "Leagile" as "Legile" or "Legaile" (–0.5 mark):** Spelling matters in formal KTU answer scripts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic: Leagile**

- ✅ **Leagile = Lean (upstream) + Agile (downstream)** combined via a **Decoupling Point (DP)**.
- ✅ **Lean** = Efficiency, waste elimination, smooth flow, push-based, forecast-driven. (8 wastes: **DOWNTIME**)
- ✅ **Agile** = Responsiveness, flexibility, pull-based, order-driven, market-sensitive.
- ✅ **Decoupling Point (DP)** = Strategic inventory location + Push/Pull boundary.
- ✅ **Postponement** = Delaying differentiation to defer risk. Two types: **Form** and **Time**.
- ✅ The DP migrates **upstream** as the product moves from Introduction → Maturity in the PLC.
- ✅ The optimal DP minimizes $TC_i = H_i + R_i$, where $H_i$ is annual holding cost and $R_i$ is annual responsiveness cost.
- ✅ Key equations to memorize:
  - $SS_i = z \cdot \sigma_0 \cdot k_i \cdot \sqrt{L_i}$
  - $H_i = SS_i \cdot h \cdot c_i$
  - $R_i = \alpha \cdot L_{down,i} \cdot D_{op}$
- ✅ Real-world exemplars: **Zara** (apparel), **Benetton** (undyed garments), **Dell** (built-to-order PCs), **HP printers** (region-specific packaging).
- ✅ KTU 2024 scheme marks this topic as a **14-mark Part B question** topic with a strong likelihood of numerical sub-parts.
- ✅ Always show units in every calculation step. **No marks** for unit-less final answers.
- ✅ Industrial examples should be from **Kerala-based industries** if possible (e.g., **KSRTC spare parts** supply chain, **Coir industry**, **Spice export chain** from Idukki/Wayanad) to score extra "context" marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resilient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET524 - LOGISTICS & SUPPLY CHAIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Resilient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Supply Chain Resilience (SCR)** is formally defined as the *adaptive capability* of a supply chain network to prepare for, withstand, recover from, and evolve in the face of unexpected disruptive events, thereby preserving business continuity, customer service levels, and financial performance.

The term **"Resilient Supply Chain"** is derived from the Latin root *resilire* (to spring back). In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, resilience is not merely a defensive posture but a **proactive strategic design philosophy** that integrates risk anticipation with rapid operational recovery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Module 1 Highlight):**
> A Resilient Supply Chain is one that demonstrates the ability to **return to its original operational state (or a more desirable state)** within an acceptable post-disruption timeframe, while maintaining the structural integrity of its upstream and downstream value streams.

The three operational pillars of resilience (per the KTU syllabus) are:

1. **Anticipation (Pre-Disruption):** Risk identification and vulnerability mapping.
2. **Resistance (During Disruption):** Absorbing the shock without catastrophic failure.
3. **Recovery (Post-Disruption):** Rapid restoration of service and inventory flows.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The "First-Time Reader" View)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Immune System:**
> Think of a supply chain as the human body. A **non-resilient supply chain** is like a person with a weak immune system — even a common cold (a minor port delay) can put them in the hospital (production halt). A **resilient supply chain** is like a person with a strong immune system — the same cold is fought off quickly (inventory buffer kicks in, alternate supplier activates), and the body returns to full strength within days.

**Another Simple Analogy — The Bamboo Tree:**
Unlike an oak tree that snaps in a storm, a **bamboo tree bends with the wind and springs back**. This is the essence of resilience: **flexibility under stress + rapid recovery**.

### Key Intuitive Takeaways:
- Resilience $\neq$ Avoiding Disruptions (you cannot stop a pandemic or an earthquake).
- Resilience $=$ **Minimizing the impact + Accelerating the bounce-back**.
- A resilient chain is **"anti-fragile"** — it learns from disruptions and emerges stronger.

---

## 1.3 Standard KTU Metrics (Bolded Constants & Indicators)

| Symbol | Metric | Standard Unit | Significance |
|:------:|:------:|:-------------:|:------------|
| **TTR** | Time to Recover | Hours / Days | Speed of operational restoration |
| **RPO** | Recovery Point Objective | Hours | Maximum acceptable data/inventory loss |
| **RTO** | Recovery Time Objective | Hours | Target time to resume full operations |
| **SCRI** | Supply Chain Resilience Index | % (0–100) | Composite resilience score |
| **CVaR** | Conditional Value at Risk | ₹ / USD | Financial loss in worst-case scenarios |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolded Constants for KTU Examinations:** The most frequently tested metrics are **TTR (Time to Recover)** and **SCRI (Supply Chain Resilience Index)**. Memorize the formulas in the next section.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The 4R Framework of Supply Chain Resilience (Christopher & Peck Model)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **4R Framework** as the core theoretical model. Each "R" addresses a distinct phase of the disruption lifecycle.

### R1 — Robustness (Pre-Disruption Strength)
- **Definition:** The inherent strength of the supply chain to withstand a disruption without degradation in performance.
- **How it works:** Achieved through **supplier diversification, safety stock buffers, and geographic dispersion** of facilities.
- **Why it matters:** Reduces the *initial shock magnitude* to the network.

### R2 — Redundancy (Strategic Spare Capacity)
- **Definition:** The strategic duplication of critical supply chain resources (multiple suppliers, multi-modal transport, parallel warehouses).
- **Trade-off:** Redundancy $\uparrow$ $\Rightarrow$ Cost $\uparrow$ but Resilience $\uparrow$.
- **Engineering Utility:** Analogous to **N+1 redundancy** in power grid engineering.

### R3 — Resourcefulness (Adaptive Capability)
- **Definition:** The ability to mobilize human, financial, and informational resources to respond effectively to a disruption.
- **Achieved via:** Real-time dashboards, cross-trained workforce, scenario planning drills.
- **Why it matters:** Even robust systems fail without skilled operators to execute recovery.

### R4 — Recovery (Restoration Speed)
- **Definition:** The capacity to return to normal (or improved) operational performance post-disruption.
- **Measured by:** **TTR (Time to Recover)** and **Service Level Recovery Curve**.
- **Real-world utility:** Post-COVID-19 recovery of semiconductor and pharmaceutical supply chains.

---

## 2.2 Resilience vs. Efficiency — The Fundamental Trade-off

| Parameter | Efficient Supply Chain | Resilient Supply Chain |
|:---------:|:---------------------:|:----------------------:|
| **Cost** | Low (Lean) | Higher (Buffers) |
| **Lead Time** | Short | Slightly Longer |
| **Inventory** | JIT (Just-in-Time) | JIC (Just-in-Case) |
| **Supplier Base** | Single (Best Cost) | Multiple (Best Risk) |
| **Focus** | Cost Minimization | Disruption Survival |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Efficiency** with **Resilience**. An ultra-efficient JIT system is **inherently fragile**. Resilience requires deliberate **strategic slack**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are tested regularly in KTU ESE (End Semester Examinations). Master the units, boundary conditions, and the $R(t)$ performance curve.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|:-:|:-------------|:------------------------|:-----:|:-----------------|
| 1 | **Supply Chain Resilience Index (SCRI)** | $SCRI = \dfrac{\int_{0}^{T} P_{actual}(t) \, dt}{\int_{0}^{T} P_{target}(t) \, dt} \times 100$ | % | $0 \leq SCRI \leq 100$; $100 \Rightarrow$ Perfect Resilience |
| 2 | **Performance Degradation Ratio** | $PDR = \dfrac{P_{normal} - P_{disrupted}}{P_{normal}}$ | Dimensionless | $0 \leq PDR \leq 1$ |
| 3 | **Time to Recover (TTR)** | $TTR = t_{recovery} - t_{disruption}$ | Hours / Days | Lower is better |
| 4 | **Recovery Slope** | $RS = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta t}$ | %/hour | Steeper slope $=$ Faster recovery |
| 5 | **Risk Exposure (RE)** | $RE = P_{event} \times I_{impact}$ | ₹ / USD | $P$ $=$ Probability, $I$ $=$ Impact |
| 6 | **Expected Loss (EL)** | $EL = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times L_i$ | ₹ / USD | Sum over all $n$ risk events |
| 7 | **Service Level (Resilient)** | $SL_{R} = 1 - \dfrac{Stockouts_{disruption}}{TotalOrders}$ | Ratio | Target: $\geq 0.95$ |
| 8 | **Buffer Stock for Resilience** | $BS = Z_{\alpha} \times \sigma_{D} \times \sqrt{L} + S_{disruption}$ | Units | $S_{disruption}$ is the **strategic disruption buffer** |

> **Notation Safeguard:** In all formulas, $\sigma$ represents standard deviation, $Z_{\alpha}$ is the standard normal variate for service level $\alpha$, $L$ is lead time, and $D$ is demand.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Resilience Modeling

- **Aerospace & Defense:** Resilience modeling ensures mission-critical spare parts availability under combat/logistics disruptions.
- **Pharmaceutical Cold Chain:** Resilience metrics protect temperature-sensitive vaccines (e.g., COVID-19 distribution).
- **Maritime Logistics:** Resilience frameworks guide port selection under typhoon/hurricane risk in Kerala's coastal operations.
- **E-Commerce:** Amazon's "**Two-Pizza Team + Multi-Region Redundancy**" architecture is a direct application of the 4R framework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Supply Chain Resilience Index (SCRI)

The **SCRI** is a time-weighted performance metric. We derive it from first principles.

### Step 1: Define the Performance Functions
Let $P_{target}(t)$ be the desired (target) performance trajectory of the supply chain over the time window $[0, T]$, and $P_{actual}(t)$ be the realized performance, which dips during a disruption.

$$
P_{target}(t) = P_0 \quad \text{(constant normal-state performance)}
$$

$$
P_{actual}(t) = \begin{cases} P_0, & 0 \leq t < t_d \\ P_d, & t_d \leq t < t_r \\ P_0, & t_r \leq t \leq T \end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $P_0$ $=$ Normal performance level.
- $P_d$ $=$ Degraded performance during disruption ($P_d < P_0$).
- $t_d$ $=$ Time of disruption onset.
- $t_r$ $=$ Time of full recovery.

### Step 2: Set Up the Integral Ratio
The **resilience index** is the ratio of the *area under the actual performance curve* to the *area under the target performance curve* over the same time window.

$$
SCRI = \frac{\text{Area under } P_{actual}(t)}{\text{Area under } P_{target}(t)} \times 100
$$

### Step 3: Expand the Integrals
Substituting the piecewise functions into the integrals:

$$
\int_{0}^{T} P_{actual}(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{t_d} P_0 \, dt + \int_{t_d}^{t_r} P_d \, dt + \int_{t_r}^{T} P_0 \, dt
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{T} P_{target}(t) \, dt = P_0 \cdot T
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate Each Integral
Expanding the three sub-integrals (assuming constant $P_0$ and $P_d$ within each interval):

$$
\int_{0}^{t_d} P_0 \, dt = P_0 \cdot t_d
$$

$$
\int_{t_d}^{t_r} P_d \, dt = P_d \cdot (t_r - t_d)
$$

$$
\int_{t_r}^{T} P_0 \, dt = P_0 \cdot (T - t_r)
$$

### Step 5: Combine the Sub-Integrals
Summing the three components:

$$
\int_{0}^{T} P_{actual}(t) \, dt = P_0 \cdot t_d + P_d \cdot (t_r - t_d) + P_0 \cdot (T - t_r)
$$

$$
= P_0 \cdot (t_d + T - t_r) + P_d \cdot (t_r - t_d)
$$

### Step 6: Formulate the Final SCRI Expression
Substituting back into the ratio:

$$
SCRI = \frac{P_0 \cdot (T - (t_r - t_d)) + P_d \cdot (t_r - t_d)}{P_0 \cdot T} \times 100
$$

Letting $\tau = t_r - t_d$ denote the **recovery duration**, and $\rho = P_d / P_0$ denote the **degradation ratio**:

$$
SCRI = \left[ 1 - \frac{\tau}{T} (1 - \rho) \right] \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Working Equation (Exam-Ready):**
> $$\boxed{SCRI = \left[ 1 - \dfrac{\tau}{T} (1 - \rho) \right] \times 100}$$
> where $\tau$ $=$ disruption duration, $T$ $=$ total observation window, $\rho$ $=$ ratio of disrupted to normal performance.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Evaluation of Resilience Strategies

The following table is a **high-yield KTU analytical matrix** used to compare resilience-building strategies across multiple dimensions. This format is frequently tested in **Part B (14-mark)** questions.

| Strategy | 4R Phase | Cost Impact (₹) | Lead Time Effect | Resilience Gain | KTU Real-World Example |
|:---------|:--------:|:---------------:|:----------------:|:---------------:|:-----------------------|
| **Multi-Sourcing** | Redundancy | Medium-High | Neutral | High | Apple sourcing chips from TSMC + Samsung |
| **Safety Stock Buffer** | Robustness | High (Carrying Cost) | Neutral | Medium-High | FMCG firms during monsoon disruptions |
| **Nearshoring** | Robustness + Recovery | High | Reduces | Very High | Post-COVID furniture firms shifting from China to Vietnam |
| **Digital Twin Simulation** | Resourcefulness | Low (Software) | Neutral | Medium | DHL's warehouse digital twins |
| **Dual Sourcing (70-30)** | Redundancy | Medium | Neutral | Medium | Automotive Tier-1 suppliers |
| **Supplier Collaboration Portals** | Resourcefulness | Low | Reduces | Medium | Walmart's Retail Link system |
| **Postponement Strategy** | Recovery | Low | Variable | High | Dell's configure-to-order model |
| **Geographic Diversification** | Robustness | High | Increases | Very High | Kerala spices sourcing from multiple states |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** A supply chain operates at a normal performance level of $P_0 = 1000$ units/day. A disruption occurs at $t_d = 0$ and lasts for $\tau = 10$ days, during which performance drops to $P_d = 400$ units/day. The total observation window is $T = 60$ days. Calculate the **SCRI**.

### Step 1: Identify Given Values
- $P_0 = 1000$ units/day
- $P_d = 400$ units/day
- $\tau = 10$ days
- $T = 60$ days

### Step 2: Compute the Degradation Ratio
$$
\rho = \frac{P_d}{P_0} = \frac{400}{1000} = 0.4
$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the SCRI Formula
$$
SCRI = \left[ 1 - \frac{\tau}{T} (1 - \rho) \right] \times 100
$$

$$
SCRI = \left[ 1 - \frac{10}{60} (1 - 0.4) \right] \times 100
$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Expression
$$
SCRI = \left[ 1 - \frac{10}{60} \times 0.6 \right] \times 100
$$

$$
SCRI = \left[ 1 - 0.1667 \times 0.6 \right] \times 100
$$

$$
SCRI = \left[ 1 - 0.1 \right] \times 100 = 0.9 \times 100 = 90\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $SCRI = 90\%$. This indicates a **highly resilient** supply chain (industry benchmark: $SCRI \geq 85\%$ is considered resilient).

### Step 5: Interpretation
- The 10-day disruption caused only a **10% loss in cumulative performance** over 60 days.
- This is achievable because the disruption was short relative to the observation window AND the degraded performance ($\rho = 0.4$) was still functional.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The 4R Resilience Framework — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Disruption Lifecycle] --> B[Phase 1: ANTICIPATION]
    B --> B1[Risk Identification]
    B1 --> B2[Vulnerability Mapping]
    B2 --> B3[Scenario Planning]
    B3 --> C[Phase 2: RESISTANCE]
    C --> C1[Robustness]
    C1 --> C2[Redundancy]
    C2 --> D[Phase 3: RECOVERY]
    D --> D1[Resourcefulness]
    D1 --> D2[Adaptive Response]
    D2 --> D3[Time to Recover TTR]
    D3 --> E[Phase 4: EVOLUTION]
    E --> E1[Lessons Learned]
    E1 --> E2[Network Redesign]
    E2 --> E3[Improved SCRI]
    E3 --> F[Resilient Supply Chain State]
    F -.Feedback Loop.-> B

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#6366f1,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.2 Resilience vs. Efficiency Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cost-Efficient Lean Supply Chain] -->|Add Buffers| B[Moderately Resilient]
    B -->|Add Redundancy| C[Highly Resilient]
    B -->|Optimize Buffers| A
    C -->|Reduce Redundancy| B
    B -->|Remove Buffers| A

    A1[Cost: LOW] -.-> A
    A2[Resilience: LOW] -.-> A
    B1[Cost: MEDIUM] -.-> B
    B2[Resilience: MEDIUM] -.-> B
    C1[Cost: HIGH] -.-> C
    C2[Resilience: HIGH] -.-> C

    style A fill:#fee2e2,color:#000000
    style B fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
    style C fill:#dcfce7,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.4 Risk-Resilience Heat Matrix (Conceptual Schematic)


> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Matrix:** Quadrant G (High Probability + High Impact) demands the **highest resilience investment**. Quadrant J (Low Probability + Low Impact) can be accepted with minimal buffer. The KTU examiner expects you to **map specific risks to these quadrants** in case-study questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *"Define Supply Chain Resilience. List the four pillars of the resilience framework."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Supply Chain Resilience is the **adaptive capability** of a supply chain to prepare for, absorb, recover from, and adapt to disruptions, maintaining continuity of operations and customer service.

The **four pillars (4R Framework)** proposed by Christopher and Peck are:
1. **Robustness** — Inherent strength to resist disruption.
2. **Redundancy** — Strategic duplication of resources.
3. **Resourcefulness** — Ability to mobilize resources adaptively.
4. **Recovery** — Speed of return to normal operations.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 marks for each R = 3 marks total)*

---

### Question 2: Metric Distinction
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *"Distinguish between Time to Recover (TTR) and Recovery Time Objective (RTO) with a suitable example."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | TTR (Time to Recover) | RTO (Recovery Time Objective) |
|:------:|:---------------------:|:-----------------------------:|
| **Nature** | Actual measured recovery time | Target/planned recovery time |
| **Calculation** | $t_{recovery} - t_{disruption}$ (historical) | Pre-defined service level agreement |
| **Use** | Post-incident analysis | Pre-incident planning |

**Example:** A Kerala-based spice exporter sets an **RTO of 48 hours** for port operations after a cyclone. After a real disruption, the actual **TTR was 36 hours**, exceeding the target.

*(1.5 marks distinction + 1.5 marks example = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Comprehensive Analytical Question

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
> **(a)** Explain the **4R Framework of Supply Chain Resilience** with a real-world example from the post-COVID-19 era. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** A supply chain operates at a normal performance of $P_0 = 800$ units/day. A pandemic-induced disruption lasted $\tau = 15$ days, with performance dropping to $P_d = 320$ units/day. The observation window is $T = 90$ days. Calculate the **SCRI** and interpret the result. *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to Part A(a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The 4R Framework, developed by **Christopher and Peck (2004)**, provides a comprehensive lens to analyze supply chain resilience across the disruption lifecycle.

**The Four R's (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Robustness** — The supply chain is designed to *withstand* shocks. Post-COVID-19, pharmaceutical firms like **Serum Institute of India** invested in robust cold-chain infrastructure to handle vaccine distribution disruptions.

2. **Redundancy** — Firms maintain *spare capacity* and *multiple suppliers*. **Apple Inc.** sources A-series chips from both TSMC (Taiwan) and Samsung (South Korea) to avoid single-point failures.

3. **Resourcefulness** — Firms leverage *human capital and real-time data* to respond. **Walmart** uses AI-driven demand sensing to reroute inventory during hurricanes.

4. **Recovery** — Firms restore operations *quickly*. **Toyota's** "Rescue Production System" restored operations within 6 months after the 2011 Japan tsunami.

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
The 4R framework is a *holistic* model — neglecting any one R weakens the entire resilience posture. Post-COVID-19, leading firms have shifted from lean-only strategies to **leagile** models that combine lean efficiency with agile resilience.

---

#### Solution to Part A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $P_0 = 800$ units/day
- $P_d = 320$ units/day
- $\tau = 15$ days
- $T = 90$ days

**Step 1: Calculate the Degradation Ratio (1 mark):**
$$
\rho = \frac{P_d}{P_0} = \frac{320}{800} = 0.4
$$

**Step 2: Apply the SCRI Formula (2 marks):**
$$
SCRI = \left[ 1 - \frac{\tau}{T} (1 - \rho) \right] \times 100
$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values (1 mark):**
$$
SCRI = \left[ 1 - \frac{15}{90} (1 - 0.4) \right] \times 100
$$

**Step 4: Simplify (1 mark):**
$$
SCRI = \left[ 1 - 0.1667 \times 0.6 \right] \times 100
$$

$$
SCRI = \left[ 1 - 0.1 \right] \times 100 = 90\%
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation (2 marks):**
- $SCRI = 90\% \geq 85\%$ $\Rightarrow$ **Highly Resilient Supply Chain**.
- Despite a 15-day disruption with a 60% performance drop, the cumulative loss over 90 days was only 10%, indicating **effective recovery mechanisms**.
- **Recommendation:** Maintain current resilience investment; consider further improvement in $\rho$ (degradation ratio) through better buffer strategies.

---

### Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Question

> **[KTU University Exam — Sessional Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Discuss the **strategies for building supply chain resilience** with reference to multi-sourcing, safety stock, and postponement. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** A firm faces three independent risk events with the following data: Event 1: $P = 0.3$, Loss $= 50,000$ ₹; Event 2: $P = 0.5$, Loss $= 20,000$ ₹; Event 3: $P = 0.1$, Loss $= 1,00,000$ ₹. Compute the **Expected Loss (EL)** and **Risk Exposure (RE)**. Comment on which event should be prioritized for resilience investment. *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to Part B(a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Building supply chain resilience requires a **portfolio of strategies** that address different phases of the disruption lifecycle. Three key strategies are:

**1. Multi-Sourcing Strategy (2 marks):**
- Engaging **2 or more suppliers** for the same critical component.
- **Types:** Dual sourcing (70-30), Triple sourcing (50-30-20).
- **Example:** Honda sources brake systems from both Bosch and Continental.
- **Trade-off:** Higher procurement cost vs. lower disruption risk.

**2. Safety Stock Strategy (2 marks):**
- Maintaining **buffer inventory** above the cycle stock level.
- **KTU Formula:** $BS = Z_{\alpha} \times \sigma_{D} \times \sqrt{L} + S_{disruption}$
- **Example:** Kerala's HLL (Hindustan Lifecare) maintains 60-day safety stock for medical devices.
- **Trade-off:** Carrying cost vs. service level assurance.

**3. Postponement Strategy (2 marks):**
- **Delaying final product configuration** until customer order is received.
- **Types:** Labeling postponement, packaging postponement, assembly postponement.
- **Example:** Dell's configure-to-order model postpones assembly until order receipt.
- **Benefit:** Reduces obsolete inventory risk + enhances customization.

**Conclusion (1 mark — bonus):**
A *leagile* supply chain strategically combines these approaches based on product criticality and customer demand variability.

---

#### Solution to Part B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute Expected Loss for Each Event (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

$$
EL_1 = P_1 \times L_1 = 0.3 \times 50,000 = 15,000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
EL_2 = P_2 \times L_2 = 0.5 \times 20,000 = 10,000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
EL_3 = P_3 \times L_3 = 0.1 \times 1,00,000 = 10,000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Total Expected Loss (1 mark):**
$$
EL_{total} = 15,000 + 10,000 + 10,000 = 35,000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Risk Exposure (RE) for Each Event (1 mark):**
Since $RE = P \times I$ is identical to $EL$, the $RE$ values are $15,000$ ₹, $10,000$ ₹, and $10,000$ ₹ respectively.

**Step 4: Interpretation and Prioritization (2 marks):**

| Event | Probability | Loss (₹) | Expected Loss (₹) | Priority |
|:-----:|:-----------:|:--------:|:-----------------:|:--------:|
| 1 | 0.3 | 50,000 | **15,000** | **Highest** |
| 2 | 0.5 | 20,000 | 10,000 | Medium |
| 3 | 0.1 | 1,00,000 | 10,000 | Medium |

**Recommendation:** **Event 1** should be prioritized because it has the **highest expected loss (₹15,000)**, even though Event 3 has the highest *individual* impact. Event 3, despite its catastrophic potential, has low probability and may be addressed through **insurance** rather than supply chain restructuring.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations (Module 1 — Resilience):**
>
> 1. **Confusing SCRI with Service Level:** SCRI is a *time-weighted cumulative performance ratio* (0–100%), NOT the same as instantaneous service level. Many students write "SCRI = 95% service level" — this is **incorrect** and loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Omitting Units in Formulas:** Always write units explicitly (e.g., $TTR = 36$ hours, not just $36$). The KTU examiner deducts **0.5 marks per missing unit**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Degradation Ratio ($\rho$):** Students often substitute $P_d$ directly into the SCRI formula without first computing $\rho = P_d / P_0$. This is a **common 1-mark error**.
>
> 4. **Mixing RTO and RTO:** RPO (Recovery Point Objective) and RTO (Recovery Time Objective) are **different metrics**. RPO = data loss tolerance; RTO = downtime tolerance. Swapping them loses **full marks** on that sub-question.
>
> 5. **One-Sided Risk Analysis:** When computing Expected Loss, always state **the assumption of independence** of risk events. KTU examiners specifically look for this phrase.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Resilient Supply Chains**

- **Definition:** Resilience = *Anticipate + Absorb + Recover + Adapt* (the 4 A's — alternate to 4R).
- **4R Framework:** **Robustness, Redundancy, Resourcefulness, Recovery** (Christopher & Peck, 2004).
- **Key Metrics to Memorize:**
  - $SCRI = \left[ 1 - \dfrac{\tau}{T}(1-\rho) \right] \times 100$
  - $TTR = t_r - t_d$
  - $RE = P \times I$
  - $EL = \sum P_i L_i$
- **Resilience $\neq$ Efficiency** — They are inversely related. Lean = efficient but fragile.
- **Strategies (7 to memorize):** Multi-sourcing, Safety stock, Nearshoring, Digital twins, Postponement, Geographic diversification, Supplier collaboration.
- **Industry Benchmarks:** $SCRI \geq 85\%$ = Resilient; $TTR \leq 48$ hours = Excellent recovery.
- **Real-world case to cite:** Post-COVID-19 semiconductor shortage, 2011 Japan tsunami (Toyota), Kerala floods 2018 (HLL response).
- **The Anti-Fragile Concept:** Resilience $\rightarrow$ Anti-Fragility (learns and grows stronger from disruptions).
- **Three disruption categories:** Natural (earthquakes, floods), Man-made (strikes, cyberattacks), Economic (recessions, sanctions).
- **Exam mantra:** Always show the *formula → substitution → simplification → interpretation* chain in numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dual Channel and International Supply Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET524 - LOGICS & SUPPLY CHAIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dual Channel and International Supply Chains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Dual Channel Supply Chains

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A **Dual Channel Supply Chain** is a distribution architecture in which a manufacturer (or supplier) reaches the end customer through **two distinct, parallel marketing channels** — typically combining a **Direct Channel** (Manufacturer → Consumer, often via e-commerce/online platforms) with an **Indirect Channel** (Manufacturer → Retailer → Consumer, via traditional brick-and-mortar outlets). This structure is also referred to as a **Hybrid Channel** or **Omni-Channel Distribution Network** in modern KTU syllabus parlance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Dual channel systems are examined under *Channel Conflict, Channel Coordination, and Pricing Strategy* — focus on why manufacturers adopt them, the resulting cannibalization effects, and the Stackelberg leader-follower game used to model optimal wholesale and retail pricing.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**

Imagine **Coca-Cola** in India. You can buy a Coke bottle from:
- A **kirana store** near your house (Indirect Channel — manufacturer → distributor → retailer → you)
- **Amazon.in** or the official Coca-Cola website (Direct Channel — manufacturer → courier → you)

Coca-Cola runs **both** channels simultaneously. This is a **dual channel supply chain**. The challenge? The kirana shopkeeper is now upset because Amazon is selling at ₹60 while he has to buy at ₹55 from a distributor and sell at ₹65 to compete. This is **channel conflict** — the central problem in dual channel design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A dual channel is NOT just "having a website." It is a *strategic* choice that fundamentally alters the power balance, pricing decisions, and profit-sharing between the manufacturer, the retailer, and the consumer.

---

### 1.2 International Supply Chains

**Formal KTU Definition:**
An **International Supply Chain (ISCN)** is a supply chain network whose activities — *sourcing, production, warehousing, transportation, and distribution* — span **two or more sovereign nations**, thereby exposing the network to additional layers of risk, cost, and complexity arising from **geography, customs regulation, currency, culture, and geopolitics**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module focuses on *drivers of globalization, international logistics modes, Incoterms, trade barriers, and global supply chain design decisions* (offshoring, nearshoring, reshoring).

**Conceptual Analogy:**

Think of how **Apple's iPhone** is made:
- Designed in **California, USA**
- Chips fabricated in **Taiwan (TSMC)**
- Camera sensors from **Sony, Japan**
- Display glass from **Corning, USA / China**
- Lithium for batteries mined in **Australia / Chile**
- Assembled in **Foxconn plants, Shenzhen, China**
- Sold in **150+ countries**

The supply chain is genuinely *global*. A single disruption in the Taiwan Strait or a port strike in Long Beach ripples through this network — that is the defining challenge of an international supply chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metric:** The KTU syllabus frequently tests the **Total Landed Cost (TLC)** — the true cost of a product including manufacturing, freight, duties, insurance, and inventory carrying cost incurred across borders.

---

### 1.3 Why Are These Two Topics Grouped Together?

Both are **extensions of the traditional single-firm, single-country supply chain** into more complex configurations:
- Dual Channel → extends **horizontally** (more routes to the same customer)
- International Supply Chain → extends **geographically** (same product, multiple countries)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** **"Dual = More Doors; International = More Borders."** Both increase complexity, cost components, and coordination overhead.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Dual Channel Supply Chains — Theoretical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Why do manufacturers open a direct channel?**

| Driver | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| Higher profit margin | Manufacturer captures the retailer's margin (typically **15–35%** of retail price) |
| Customer data ownership | Direct transactions yield first-party behavioral data |
| Brand control | Consistent pricing, presentation, and service |
| E-commerce boom | Digital platforms lowered the entry barrier to **near-zero** |
| Defensive response | Prevent pure-play online competitors (e.g., boAt vs. Sony) from capturing share |

**Step 2 — Types of Channel Conflict in Dual Systems**

1. **Vertical Conflict** — Between manufacturer and retailer (e.g., Apple Store vs. Best Buy).
2. **Horizontal Conflict** — Between two retailers of the same brand (e.g., Croma vs. Reliance Digital both selling Samsung).
3. **Cannibalization** — Direct sales *eat into* retail sales; the firm's own channels compete.
4. **Channel Power Imbalance** — The party with more information/pricing flexibility dominates.

**Step 3 — Pricing Strategy: The Stackelberg Game Model**

The KTU 2024 syllabus models dual channel pricing using **non-cooperative game theory**:

- The **Manufacturer (leader)** sets the wholesale price $w$ first.
- The **Retailer (follower)** then sets the retail price $p_r$.
- The **Manufacturer** also sets the direct price $p_d$ for its own online channel.

**Step 4 — Channel Coordination Mechanisms**

To resolve conflict, firms deploy:
- **Wholesale price reduction** (gives retailer margin relief)
- **Buy-back contracts** (manufacturer buys back unsold inventory)
- **Revenue sharing** (split the e-commerce revenue with the retailer)
- **Channel differentiation** (different SKUs, packaging, or service levels for each channel — *brand-line vs. store-line in FMCG*)

---

### 2.2 International Supply Chains — Theoretical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Drivers of Internationalization (KTU "Five Drivers" Framework)**

1. **Cost** — Labor arbitrage (e.g., Bangladesh garment industry).
2. **Capability** — Access to specialized skills unavailable domestically.
3. **Capacity** — Overcoming domestic production limits.
4. **Customer Proximity** — Being physically close to growth markets.
5. **Competitive Pressure** — Following rivals into new geographies.

**Step 2 — Modes of International Transportation**

| Mode | Cost per ton-km | Speed | Reliability | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Air Freight** | Highest (₹80–150) | Fastest (<24 hrs) | Very High | High-value, low-weight (electronics, pharma) |
| **Sea Freight (FCL/LCL)** | Lowest (₹2–6) | Slowest (15–40 days) | Moderate | Bulky, low-value (textiles, machinery) |
| **Road (Cross-border)** | Medium (₹10–20) | Fast (1–5 days) | High | Regional (e.g., India–Bangladesh) |
| **Rail (Trans-Eurasian)** | Low-Medium (₹5–12) | Medium (12–20 days) | High | China–Europe (Chengdu–Duisburg) |
| **Multimodal** | Composite | Composite | Composite | Most ISCNs in reality |

**Step 3 — Incoterms (International Commercial Terms) — KTU Critical**

Incoterms define the **transfer point of cost and risk** between buyer and seller. The 11 terms standardized by the **International Chamber of Commerce (ICC)** are grouped by E/F/C/D:

- **E-Term (Departure):** EXW (Ex Works)
- **F-Term (Main Carriage Unpaid by Seller):** FCA, FAS, FOB
- **C-Term (Main Carriage Paid by Seller):** CFR, CIF, CPT, CIP
- **D-Term (Arrival):** DAP, DPU, DDP

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The student is *expected* to know the difference between **FOB (Free On Board)** — risk transfers at the ship's rail at the port of origin — and **CIF (Cost, Insurance, Freight)** — seller pays freight + insurance up to destination port.

**Step 4 — Total Landed Cost Formula (TLC)**

TLC is the *single most important* number in international sourcing decisions. The KTU-accepted formulation is:

$$
\text{TLC} = C_{\text{prod}} + C_{\text{freight}} + C_{\text{duty}} + C_{\text{ins}} + C_{\text{inv}} + C_{\text{other}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{prod}}$ = Production cost (FOB factory price)
- $C_{\text{freight}}$ = International freight (ocean/air/land)
- $C_{\text{duty}}$ = Customs duty + GST/VAT
- $C_{\text{ins}}$ = Cargo insurance premium
- $C_{\text{inv}}$ = In-transit inventory carrying cost
- $C_{\text{other}}$ = Documentation, port handling, demurrage

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula / Term | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Direct demand function | $d = a - b p_d$ | $a,b > 0$ |
| 2 | Retail demand function | $r = \alpha - \beta p_r$ | $\alpha,\beta > 0$ |
| 3 | Manufacturer profit (dual) | $\pi_m = (w - c) r + (p_d - c) d$ | Includes both channels |
| 4 | Retailer profit | $\pi_r = (p_r - w) r$ | Standard retailer margin |
| 5 | Stackelberg equilibrium $p_d^*$ | $p_d^{*} = \dfrac{a + c}{2}$ | Under symmetric demand |
| 6 | Stackelberg equilibrium $p_r^{*}$ | $p_r^{*} = \dfrac{\alpha + w}{2}$ | Follower reaction |
| 7 | Optimal wholesale $w^*$ | $w^{*} = \dfrac{\alpha + c}{2}$ | Manufacturer's choice |
| 8 | Total Landed Cost (TLC) | $\text{TLC} = C_p + C_f + C_d + C_i + C_{inv}$ | INR / USD per unit |
| 9 | In-transit inventory cost | $C_{inv} = I \cdot T \cdot h$ | $I$ = inventory value, $T$ = transit days, $h$ = holding rate |
| 10 | Freight cost (ton-km) | $C_f = \rho \cdot D$ | $\rho$ = rate (₹/ton-km), $D$ = ton-km |
| 11 | Effective duty rate | $d_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\text{Assessable Value} \times \text{Duty\%}}{1 - \text{Duty\%}}$ | For inclusive pricing |
| 12 | Currency hedge ratio | $H = \dfrac{\text{Forged Exposure}}{\text{Total Exposure}}$ | $0 \le H \le 1$ |
| 13 | Safety stock (international) | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Longer $L$ → larger $SS$ |
| 14 | Lead-time variance | $\text{Var}(L) = \text{Var}(L_{\text{prod}}) + \text{Var}(L_{\text{trans}})$ | Additive under independence |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` not `|` in markdown tables.** The KTU examiner strictly deducts marks if a formula column breaks due to a stray pipe character in an absolute value or determinant.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Industry Utility

- **Dual Channel:** Practically used by **every major CPG, electronics, and apparel brand** in India — HUL, ITC, Samsung, Tata, Reliance Retail, Adidas, Apple.
- **International Supply Chains:** Foundation of **global trade worth ~$32 trillion (2024)**. The semiconductor, pharmaceutical, automotive, and apparel industries cannot operate without it.
- **Engineering crossover:** ISCN design intersects with **industrial engineering** (network optimization), **civil engineering** (port/terminal design), and **electrical engineering** (ICT infrastructure for tracking).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stackelberg Equilibrium in a Dual-Channel System

**Setup (Standard KTU Model):**

A manufacturer sells a product through:
- **Channel 1 (Direct):** Sets price $p_d$, demand $d = a - b p_d$
- **Channel 2 (Retail):** Sets price $p_r$ via retailer who pays wholesale $w$, demand $r = \alpha - \beta p_r$

Assume **no cross-price effect** for simplicity (basic KTU form).

**Profit Functions:**

$$
\pi_m = (w - c) r + (p_d - c) d
$$

$$
\pi_r = (p_r - w) r
$$

Where $c$ = unit production cost.

---

**Step 1 — Retailer's Best Response (Follower)**

The retailer maximizes $\pi_r$ w.r.t. $p_r$:

$$
\frac{\partial \pi_r}{\partial p_r} = (p_r - w)(- \beta) + (\alpha - \beta p_r)(1) = 0
$$

Expand:

$$
-\beta p_r + \beta w + \alpha - \beta p_r = 0
$$

Collect $p_r$ terms:

$$
\alpha + \beta w - 2 \beta p_r = 0
$$

Solve for $p_r$:

$$
p_r^{*}(w) = \frac{\alpha + \beta w}{2 \beta} = \frac{\alpha}{2\beta} + \frac{w}{2}
$$

**Interpretation:** The retailer's optimal price is *half-way* between the choke price $\alpha/\beta$ and the wholesale price $w$.

---

**Step 2 — Manufacturer's Direct-Channel Price**

The manufacturer sets $p_d$ to maximize direct profit, holding $d = a - b p_d$:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial p_d} \left[ (p_d - c)(a - b p_d) \right] = 0
$$

Expand:

$$
(a - b p_d) + (p_d - c)(-b) = 0
$$

$$
a - b p_d - b p_d + b c = 0
$$

$$
a + b c = 2 b p_d
$$

$$
p_d^{*} = \frac{a + b c}{2 b} = \frac{a}{2b} + \frac{c}{2}
$$

**Interpretation:** The optimal direct price is *halfway* between the choke price $a/b$ and the marginal cost $c$.

---

**Step 3 — Manufacturer's Wholesale Price (Leader Problem)**

Substitute $p_r^{*}(w)$ into retail demand:

$$
r(w) = \alpha - \beta \left( \frac{\alpha + \beta w}{2\beta} \right) = \alpha - \frac{\alpha + \beta w}{2} = \frac{\alpha - \beta w}{2}
$$

Manufacturer's profit becomes:

$$
\pi_m(w) = (w - c) \cdot \frac{\alpha - \beta w}{2} + (p_d^{*} - c)(a - b p_d^{*})
$$

The second term is **constant w.r.t. $w$** (manufacturer already chose $p_d$ optimally), so we maximize only the wholesale-channel portion:

$$
\Pi(w) = (w - c)(\alpha - \beta w)
$$

Take the derivative:

$$
\frac{d\Pi}{dw} = (\alpha - \beta w) + (w - c)(-\beta) = \alpha - \beta w - \beta w + \beta c
$$

$$
\frac{d\Pi}{dw} = \alpha + \beta c - 2 \beta w = 0
$$

$$
w^{*} = \frac{\alpha + \beta c}{2 \beta} = \frac{\alpha}{2\beta} + \frac{c}{2}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Equilibrium Summary**

$$
\boxed{\, w^{*} = \frac{\alpha}{2\beta} + \frac{c}{2}, \quad p_r^{*} = \frac{3\alpha}{4\beta} + \frac{c}{4}, \quad p_d^{*} = \frac{a}{2b} + \frac{c}{2} \,}
$$

**Sanity Check (Numerical):** Let $\alpha = 100$, $\beta = 1$, $a = 100$, $b = 1$, $c = 10$:

$$
w^{*} = 50 + 5 = 55
$$

$$
p_r^{*} = 75 + 2.5 = 77.5
$$

$$
p_d^{*} = 50 + 5 = 55
$$

Retailer margin: $77.5 - 55 = 22.5$ ✓ (positive — retailer participates)

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Landed Cost (TLC) for an Imported Component

**Problem:** An Indian firm imports a sub-assembly from Vietnam.
- FOB factory price: **$50/unit**
- Ocean freight: **$4/unit**
- Marine insurance: **1.5% of CIF value**
- Basic customs duty: **10%**
- Social welfare surcharge: **10% of duty**
- IGST: **18%** (on assessable + duty + surcharge)
- Transit time: **25 days**
- Annual holding cost rate: **12%**
- Exchange rate: **₹83 / $1**

Find the **TLC in ₹ per unit**.

---

**Step 1 — Compute CIF Value**

$$
\text{CIF} = F + I + F = 50 + (0.015 \times 50) + 50 = 100.75 \text{ USD}
$$

Wait — the correct definition is $\text{CIF} = \text{FOB} + \text{Freight} + \text{Insurance}$:

$$
\text{CIF} = 50 + 4 + 0.015 \times 54 = 50 + 4 + 0.81 = 54.81 \text{ USD}
$$

**Insurance is typically computed on (FOB + Freight) = 110% of FOB under ICC-C clauses.**

$$
\text{CIF} = 54.81 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 2 — Basic Customs Duty (BCD)**

$$
\text{BCD} = 0.10 \times 54.81 = 5.481 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 3 — Social Welfare Surcharge (SWS) @ 10% of BCD**

$$
\text{SWS} = 0.10 \times 5.481 = 0.5481 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 4 — Assessable Value for IGST**

$$
A_{IGST} = \text{CIF} + \text{BCD} + \text{SWS} = 54.81 + 5.481 + 0.5481 = 60.8391 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 5 — IGST @ 18%**

$$
\text{IGST} = 0.18 \times 60.8391 = 10.9510 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Customs + Tax Cost in USD**

$$
C_{\text{duty, total}} = 5.481 + 0.5481 + 10.9510 = 16.980 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 7 — In-Transit Inventory Carrying Cost**

In-transit capital tied up $\approx$ CIF + Duty payments (approximated as $C_{\text{inv}} = C_{\text{product}} \cdot h \cdot (T/365)$):

$$
C_{inv} = 54.81 \times 0.12 \times \frac{25}{365} = 54.81 \times 0.00822 = 0.4506 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to ₹ and Sum**

Total USD cost = $54.81 + 16.980 + 0.4506 = 72.24$ USD

$$
\text{TLC} = 72.24 \times 83 = \mathbf{₹\,5{,}995.92 \text{ per unit}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The component *appears* to cost ₹4,150 FOB (= $50 \times 83$) but the **true landed cost is ₹5,996** — a **44.5% markup** purely from logistics and statutory levies. This is the central insight of the TLC framework.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Dimension | Single Channel | Dual Channel | International |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of intermediaries** | One | Multiple in parallel | Many across borders |
| **Pricing decisions** | Single price | Two prices + wholesale | Multiple FX-adjusted prices |
| **Lead time** | 1–7 days | 1–10 days | 15–45 days |
| **Information flow** | Simple | Complex (channel data) | Highly complex |
| **Risk types** | Demand, supply | + Channel conflict | + FX, geopolitical, customs |
| **Inventory locations** | 1–2 | 2–5 | 5–20+ globally |
| **Cost components** | 4–5 | 6–8 | 10–15+ |
| **Coordination difficulty** | Low | Medium | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Dual Channel Supply Chain — Functional Architecture

<!-- DIAGRAM_START -->
```mermaid
graph TD
    M1[Manufacturer]
    M1 -->|Wholesale price w| R1[Retailer Channel]
    M1 -->|Direct price pd| DC1[Direct E Commerce Site]
    R1 -->|Retail price pr| END1[End Customer]
    DC1 -->|Direct delivery| END1
    M1 -.->|Channel conflict signal| CC1[Conflict Resolution Mechanism]
    CC1 -->|Buyback contract| R1
    CC1 -->|Revenue share| DC1
    R1 -.->|Demand data feedback| M1
    DC1 -.->|Demand data feedback| M1
```
<!-- DIAGRAM_END -->

**Node Legend:**
- `M1` = Manufacturer (Stackelberg leader)
- `R1` = Traditional retail channel
- `DC1` = Direct digital channel
- `CC1` = Coordination mechanism (contracts, rebates)
- Solid arrows = physical / monetary flows; dashed arrows = information / control signals

---

### 4.2 International Supply Chain — Block Functional Flow

<!-- DIAGRAM_START -->
```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Source Country Supplier]
    A2[Origin Port]
    A3[Customs Origin]
    A4[International Freight Sea Air Rail]
    A5[Destination Port]
    A6[Customs Destination]
    A7[DC in Destination Country]
    A8[Local Distribution]
    A9[End Customer]
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9
    A6 -.->|Incoterm FOB CIF DDP| A4
    A7 -.->|FX risk hedging| A1
    A7 -.->|Safety stock adjustment| A8
```
<!-- DIAGRAM_END -->

---

### 4.3 Decision Matrix — Channel Selection in Dual Systems

<!-- DIAGRAM_START -->
```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Product type}
    Q1 -->|High involvement durable| R1[Indirect retailer dominant]
    Q1 -->|Low involvement FMCG| R2[Dual channel both]
    Q1 -->|Digital native| R3[Direct dominant]
    R1 --> P1{Service complexity}
    R1 --> P2{Brand experience}
    P1 -->|High| X1[Authorized dealer network]
    P1 -->|Low| X2[Mass retail]
    P2 -->|Premium| Y1[Brand flagship store]
    P2 -->|Standard| Y2[Multi brand outlet]
```
<!-- DIAGRAM_END -->

---

### 4.4 International Sourcing Decision Tree

<!-- DIAGRAM_START -->
```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Total Landed Cost TLC]
    S2{Is TLC < Domestic cost plus 15 percent}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[Source internationally]
    S2 -->|No| S4[Source domestically]
    S3 --> S5{Geopolitical risk index acceptable}
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Proceed with supplier qualification]
    S5 -->|No| S7[Consider nearshoring alternative]
    S6 --> S8[Sign Incoterm based contract e.g. FOB CIF]
    S7 --> S9[Re-evaluate supplier base]
```
<!-- DIAGRAM_END -->

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Diagram Convention:** All Mermaid node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `M1`, `Q1`, `S6`) and labels are plain text — no markdown formatting inside quoted labels — to ensure clean rendering on the KTU learning portal.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a dual channel supply chain. State any two causes of channel conflict in such a system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **dual channel supply chain** is a distribution network in which a manufacturer serves the end market through **two distinct channels simultaneously** — typically a *direct channel* (manufacturer → consumer, often online) and an *indirect channel* (manufacturer → retailer → consumer).

Two causes of channel conflict:
1. **Price discrepancy** — Direct channel may undercut retailer price, eroding retailer margins. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Cannibalization** — Sales in the direct channel reduce retailer sales of the same product, creating zero-sum resentment. *(1.5 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is an Incoterm? Distinguish between FOB and CIF.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Incoterm (International Commercial Term)** is a standardized pre-defined trade term published by the **International Chamber of Commerce (ICC)** that defines the responsibilities of buyers and sellers regarding the **delivery of goods, transfer of risk, and allocation of costs** in international transactions. *(1.5 marks)*

| Aspect | FOB (Free On Board) | CIF (Cost, Insurance, Freight) |
|---|---|---|
| Risk transfer | At ship's rail at origin port | At destination port |
| Freight paid by | Buyer | Seller |
| Insurance paid by | Buyer | Seller (minimum coverage) |

*(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, usually split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question** from the choice provided.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Dual Channel Focus*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A manufacturer operates a dual channel. The retail demand is $r = 100 - p_r$ and direct demand is $d = 80 - 2 p_d$. The unit cost is $c = 10$. Using the **Stackelberg game** (manufacturer as leader), determine the optimal wholesale price $w^{*}$, the retail price $p_r^{*}$, and the direct price $p_d^{*}$. Calculate the profits of the manufacturer and the retailer at equilibrium. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **four channel coordination mechanisms** that a manufacturer can deploy to resolve conflict with retailers in a dual channel system. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-step:**

**Step 1 — Retailer's reaction function:**

Retailer maximizes $\pi_r = (p_r - w)(100 - p_r)$.

$$
\frac{\partial \pi_r}{\partial p_r} = (100 - p_r) - (p_r - w) = 0
$$

$$
100 - 2 p_r + w = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; p_r^{*} = \frac{100 + w}{2}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Manufacturer's direct channel:**

$$
\pi_{m,d} = (p_d - 10)(80 - 2 p_d)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial p_d} = 80 - 2 p_d - 2 p_d + 20 = 0
$$

$$
100 = 4 p_d \;\Rightarrow\; p_d^{*} = 25
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Wholesale price optimization:**

Retail demand as a function of $w$:

$$
r(w) = 100 - \frac{100 + w}{2} = \frac{100 - w}{2}
$$

Manufacturer's wholesale-channel profit:

$$
\pi_{m,w} = (w - 10) \cdot \frac{100 - w}{2}
$$

FOC:

$$
\frac{100 - w}{2} - \frac{w - 10}{2} = 0
$$

$$
100 - w - w + 10 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; w^{*} = 55
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Final values:**

$$
p_r^{*} = \frac{100 + 55}{2} = 77.5
$$

$$
p_d^{*} = 25
$$

**Step 5 — Profits:**

Retailer: $\pi_r = (77.5 - 55)(100 - 77.5) = 22.5 \times 22.5 = \mathbf{506.25}$

Manufacturer retail channel: $\pi_{m,r} = (55 - 10)(22.5) = 45 \times 22.5 = 1012.5$

Manufacturer direct channel: $\pi_{m,d} = (25 - 10)(80 - 50) = 15 \times 30 = 450$

Total manufacturer: $\pi_m = 1012.5 + 450 = \mathbf{1462.5}$

*(3 marks)*

**Final Answer Table:**

| Variable | Value |
|---|---|
| $w^{*}$ | 55 |
| $p_r^{*}$ | 77.5 |
| $p_d^{*}$ | 25 |
| $\pi_m$ | 1462.5 |
| $\pi_r$ | 506.25 |

---

**(b) Four Channel Coordination Mechanisms:**

1. **Wholesale Price Reduction** — Lower $w$ to give the retailer more margin, reducing incentive to defect. *(1.75 marks)*
2. **Buy-Back Contract** — Manufacturer agrees to repurchase unsold retailer inventory at a partial price, transferring demand risk back. *(1.75 marks)*
3. **Revenue Sharing Contract** — Manufacturer shares a fraction (e.g., 30%) of direct-channel revenue with the retailer in exchange for cooperation. *(1.75 marks)*
4. **Channel Differentiation (SKU Partitioning)** — Manufacturer offers *different versions* (e.g., brand-line vs. economy-line) in each channel to minimize head-to-head competition. *(1.75 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *International Supply Chain Focus*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** An Indian electronics firm sources a component from China.
- FOB price: **CNY 350/unit**
- Ocean freight: **CNY 40/unit**
- Insurance: **1.2% of (FOB + Freight)**
- Basic customs duty: **7.5%**
- IGST: **18%** (on CIF + duty)
- Transit time: **30 days**
- Annual holding cost rate: **15%**
- Exchange rate: **CNY 1 = ₹11.50**

Calculate the **Total Landed Cost (TLC) in ₹ per unit**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **five drivers of internationalization** that motivate firms to design international supply chains. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-step TLC Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Insurance**

Insurance base = FOB + Freight = $350 + 40 = 390$ CNY
Insurance = $0.012 \times 390 = 4.68$ CNY

**Step 2 — CIF Value**

$$
\text{CIF} = 350 + 40 + 4.68 = 394.68 \text{ CNY}
$$

**Step 3 — Basic Customs Duty (BCD)**

$$
\text{BCD} = 0.075 \times 394.68 = 29.601 \text{ CNY}
$$

**Step 4 — Assessable Value for IGST**

$$
A = 394.68 + 29.601 = 424.281 \text{ CNY}
$$

**Step 5 — IGST**

$$
\text{IGST} = 0.18 \times 424.281 = 76.371 \text{ CNY}
$$

**Step 6 — In-Transit Inventory Carrying Cost**

$$
C_{inv} = 394.68 \times 0.15 \times \frac{30}{365} = 394.68 \times 0.01233 = 4.866 \text{ CNY}
$$

**Step 7 — Total Cost in CNY**

$$
\text{Total} = 394.68 + 29.601 + 76.371 + 4.866 = 505.518 \text{ CNY}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to ₹**

$$
\text{TLC} = 505.518 \times 11.50 = \mathbf{₹\,5{,}813.46 \text{ per unit}}
$$

*(7 marks — 0.875 each step)*

**Insight:** The FOB price of $350$ CNY = ₹4,025. The **TLC is ₹5,813** — a **44.4% markup**, validating the strategic importance of TLC.

---

**(b) Five Drivers of Internationalization:**

1. **Cost Driver** — Labor, land, and resource arbitrage. Example: garment manufacturing in Bangladesh at $0.50/hr vs. India at $1.80/hr. *(1.4 marks)*
2. **Capability Driver** — Access to technology/skills not available domestically. Example: semiconductor fabrication in Taiwan (TSMC). *(1.4 marks)*
3. **Capacity Driver** — Domestic capacity saturation forces expansion. Example: Tata Motors' plant in Thailand for ASEAN markets. *(1.4 marks)*
4. **Customer Proximity Driver** — Physical closeness to growth markets reduces lead time and tariff burden. Example: Suzuki's manufacturing in India for the Indian market. *(1.4 marks)*
5. **Competitive Driver** — Following global rivals into new markets to defend share. Example: Hyundai entering India after Maruti's dominance. *(1.4 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing the Stackelberg order** — Writing retailer's reaction *after* the manufacturer's wholesale optimization. The **correct sequence is**: (i) retailer reacts, (ii) manufacturer *anticipates* this reaction, (iii) manufacturer optimizes $w$ first. Reverse it and lose 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting IGST is on (CIF + BCD), not on CIF alone** — Half the duty calculation marks vanish.
> 3. **Insurance base** — Many compute insurance on FOB only. ICC standard is **(FOB + Freight) × 1.1** as the insurable value.
> 4. **No units in final answer** — Examiner deducts **0.5 mark** if TLC is written as a number without "₹/unit" or "USD/unit".
> 5. **Treating Incoterm and Incoterms version interchangeably** — Specify **"Incoterms 2020"** in the answer; older versions (2010) have different D-Terms.
> 6. **In dual channel, omitting the retailer's reaction function** — The Stackelberg derivation is incomplete without $p_r^{*}(w)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Dual channel = two parallel routes** to the customer (Direct + Indirect).
- **Channel conflict** is the *primary disadvantage*; **coordination contracts** are the cure.
- **Stackelberg game** — Manufacturer is **leader**, retailer is **follower**; manufacturer *commits* to $w$ first, retailer reacts with $p_r$.
- Standard optimal $w^{*} = \dfrac{\alpha}{2\beta} + \dfrac{c}{2}$, $p_r^{*} = \dfrac{3\alpha}{4\beta} + \dfrac{c}{4}$, $p_d^{*} = \dfrac{a}{2b} + \dfrac{c}{2}$.
- **International supply chain** adds customs, FX, geopolitical, and cultural layers on top of standard logistics.
- **Incoterms 2020** — 11 terms in 4 groups: **E, F, C, D**.
- **TLC = Product + Freight + Duty + Insurance + Inventory carrying + Other** — always compute; never trust the FOB price.
- **5 Drivers of Internationalization** — Cost, Capability, Capacity, Customer, Competitive.
- **Lead time variance adds** — $\text{Var}(L) = \text{Var}(L_{\text{prod}}) + \text{Var}(L_{\text{trans}})$.
- **Safety stock grows with $\sqrt{L}$** — international channels need **larger safety stock** than domestic ones for the same service level.
- **In-transit inventory cost** = $I \cdot h \cdot T$ — often forgotten in cost analyses.
- **FX risk** can be hedged via forwards/options; the **hedge ratio** is a key strategic lever.
- **Mode choice rule of thumb:** high-value + low-weight → **Air**; low-value + bulky → **Sea**; time-sensitive + regional → **Road/Rail**.
- **Dual channel direct price is typically LOWER than retail price** but HIGHER than wholesale — verify via $p_d^{*} \gtrless p_r^{*}$ using the demand parameters.
- **KTU 2024 marker bias:** Marks are awarded for *derivation steps*, not just the final number — always show the FOC and substitution explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forecasting and Aggregate Planning Models for Supply Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET524 - LOGISTICS & SUPPLY CHAIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forecasting and Aggregate Planning Models for Supply Chains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Forecasting in Supply Chains

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Forecasting is the process of predicting future demand for products and services by analyzing historical data, market intelligence, and causal factors. In supply chain engineering, forecasting serves as the foundational input for all downstream planning activities — procurement, production scheduling, inventory positioning, and distribution planning.*

**The Three Pillars of Supply Chain Forecasting:**
- **Demand Forecasting** — predicting customer demand for finished goods.
- **Supply Forecasting** — anticipating availability of raw materials and components.
- **Collaborative Forecasting (CPFR)** — joint forecasting between trading partners.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forecasting Horizon Classification (Mandatory for KTU):**
> - **Short-Range Forecast:** 0 to 3 months — used for operational decisions (ordering, scheduling).
> - **Medium-Range Forecast:** 3 months to 1 year — drives aggregate planning and production budgets.
> - **Long-Range Forecast:** 1 year to 5+ years — supports strategic capacity and capital investment decisions.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Weather & Grocery Shop
Imagine you own a grocery shop in Kerala that sells **umbrellas**. Last June you sold **300 units**, July **450 units**, and August **150 units**. By looking at this historical pattern, you can *forecast* September's demand. You are not 100% certain — it is a *probabilistic estimate*, not a guarantee. Forecasting is precisely this act of converting the **echo of the past** into a **guide for the future**. The better your forecast, the less you waste (excess inventory) and the less you disappoint customers (stockouts) — both of which directly affect **profit and customer service level**.

---

## 1.2 Aggregate Planning in Supply Chains

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Aggregate Planning is a medium-range (typically 3 to 18 months) operational planning process that establishes a coordinated production, workforce, and inventory strategy to match fluctuating demand with available capacity at minimum total cost. It deals with "product families" or "aggregated units" rather than individual SKUs.*

**Key Aggregate Planning Levers (KTU High-Yield):**
- **Workforce Level** — hiring, layoffs, overtime, undertime.
- **Production Rate** — machines, line speed, subcontracting.
- **Inventory Levels** — build-up and draw-down of finished goods.
- **Backorders / Lost Sales** — demand postponement strategy.
- **Pricing & Promotion** — demand influencing levers.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Caterer's Menu
A caterer preparing for a 6-month wedding season must decide *today* how many cooks to hire. If she over-hires, she bleeds salary cash. If she under-hires, she misses orders. She can also **pre-cook** certain dishes (inventory), **outsource** to freelance cooks (subcontract), or **delay** delivery of food to a Tuesday wedding if Saturday is overloaded (backorders). Aggregate planning is the caterer's master plan balancing **cost, capacity, and customer promise** across the season.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic is Critical in KTU 2024 Scheme
This module is the **mathematical backbone** of the entire Supply Chain course. Module 1 lays the quantitative foundation (forecasting + aggregate planning), Module 2 (typically) progresses to inventory, and Module 3 to network design. Almost every **14-mark question in ESE** will require you to:
- Apply a forecasting method on a demand series.
- Compute an aggregate plan and choose between strategies.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** "Forecasting error analysis" (MAD/MSE/MAPE) and "Chase vs. Level strategy comparison" are the **two most frequently repeated themes** in past KTU university exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Demand (Time Series Decomposition)

Every demand pattern can be decomposed into four classical components:

| Component | Symbol | Description | Example |
|-----------|--------|-------------|---------|
| **Level** | $L$ | The average/baseline demand around which the series fluctuates | Daily bread sales in a bakery |
| **Trend** | $T$ | Long-term upward or downward movement | Rising EV sales year-on-year |
| **Seasonality** | $S$ | Predictable, repeating pattern within a year | Ice-cream sales in Kerala summer |
| **Random/Noise** | $\varepsilon$ | Unpredictable variation | Sudden festival spike |

The multiplicative and additive decomposition models are:

$$D_t = L \times T \times S \times \varepsilon \quad \text{(Multiplicative)}$$

$$D_t = L + T + S + \varepsilon \quad \text{(Additive)}$$

---

## 2.2 Qualitative Forecasting Methods

> [!NOTE]
> Used when **historical numerical data is scarce or non-existent** (new products, disruptive technology, market entry).

- **Delphi Method** — Anonymous iterative expert panel; converges to consensus.
- **Market Research / Surveys** — Direct customer polling.
- **Sales Force Composite** — Aggregated estimates from front-line salespeople.
- **Executive Opinion / Jury of Experts** — Top-management judgment-based estimates.

---

## 2.3 Quantitative Forecasting Methods (Numerical Core)

### A. Time-Series Methods (only past demand used)

#### (i) Naive Forecast
The simplest possible method: tomorrow's demand equals today's demand.

$$F_{t+1} = D_t$$

#### (ii) Simple Moving Average (SMA) of order $n$

$$F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=t-n+1}^{t} D_i$$

- Smooths out random fluctuations.
- Equal weight to all $n$ periods.
- **Disadvantage:** Older data influences forecast equally to recent data.

#### (iii) Weighted Moving Average (WMA)

$$F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot D_{t-i+1}, \quad \text{where } \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$$

- Higher weight on recent data, lower weight on older data.
- Weights are chosen empirically (most recent typically $w_1 \geq 0.5$).

#### (iv) Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1 - \alpha) F_t$$

- **Smoothing constant** $\alpha \in [0, 1]$.
- High $\alpha$ (close to 1) → reactive, tracks recent demand.
- Low $\alpha$ (close to 0) → smooth, stable forecast.
- Equivalent to WMA with exponentially decaying weights.

#### (v) Trend-Adjusted Exponential Smoothing (Holt's Two-Parameter Method)

$$L_t = \alpha D_t + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$$

$$T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) T_{t-1}$$

$$F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot T_t$$

- Captures **both level and trend** — essential for products with growth/decline.
- $\beta$ is the trend smoothing constant.
- $m$ = number of periods ahead.

### B. Causal / Associative Methods (independent variables used)

#### Linear Regression (Trend Line)
Models demand as a linear function of time (or another causal variable $x$):

$$D = a + b \cdot t$$

The slope and intercept are estimated by **least-squares minimization**:

$$b = \frac{n \sum tD - \sum t \sum D}{n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2}$$

$$a = \frac{\sum D}{n} - b \cdot \frac{\sum t}{n}$$

---

## 2.4 Forecast Accuracy Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)** | $MAD = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum \vert D_i - F_i \vert$ | Average magnitude of error; **most common in KTU** |
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum (D_i - F_i)^2$ | Penalizes large errors heavily |
| **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)** | $MAPE = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum \dfrac{\vert D_i - F_i \vert}{D_i} \times 100$ | Scale-independent; expressed as % |
| **Tracking Signal (TS)** | $TS = \dfrac{\text{Cumulative Error (RSFE)}}{\text{MAD}}$ | Detects systematic bias; $\vert TS \vert \leq 4$ is acceptable |

Where the **Running Sum of Forecast Errors (RSFE)** is:

$$RSFE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_i - F_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Rule:** Use `\vert` (NOT `|`) in LaTeX inside tables to prevent syntax breakage.

---

## 2.5 Aggregate Planning Strategies

### Strategy 1: Chase Strategy
> Production matches demand period-by-period; **workforce level fluctuates**.

- ✅ Low inventory holding cost
- ❌ High hiring/firing cost; morale issues

### Strategy 2: Level Strategy
> Production and workforce are kept **constant**; inventory absorbs demand variation.

- ✅ Stable workforce, low turnover
- ❌ High inventory holding + shortage/backorder costs

### Strategy 3: Mixed / Hybrid Strategy
> Combination of both — uses overtime, subcontracting, and partial level planning.

- ✅ Lowest total cost in most real situations
- ❌ Complex to manage

---

## 2.6 Aggregate Planning Cost Components

| Cost Type | Symbol | Triggered When |
|-----------|--------|----------------|
| **Production (Regular Time)** | $C_{P}$ | Every unit made in regular hours |
| **Overtime Production** | $C_{OT}$ | Production above regular capacity |
| **Subcontracting** | $C_{SC}$ | Outsourced units |
| **Hiring Cost** | $C_{H}$ | Workforce expansion |
| **Layoff / Firing Cost** | $C_{L}$ | Workforce reduction |
| **Inventory Holding** | $C_{H}$ | Carrying leftover stock per period |
| **Backorder / Shortage** | $C_{B}$ | Unmet demand carried forward |

**Total Cost Equation (KTU Board Exam Favorite):**

$$\text{TC} = C_{P} \sum P_t + C_{OT} \sum OT_t + C_{SC} \sum SC_t + C_{H} \sum H_t + C_{L} \sum L_t + C_{B} \sum B_t$$

---

## 2.7 Real-World Industrial Application

- **Amazon** uses Holt-Winters forecasting for **billions of SKUs** daily.
- **Hindustan Unilever** uses level strategy with safety stock for FMCG.
- **Apple** uses chase strategy for iPhone launches (variable demand, premium cost).
- **Tata Steel** uses linear regression for commodity demand linked to GDP/auto sales.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Forecast by Simple Exponential Smoothing

**Problem (Modeled on KTU 2019 Pattern):**
The quarterly demand for a product (in units) over 5 quarters is given below. Use **Simple Exponential Smoothing with $\alpha = 0.3$** to forecast demand for periods 2 through 6. Take $F_1 = D_1$. Also compute MAD, MSE, and Tracking Signal.

| Period $t$ | Actual Demand $D_t$ |
|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 200 |
| 2 | 250 |
| 3 | 240 |
| 4 | 280 |
| 5 | 230 |

### Step 1: Initialize
$F_1 = D_1 = 200$ (given as starting condition)

### Step 2: Apply SES Recursion $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ with $\alpha = 0.3$

$$F_2 = 0.3(200) + 0.7(200) = 60 + 140 = 200$$

$$F_3 = 0.3(250) + 0.7(200) = 75 + 140 = 215$$

$$F_4 = 0.3(240) + 0.7(215) = 72 + 150.5 = 222.5$$

$$F_5 = 0.3(280) + 0.7(222.5) = 84 + 155.75 = 239.75$$

$$F_6 = 0.3(230) + 0.7(239.75) = 69 + 167.825 = 236.825 \approx 237$$

### Step 3: Compute Forecast Errors $e_t = D_t - F_t$

$$e_2 = 250 - 200 = 50$$

$$e_3 = 240 - 215 = 25$$

$$e_4 = 280 - 222.5 = 57.5$$

$$e_5 = 230 - 239.75 = -9.75$$

### Step 4: Compute Absolute Errors and Squared Errors

| $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $e_t^2$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|-------------------|---------|
| 2 | 250 | 200.00 | 50.00 | 50.00 | 2500.00 |
| 3 | 240 | 215.00 | 25.00 | 25.00 | 625.00 |
| 4 | 280 | 222.50 | 57.50 | 57.50 | 3306.25 |
| 5 | 230 | 239.75 | -9.75 | 9.75 | 95.06 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **122.75** | **142.25** | **6526.31** |

### Step 5: Compute Error Metrics (n = 4 valid forecasts)

$$MAD = \frac{142.25}{4} = 35.56 \text{ units}$$

$$MSE = \frac{6526.31}{4} = 1631.58 \text{ units}^2$$

### Step 6: Tracking Signal

$$RSFE = 122.75 \quad (\text{sum of signed errors})$$

$$TS = \frac{RSFE}{MAD} = \frac{122.75}{35.56} = 3.45$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $\vert TS \vert = 3.45 \leq 4$, the forecast is **acceptable** but approaching the upper control limit. This indicates a **mild positive bias** — actual demand is consistently exceeding the forecast. Recommendation: increase $\alpha$ or shift to trend-adjusted method.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Linear Regression Forecast

**Problem:** Use **Least-Squares Linear Regression** with the following 6-period demand series to forecast periods 7 and 8.

| $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $D$ | 50 | 56 | 60 | 63 | 67 | 70 |

### Step 1: Tabulate the required sums

| $t$ | $D$ | $tD$ | $t^2$ |
|-----|-----|------|-------|
| 1 | 50 | 50 | 1 |
| 2 | 56 | 112 | 4 |
| 3 | 60 | 180 | 9 |
| 4 | 63 | 252 | 16 |
| 5 | 67 | 335 | 25 |
| 6 | 70 | 420 | 36 |
| **$\sum = 21$** | **$\sum = 366$** | **$\sum = 1349$** | **$\sum = 91$** |

### Step 2: Compute Slope $b$

$$b = \frac{n \sum tD - \sum t \sum D}{n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2}$$

$$b = \frac{6(1349) - (21)(366)}{6(91) - (21)^2} = \frac{8094 - 7686}{546 - 441} = \frac{408}{105} \approx 3.886$$

### Step 3: Compute Intercept $a$

$$a = \frac{\sum D}{n} - b \cdot \frac{\sum t}{n} = \frac{366}{6} - 3.886 \times \frac{21}{6}$$

$$a = 61.0 - 3.886 \times 3.5 = 61.0 - 13.60 = 47.40$$

### Step 4: Forecast Equation

$$D = 47.40 + 3.886 \, t$$

### Step 5: Forecasts

$$F_7 = 47.40 + 3.886(7) = 47.40 + 27.20 = 74.60 \approx 75 \text{ units}$$

$$F_8 = 47.40 + 3.886(8) = 47.40 + 31.09 = 78.49 \approx 78 \text{ units}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Aggregate Planning (Chase vs Level)

**Problem (Full 14-Mark KTU Style):**
A company expects demand of **100, 120, 80, 150** units across 4 months. Beginning inventory = 0, ending inventory must be 0. The company currently employs 10 workers producing 25 units/worker/month. Relevant costs:
- Regular-time production cost: ₹30/unit
- Inventory holding: ₹5/unit/month
- Hiring cost: ₹400/worker
- Firing cost: ₹600/worker
- Subcontracting: ₹50/unit
- Lost sales / backorder: ₹100/unit

Compare the **Chase Strategy** and the **Level Strategy** based on total cost.

### CHASE STRATEGY
Workforce is varied to match demand exactly each month. No inventory carry-over.

| Month | Demand | Workers Required | Hiring | Firing | Production Cost | Sub Total |
|-------|--------|------------------|--------|--------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | 100 | 4 | (10-4)→fire 6 × 600 = 3600 | — | 100 × 30 = 3000 | 6600 |
| 2 | 120 | 5 | (5-4)→hire 1 × 400 = 400 | — | 120 × 30 = 3600 | 4000 |
| 3 | 80 | 4 | — | (4-5)→fire 1 × 600 = 600 | 80 × 30 = 2400 | 3000 |
| 4 | 150 | 6 | (6-4)→hire 2 × 400 = 800 | — | 150 × 30 = 4500 | 5300 |
| **Total** | | | | | | **₹18,900** |

### LEVEL STRATEGY
Workforce constant at 6 workers (avg demand / 25 = 6). Production constant = 150/month. Use inventory to absorb variation.

| Month | Demand | Prod | Inv. Begin | Inv. End | Holding Cost | Prod Cost |
|-------|--------|------|------------|----------|--------------|-----------|
| 1 | 100 | 150 | 0 | 50 | 5×50 = 250 | 4500 |
| 2 | 120 | 150 | 50 | 80 | 5×80 = 400 | 4500 |
| 3 | 80 | 150 | 80 | 150 | 5×150 = 750 | 4500 |
| 4 | 150 | 150 | 150 | 150→0 (end) | 0 (closing) | 4500 |
| **Total** | | | | | **₹1400** | **₹18,000** |

**Hiring:** From 10 to 6 workers → fire 4 → 4×600 = ₹2400 (one-time)

$$\text{Total Level Cost} = 18000 + 1400 + 2400 = \textbf{₹21,800}$$

### DECISION
$$\text{Chase Total Cost} = \text{₹ } 18,900 \;\; < \;\; \text{Level Total Cost} = \text{₹ } 21,800$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** The **Chase Strategy** is preferred in this scenario because the demand fluctuation is high and hiring/firing costs are relatively moderate. In practice, a **mixed strategy** often wins — and this is the most common refinement asked in Part (b) of 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.4 Summary of Computation Logic
- **Forecast question:** Always compute $\alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ step-by-step. Never combine periods.
- **Regression question:** Tabulate $t$, $D$, $tD$, $t^2$ BEFORE applying formulas.
- **Aggregate planning:** Always start with a **workforce table** mapping demand → workers needed → hire/fire decisions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Forecasting & Planning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Historical Demand Data] --> B[Data Cleaning and Validation]
    B --> C{Data Pattern Type}
    C -->|Numerical Past| D[Quantitative Methods]
    C -->|No Past Data| E[Qualitative Methods]
    D --> D1[Time Series Models]
    D --> D2[Causal Regression Models]
    D1 --> F1[Naive Forecast]
    D1 --> F2[Moving Average]
    D1 --> F3[Exponential Smoothing]
    D1 --> F4[Trend Adjusted Holt]
    D2 --> F5[Linear Regression]
    E --> E1[Delphi Method]
    E --> E2[Market Research]
    E --> E3[Jury of Executive Opinion]
    F1 --> G[Forecast Output]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    G --> H[Error Analysis MAD MSE MAPE]
    H --> I{Tracking Signal within 4}
    I -->|Yes| J[Accept Forecast]
    I -->|No| K[Re tune Parameters]
    K --> D
    J --> L[Aggregate Planning Input]
    L --> M[Choose Strategy Chase Level Hybrid]
    M --> N[Production Workforce Inventory Plan]
    N --> O[Execute and Monitor]
```

## 4.2 Decision Matrix — Selecting a Forecasting Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Decision Start] --> B{Available Data}
    B -->|More than 50 data points| C{Trend Present}
    B -->|Less than 50 points| D[Simple Moving Average]
    B -->|No data| E[Qualitative Methods]
    C -->|No| F[Exponential Smoothing]
    C -->|Yes Linear| G[Holt Trend Adjusted]
    C -->|Yes Seasonal| H[Winters Triple Smoothing]
    F --> I[Compute MAD MAPE]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    D --> I
    E --> I
    I --> J{Error Acceptable}
    J -->|Yes| K[Deploy Forecast]
    J -->|No| L[Adjust alpha or method]
    L --> F
```

## 4.3 Aggregate Planning Strategy Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Demand Forecast Input] --> Decision{Strategy Selection}
    Decision -->|Chase| C1[Workforce matches demand every period]
    Decision -->|Level| L1[Constant production rate every period]
    Decision -->|Mixed| M1[Overtime and Subcontracting combined]
    C1 --> C2[Hiring and Firing costs dominant]
    C1 --> C3[Low inventory holding]
    C1 --> C4[Output: Variable Headcount Plan]
    L1 --> L2[Inventory buildup and drawdown]
    L1 --> L3[High inventory holding cost]
    L1 --> L4[Output: Constant Headcount Plan]
    M1 --> M2[Selective overtime in peak months]
    M1 --> M3[Subcontracting for demand spikes]
    M1 --> M4[Output: Hybrid Resource Plan]
    C4 --> CostEval[Total Cost Comparison]
    L4 --> CostEval
    M4 --> CostEval
    CostEval --> FinalOpt[Choose Minimum Total Cost Plan]
```

## 4.4 Forecast Error Feedback Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Generate Forecast F t] --> B[Observe Actual Demand D t]
    B --> C[Compute Error e t = D t minus F t]
    C --> D[Update RSFE and MAD]
    D --> E{Tracking Signal Exceeds 4}
    E -->|Yes| F[Bias Detected: Re tune Model]
    E -->|No| G[Forecast Acceptable: Proceed]
    F --> A
    G --> H[Pass to Aggregate Planning Module]
```
