# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define Aggregate Planning. List any two strategies used in aggregate planning. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
> Aggregate planning is a medium-range (3 to 18 months) planning process that matches production capacity with forecasted demand at minimum total cost by adjusting workforce levels, production rates, inventory, and backorders. It works on aggregated product families rather than individual SKUs.
>
> **Two strategies:**
> 1. **Chase Strategy** — workforce is varied to exactly match demand each period.
> 2. **Level Strategy** — workforce and production are held constant; inventory absorbs fluctuations.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for strategies with brief explanation)*

### Question 2: Differentiate between Qualitative and Quantitative forecasting methods. Give one example of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Qualitative | Quantitative |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| **Basis** | Judgment, opinion, intuition | Numerical historical data |
| **Data Requirement** | Little or no past data | Requires large dataset |
| **Best For** | New products, disruptive tech | Established products |
| **Example** | Delphi Method, Market Research | Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing |

*(3 marks)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** The monthly demand (in units) for a product is given below. Use **Simple Exponential Smoothing with α = 0.4** to forecast demand for months 2 to 7. Take $F_1 = D_1$. Compute **MAD and Tracking Signal** for the forecast. Comment on the acceptability of the forecast. **(7 marks)**

| Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|-------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand | 100 | 120 | 110 | 130 | 125 | 140 |

**(b)** Explain **Causal (Associative) forecasting** with a suitable example. How is **Linear Regression** used to build a causal forecast? State the formulas for slope and intercept. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION (a)

**Step 1: Initialize**
$F_1 = D_1 = 100$, $\alpha = 0.4$

**Step 2: Apply SES recursion $F_{t+1} = 0.4 D_t + 0.6 F_t$**

$$F_2 = 0.4(100) + 0.6(100) = 100.0$$

$$F_3 = 0.4(120) + 0.6(100.0) = 48.0 + 60.0 = 108.0$$

$$F_4 = 0.4(110) + 0.6(108.0) = 44.0 + 64.8 = 108.8$$

$$F_5 = 0.4(130) + 0.6(108.8) = 52.0 + 65.28 = 117.28$$

$$F_6 = 0.4(125) + 0.6(117.28) = 50.0 + 70.368 = 120.368$$

$$F_7 = 0.4(140) + 0.6(120.368) = 56.0 + 72.221 = 128.221 \approx 128$$

**Step 3: Compute errors**

| $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|-------------------|
| 2 | 120 | 100.00 | 20.00 | 20.00 |
| 3 | 110 | 108.00 | 2.00 | 2.00 |
| 4 | 130 | 108.80 | 21.20 | 21.20 |
| 5 | 125 | 117.28 | 7.72 | 7.72 |
| 6 | 140 | 120.37 | 19.63 | 19.63 |
| **Sum** | | | **70.55** | **70.55** |

**Step 4: Compute MAD and Tracking Signal**

$$MAD = \frac{70.55}{5} = 14.11 \text{ units}$$

$$RSFE = 70.55 \quad ; \quad TS = \frac{70.55}{14.11} = 5.0$$

> **Comment:** $\vert TS \vert = 5.0 > 4$ → Forecast shows **persistent positive bias**. The simple exponential smoothing is **unacceptable**; we must re-tune (increase $\alpha$) or shift to a **trend-adjusted (Holt's) method** to capture the upward trend visible in the demand series.

### MODEL SOLUTION (b)

**Causal (Associative) Forecasting:**
> Causal forecasting identifies and uses **independent variables (leading indicators)** that *cause* demand to change. Unlike time-series methods that use only past demand, causal methods incorporate external factors such as price, advertising spend, GDP, weather, etc.

**Example:** Forecasting ice-cream sales using **temperature** as the leading indicator. As temperature rises, ice-cream demand rises — a clear causal relationship.

**Linear Regression Model:**

$$D = a + b \cdot x$$

where $x$ is the causal variable (e.g., temperature) and $D$ is demand.

**Least-Squares Estimates:**

$$b = \frac{n \sum xD - \sum x \sum D}{n \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$$

$$a = \frac{\sum D - b \sum x}{n}$$

The forecast for a future period is obtained by plugging in the expected value of $x$:

$$F_{t+1} = a + b \cdot x_{t+1}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - July 2023]`

**(a)** Define **Forecasting** in supply chain management. Compare **Chase and Level strategies** of aggregate planning on any five parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm forecasts the demand for the next 4 quarters as **2000, 3000, 2500, and 4000 units**. The firm employs 20 workers producing 200 units/worker/quarter. Beginning and ending inventory = 0. Relevant costs:
- Regular production: ₹40/unit
- Inventory holding: ₹8/unit/quarter
- Hiring: ₹500/worker; Firing: ₹800/worker

Develop an **aggregate plan using the Chase Strategy** and compute the total cost. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION (a)

**Forecasting Definition** (3 marks):
> Forecasting in supply chain management is the systematic estimation of future demand using historical data, market intelligence, and causal analysis. It serves as the primary input for planning procurement, production, distribution, and inventory.

**Comparison: Chase vs Level Strategy** (4 marks):

| Parameter | Chase Strategy | Level Strategy |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Workforce** | Variable — hire/fire each period | Constant throughout |
| **Inventory** | Near zero; no carry-over | Builds up and depletes |
| **Inventory Cost** | Low | High |
| **Hiring/Firing Cost** | High | Low (one-time only) |
| **Workforce Morale** | Poor (job insecurity) | High (stable jobs) |
| **Best For** | Skilled, low-cost labor markets | High-skill, scarce labor |

### MODEL SOLUTION (b)

**Workforce Calculations:**

| Quarter | Demand | Workers Required | Workers Change | Hiring Cost | Firing Cost | Prod Cost |
|---------|--------|------------------|----------------|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | 2000 | 10 | 20→10 = fire 10 | — | 10×800 = 8000 | 2000×40 = 80,000 |
| 2 | 3000 | 15 | 10→15 = hire 5 | 5×500 = 2500 | — | 3000×40 = 120,000 |
| 3 | 2500 | 12.5→13 | 15→13 = fire 2 | — | 2×800 = 1600 | 2500×40 = 100,000 |
| 4 | 4000 | 20 | 13→20 = hire 7 | 7×500 = 3500 | — | 4000×40 = 160,000 |
| **TOTAL** | | | | **₹6,000** | **₹9,600** | **₹4,60,000** |

$$\text{Total Chase Cost} = 6{,}000 + 9{,}600 + 4{,}60{,}000 = \textbf{₹ } 4{,}75{,}600$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Level strategy would keep 12 workers (avg demand/200) constant, producing 2400/quarter. Inventory would absorb the difference, but holding costs would add up. For a comparison answer, the student is expected to also compute the level strategy cost.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Forecasting and Aggregate Planning:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert α to its complement** — writing $0.4 \times F_t$ instead of $0.6 \times F_t$ (a 1-mark deduction cascade).
> 2. **Mixing up MAD and MSE** — using $(D-F)^2$ when MAD is asked (or vice versa). Always read the question twice.
> 3. **Wrong indexing in SES** — many students write $F_t = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_{t-1}$ for the *forecast of period t*, but the correct recursion for *next period's* forecast is $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$. **Off-by-one errors cost 2-3 marks.**
> 4. **Aggregate Plan: ignoring hiring cost from initial workforce adjustment.** When shifting to a level strategy, students often forget the one-time cost of moving from 20 to 12 workers.
> 5. **Forecast for Holt's method** — students compute $L_t$ and $T_t$ correctly but forget the "$+m \cdot T_t$" term for multi-period-ahead forecasts.
> 6. **TS formula error** — dividing RSFE by MAD when MAD = 0 (constant series) — this should be flagged as a divide-by-zero issue, not a silent failure.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 Core:**
> - ⭐ Forecasting is the **input** to aggregate planning; both are sequential, not parallel.
> - ⭐ **Time series** = past demand only; **Causal** = includes external variables.
> - ⭐ **Five classic methods:** Naive, MA, WMA, SES, Holt's (trend-adjusted).
> - ⭐ **MAD** is the most common error metric in KTU questions.
> - ⭐ **Tracking Signal range:** $\vert TS \vert \leq 4$ → acceptable; otherwise re-tune.
> - ⭐ **Three aggregate planning strategies:** Chase, Level, Mixed.
> - ⭐ **Hire/Fire + Production + Holding + Backorder** = Total Cost.
> - ⭐ Always start aggregate planning with a **workforce table** showing transitions.
> - ⭐ Linear regression formulas require a **summation table** before computation.
> - ⭐ Holt's two-parameter model handles **trends**; Winters handles **trend + seasonality**.
> - ⭐ When $\vert TS \vert > 4$, the forecast is **biased**, not just inaccurate — choose a different model.
> - ⭐ **No-code rule:** In KTU 2024 engineering exams, do **not** write Python/algorithms unless the course is a programming elective.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Forecasting and Aggregate Planning Models for Supply Chains**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cycle Inventory Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET524 - LOGISTICS & SUPPLY CHAIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cycle Inventory Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — CYCLE INVENTORY MODELS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cycle Inventory (also called Cycle Stock)** is the average inventory a firm builds up when it produces or procures a fixed quantity **Q** of a product, and that quantity is consumed gradually until the next replenishment arrives. It is the *repeating, saw-tooth portion* of on-hand inventory that exists **purely because orders are placed in lots rather than continuously**.

In the formal KTU 2024 lexicon, cycle inventory is the portion of total inventory that results from ordering (or producing) in **discrete lots of size Q**, and it is a direct consequence of the **fixed-order-cost vs. holding-cost trade-off** that is the heart of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) family of models.

Mathematically, when an order of size **Q** is received and demand is drawn down at a constant rate **D** (units/year), the inventory level swings linearly from **Q down to 0**, repeating every cycle. The **average cycle inventory** is therefore:

$$\bar{I}_{cycle} = \frac{Q}{2}$$

The companion metrics used in KTU problems are:

- **Cycle time (T)** = period between two consecutive orders = **Q / D** (in years).
- **Reorder Point (ROP)** = demand during lead time **L** = **D × L** (for deterministic demand).
- **Number of orders per year** = **D / Q**.
- **Order frequency** = **1 / T = D / Q** orders/year.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — the water-bottle refill cycle.** Imagine you are thirsty at home. Instead of buying one bottle of water every time you take a sip, you go to the store once a week and buy a pack of **10 bottles**. After a week, you finish all 10 and refill again. The average number of bottles lying on your kitchen counter throughout the week is **10/2 = 5 bottles**. Those 5 bottles are your *cycle inventory*. You could reduce it to 1 by buying one bottle every day, but then you would have to *walk to the store every day* (extra ordering effort/cost). The cycle inventory is therefore the price you pay to **batch your purchases** and amortise a fixed ordering cost across many units.

In the same way, every firm — whether a kirana store, a Maruti assembly line, or an Amazon FC — orders or produces in lots, and that batching creates a permanent, repeating stock of items. The goal of cycle inventory models is to **find the lot size Q that makes this batching the cheapest possible**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used Throughout Module 2

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **D** | Annual demand rate | units/year |
| **Q** | Order quantity (lot size) | units/order |
| **S** | Fixed ordering cost per order | ₹/order |
| **H** | Holding (carrying) cost per unit per year | ₹/unit/year |
| **P** | Unit purchase / production cost | ₹/unit |
| **h** | **H = i × P**, where *i* is the holding-charge fraction (typically 0.20 – 0.30) | ₹/unit/year |
| **p** | Production rate (EPQ model) | units/year |
| **L** | Replenishment lead time | days / weeks |
| **R** | Reorder point | units |
| **T** | Cycle time = Q/D | years |
| **TC** | Total annual relevant cost | ₹/year |

### 1.4 The Three Classical Cycle Inventory Models Covered in KTU

1. **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** — instantaneous replenishment, constant demand, no stockouts.
2. **Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)** — finite production rate, constant demand, no stockouts.
3. **Quantity-Discount (All-Units) Model** — EOQ extended to handle price breaks offered by suppliers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model

The EOQ model (also called the *Wilson-Lot-Size* or *Square-Root Formula*) is built on seven classical assumptions:

1. Demand **D** is **known, constant, and independent** of time.
2. Lead time **L** is known and **constant**.
3. Order is received **instantaneously** (i.e., the entire lot arrives at once).
4. **No stockouts are permitted** (i.e., inventory is replenished the instant it hits zero).
5. Order cost **S** is **fixed per order**, independent of Q.
6. Holding cost **H** is **linear** in average inventory (₹/unit/year).
7. Only the **product itself** is considered (no joint ordering).

> [!NOTE]
> Under these assumptions, the total annual relevant cost is the sum of three components: **purchase cost + ordering cost + holding cost**. Because purchase cost **D × P** does **not depend on Q** (price is fixed), we can ignore it when finding the optimal Q. The relevant cost simplifies to the **ordering + holding cost** pair.

### 2.2 Why the Total Cost Curve is U-Shaped — The Core "Why"

- **If Q is very small** (e.g., order 1 unit at a time) → you place **D** orders per year. Ordering cost = **D × S** is *huge*. Holding cost ≈ 0.
- **If Q is very large** (e.g., order 1 year’s supply at once) → ordering cost ≈ 0, but average inventory = Q/2 is *huge*. Holding cost is enormous.
- **Somewhere in between**, the two opposing forces balance, producing a **U-shaped total cost curve**. The bottom of the U is the EOQ.

This is the classical *square-root trade-off* and the cornerstone of every KTU Module-2 problem.

### 2.3 EPQ (Economic Production Quantity) Model

When the firm **produces internally** rather than ordering from outside, the production rate **p** (units/year) is typically **larger than demand D**; therefore, inventory **builds up gradually** during production and is **depleted gradually** between production runs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The EPQ model relaxes assumption (3) of EOQ — replenishment is **not instantaneous**, but spread over a production run of length **Q/p** years. The saw-tooth becomes a **trapezoid**.

Key EPQ results:

$$\text{Production run length} = \frac{Q}{p}$$

$$\text{Inventory builds up at rate } (p - D) \text{ during production}$$

$$\text{Maximum inventory (peak) } = Q \left(1 - \frac{D}{p}\right)$$

$$\text{Average inventory} = \frac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{D}{p}\right)$$

The optimal lot size is:

$$Q^*_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{p - D}}$$

Notice that when **p → ∞** (instantaneous replenishment), the second square-root term → 1, and the EPQ collapses back to the plain EOQ. This is the canonical "EOQ is a special case of EPQ" relationship KTU examiners love to test.

### 2.4 Quantity-Discount Model

Suppliers often quote **tiered prices**: "Buy 0–499 units → ₹100/unit, 500–999 units → ₹95/unit, ≥ 1000 units → ₹90/unit." KTU Module 2 uses the **All-Units Discount** variant — i.e., if your order size **Q crosses the price-break quantity**, *every* unit in that order is billed at the lower price.

> [!NOTE]
> The classical KTU decision procedure (must be reproduced in the exam for full marks):
> 1. For each price break **k** (in increasing order of price), compute the EOQ for that price: $Q^*_k = \sqrt{2DS / (i \cdot P_k)}$.
> 2. **Adjust upward** to the price-break quantity if $Q^*_k$ falls *below* the break (you must order at least the break quantity to enjoy that price).
> 3. Compute the **total annual cost** (purchase + ordering + holding) for each candidate.
> 4. **Pick the candidate with the lowest total cost** — it is *not* automatically the lowest price tier.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q^*_{EOQ} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ | Optimal order quantity | units |
| 2 | $T^* = Q^*/D = \sqrt{2S/(DH)}$ | Optimal time between orders | years |
| 3 | $N^* = D/Q^* = \sqrt{DH/(2S)}$ | Optimal number of orders/year | orders/yr |
| 4 | $\bar{I}_{cycle} = Q/2$ | Average cycle inventory | units |
| 5 | $R = D \times L$ | Reorder point (deterministic) | units |
| 6 | $TC_{rel}(Q) = (D/Q)S + (Q/2)H$ | Annual ordering + holding cost | ₹/yr |
| 7 | $TC_{total}(Q) = D\cdot P + (D/Q)S + (Q/2)H$ | Total annual cost (incl. purchase) | ₹/yr |
| 8 | $H = i \times P$ | Holding cost from carrying-charge rate | ₹/unit/yr |
| 9 | $Q^*_{EPQ} = \sqrt{2DS/H} \cdot \sqrt{p/(p-D)}$ | Optimal production lot size | units |
| 10 | $\bar{I}_{EPQ} = (Q/2)(1 - D/p)$ | Average cycle inventory (EPQ) | units |
| 11 | $t_p = Q/p$ | Production run length | years |
| 12 | $t_d = Q/D \cdot [p/(p-D)]$ | Cycle length (EPQ) | years |
| 13 | $\text{Savings}_{new-old} = TC(Q^*) - TC(Q_{old})$ | Cost saving from optimising Q | ₹/yr |
| 14 | $\theta = \text{EOQ}/D$ | Average flow time through cycle stock | years |
| 15 | $\theta = Q/(2D)$ | Average flow time (when cycle inventory is $Q/2$) | years |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units discipline rule for KTU valuation:** If D is in *units/month* and S is in *₹/order*, then H **must also** be in *₹/unit/month*. Mixing annual and monthly rates inside the same square-root is the **#1 mark-killer** in EPQ/EOQ problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EOQ Formula

We start from the total annual relevant cost as the sum of the ordering and holding components.

### Step 1 — Express the two cost components

- **Number of orders per year** = D / Q (because D units must be replenished, Q units per order).
- **Annual ordering cost** = (number of orders) × (cost per order) = (D / Q) × S.
- **Average cycle inventory** = Q / 2 (because inventory falls linearly from Q to 0 in one cycle).
- **Annual holding cost** = (average inventory) × (holding cost per unit per year) = (Q / 2) × H.

### Step 2 — Write the total annual cost function

$$TC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q}\,S}_{\text{ordering}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2}\,H}_{\text{holding}}$$

We ignore the constant purchase term $D \cdot P$ because it does not depend on Q and therefore does not affect the optimality condition.

### Step 3 — Take the first derivative with respect to Q

$$\frac{d\,TC(Q)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Set the derivative to zero and solve

Setting the derivative to zero corresponds to the bottom of the U-shaped cost curve:

$$-\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

Bring the negative term to the right-hand side:

$$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^{2}}$$

Cross-multiply:

$$H \cdot Q^{2} = 2DS$$

Solve for Q:

$$Q^{2} = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

This is the **Economic Order Quantity**.

### Step 5 — Verify it is a minimum using the second-derivative test

$$\frac{d^{2}\,TC(Q)}{dQ^{2}} = \frac{2DS}{Q^{3}} \;>\; 0 \quad \text{for all } Q>0$$

The second derivative is strictly positive, confirming the stationary point is a **global minimum**.

### Step 6 — Substitute $Q^*$ back into TC to get minimum cost

$$TC_{\min} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}}S + \frac{Q^{*}}{2}H$$

Using $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$, the two terms at optimum are **equal**:

$$TC_{\min} = \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}\,H = \sqrt{2DSH}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key interpretation:** At the optimal lot size, the **annual ordering cost equals the annual holding cost** — both equal $\sqrt{DSH/2}$. This is the famous "intersection point" that KTU examiners frequently ask students to comment on (often worth 2 marks in a 14-mark question).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How Each Input Changes Q*

| What changes? | Direction of change | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Demand **D** doubles | $Q^{*}$ grows by factor $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ | More units to spread fixed cost over |
| Order cost **S** doubles | $Q^{*}$ grows by factor $\sqrt{2}$ | Larger lot amortises bigger fixed cost |
| Holding cost **H** doubles | $Q^{*}$ shrinks by factor $1/\sqrt{2}$ | Holding becomes more expensive → order less |
| Unit price **P** doubles (so H = iP doubles) | $Q^{*}$ shrinks by factor $1/\sqrt{2}$ | Same as above |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> A retailer sells **10,000** units/year of a product. Ordering cost **S = ₹200/order**. Holding cost **H = ₹10/unit/year**. Find the EOQ, the optimal number of orders per year, the cycle time, and the minimum total cost.

### Given

- D = 10,000 units/year
- S = ₹200/order
- H = ₹10/unit/year

### Step 1 — Compute EOQ

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 10000 \cdot 200}{10}} = \sqrt{\frac{4{,}000{,}000}{10}} = \sqrt{400{,}000} = 632.46 \text{ units}$$

### Step 2 — Optimal number of orders per year

$$N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{10{,}000}{632.46} = 15.81 \text{ orders/year}$$

### Step 3 — Optimal cycle time

$$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{632.46}{10{,}000} = 0.06325 \text{ year} \approx 23.1 \text{ days}$$

### Step 4 — Minimum total relevant cost

$$TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 D S H} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 200 \cdot 10} = \sqrt{40{,}000{,}000} = ₹6{,}324.56/\text{year}$$

### Step 5 — Verify ordering = holding at optimum

$$\text{Ordering cost} = \frac{10000}{632.46} \times 200 = ₹3{,}162.28$$

$$\text{Holding cost} = \frac{632.46}{2} \times 10 = ₹3{,}162.28 \quad \checkmark$$

Both are equal, as the theory predicts.

## 3.4 EPQ Worked Numerical Example

> A factory produces an item at rate **p = 20,000 units/year**, demand is **D = 5,000 units/year**, ordering / setup cost **S = ₹100/run**, holding cost **H = ₹8/unit/year**. Find EPQ, the maximum inventory level, and the production run length.

### Step 1 — Compute EPQ

$$Q^{*}_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{p - D}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 5000 \cdot 100}{8}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{20000}{15000}}$$

$$= \sqrt{125{,}000} \cdot \sqrt{1.3333} = 353.55 \cdot 1.1547 = 408.25 \text{ units}$$

### Step 2 — Maximum (peak) inventory

$$I_{\max} = Q^{*}_{EPQ}\left(1 - \frac{D}{p}\right) = 408.25 \times \left(1 - \frac{5000}{20000}\right) = 408.25 \times 0.75 = 306.19 \text{ units}$$

### Step 3 — Production run length

$$t_p = \frac{Q^{*}}{p} = \frac{408.25}{20000} = 0.02041 \text{ year} \approx 7.45 \text{ days}$$

## 3.5 Quantity-Discount Worked Numerical Example

> A supplier offers: 0–499 units → ₹10/unit, 500–999 units → ₹9.50/unit, ≥ 1000 units → ₹9.00/unit. Annual demand **D = 5,000 units**, order cost **S = ₹100/order**, holding-charge rate **i = 0.20**. Find the optimal order quantity.

### Step 1 — Compute holding cost at each price tier

$$H_1 = 0.20 \times 10 = ₹2.00, \quad H_2 = 0.20 \times 9.50 = ₹1.90, \quad H_3 = 0.20 \times 9.00 = ₹1.80$$

### Step 2 — Compute EOQ at each tier (unconstrained)

$$Q^*_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 5000 \cdot 100}{2.00}} = \sqrt{500{,}000} = 707.1 \text{ units}$$

$$Q^*_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 5000 \cdot 100}{1.90}} = \sqrt{526{,}315.8} = 725.5 \text{ units}$$

$$Q^*_3 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 5000 \cdot 100}{1.80}} = \sqrt{555{,}555.6} = 745.4 \text{ units}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the KTU adjustment rule

- $Q^*_1 = 707.1$ is **above the 500 break** → feasible as 707.1 in tier 2 (price ₹9.50).
- $Q^*_2 = 725.5$ is feasible in tier 2 (≥ 500 and < 1000).
- $Q^*_3 = 745.4$ is **below the 1000 break** → adjust **up to 1000** in tier 3.

### Step 4 — Total cost for each candidate

For Q = 707.1 @ ₹9.50:

$$TC_1 = 5000(9.50) + \frac{5000}{707.1}(100) + \frac{707.1}{2}(1.90) = 47{,}500 + 707.1 + 671.7 = 48{,}878.8$$

For Q = 725.5 @ ₹9.50:

$$TC_2 = 5000(9.50) + \frac{5000}{725.5}(100) + \frac{725.5}{2}(1.90) = 47{,}500 + 689.2 + 689.2 = 48{,}878.4$$

For Q = 1000 @ ₹9.00:

$$TC_3 = 5000(9.00) + \frac{5000}{1000}(100) + \frac{1000}{2}(1.80) = 45{,}000 + 500 + 900 = 46{,}400$$

### Step 5 — Decision

**Q\* = 1000 units** in tier 3 gives the lowest total cost of **₹46,400/year**, even though the *unit price* is the lowest there. The savings versus tier 2 are ₹48,878.4 − 46,400 ≈ ₹2,478/year.
