# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. Define the term **Cycle Inventory** and write its formula for a firm ordering in lot size Q. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Cycle inventory, also called *cycle stock*, is the average amount of inventory a firm holds as a direct result of producing or procuring in fixed lot sizes **Q** rather than continuously. It is the repeating, saw-tooth portion of on-hand inventory that swings between **Q** (just after a replenishment) and **0** (just before the next replenishment).
>
> **Formula (1 mark):** Under constant demand D, the average cycle inventory is:
>
> $$\bar{I}_{cycle} = \frac{Q}{2}$$

---

### Q2. State the assumption of **instantaneous replenishment** in the EOQ model and explain why it is the key difference between the EOQ and the EPQ models. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Assumption (1 mark):** In the basic EOQ model, it is assumed that the entire order of size Q is received **in one instant** the moment inventory hits zero; the lead time is fixed but the receipt itself takes zero time.
>
> **Key difference (2 marks):** The EPQ (Economic Production Quantity) model **removes this assumption**. Production (or receiving) happens at a finite rate **p** > D, so inventory **builds up gradually** over a production run of length **Q/p**, reaches a peak $Q(1 - D/p)$, and only **then** starts being depleted by demand. EOQ is therefore the limiting case of EPQ as **p → ∞**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Q-A or Q-B)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

A manufacturing firm uses **12,000 units/year** of a raw material. The cost of placing one order is **₹150**, and the holding cost is **₹6 per unit per year**. Lead time is **15 days** and the firm operates 300 days/year.

**(a)** Determine the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**, the **optimal number of orders per year**, the **cycle time**, and the **reorder point**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the **total annual cost** of operating at the EOQ. The firm is currently ordering **800 units** at a time; calculate the **annual savings** if it switches to the EOQ. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**Given:** D = 12,000 units/yr, S = ₹150/order, H = ₹6/unit/yr, L = 15 days, operating days = 300/yr.

**EOQ (2 marks):**

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 150}{6}} = \sqrt{600{,}000} = 774.6 \text{ units}$$

**Optimal number of orders per year (2 marks):**

$$N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{12{,}000}{774.6} = 15.49 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Cycle time (1.5 marks):**

$$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{774.6}{12{,}000} = 0.0645 \text{ year}$$

Converting to days: $T^* = 0.0645 \times 300 = 19.36$ days.

**Reorder point (1.5 marks):** Daily demand = D / operating days = 12,000 / 300 = 40 units/day.

$$R = d \times L = 40 \times 15 = 600 \text{ units}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Annual ordering cost at EOQ (1 mark):**

$$\frac{D}{Q^{*}} \times S = \frac{12{,}000}{774.6} \times 150 = ₹2{,}323.8$$

**Annual holding cost at EOQ (1 mark):**

$$\frac{Q^{*}}{2} \times H = \frac{774.6}{2} \times 6 = ₹2{,}323.8$$

**Total annual cost at EOQ (1 mark):**

$$TC_{EOQ} = 2{,}323.8 + 2{,}323.8 = ₹4{,}647.6/\text{year}$$

**Total annual cost at Q = 800 (1.5 marks):**

$$TC_{800} = \frac{12{,}000}{800} \times 150 + \frac{800}{2} \times 6 = 2{,}250 + 2{,}400 = ₹4{,}650/\text{year}$$

**Annual savings (1 mark):**

$$\text{Savings} = TC_{800} - TC_{EOQ} = 4{,}650 - 4{,}647.6 = ₹2.4/\text{year}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part-A style):**
> 1. Mixing annual and daily units in the reorder-point formula is the **single most common mistake** — *always* convert L to the same time unit as D.
> 2. Forgetting to convert "days/year" of plant operation when computing cycle time in days costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. Reporting Q\* as 774 (rounded down) instead of 774.6 (or 775) is acceptable; reporting 700 or 800 due to early rounding is **not**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

A factory produces a component at the rate of **30,000 units/year**, against a steady demand of **6,000 units/year**. Setup cost per production run is **₹250**, and the holding cost is **₹10 per unit per year**. Assume the production starts each time inventory hits zero.

**(a)** Derive the **Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)** from first principles and compute its numerical value for the data given. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the **maximum inventory level**, the **production run length**, and the **length of one complete cycle**. Also determine the **total annual cost (excluding purchase)** at the EPQ. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**Setup (2 marks):** Let Q be the production lot size. The average inventory under EPQ is $\bar{I} = (Q/2)(1 - D/p)$ because inventory builds up at rate $(p - D)$ during production and falls at rate $D$ after production stops.

**Total relevant cost function (1 mark):**

$$TC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q}S}_{\text{setup}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{D}{p}\right)H}_{\text{holding}}$$

**First derivative (2 marks):**

$$\frac{d\,TC}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \frac{D}{p}\right)H = 0$$

**Solve for Q (2 marks):**

$$Q^{2} = \frac{2DS}{H\left(1 - D/p\right)} = \frac{2DS}{H} \cdot \frac{p}{p - D}$$

$$Q^{*}_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{p - D}}$$

**Numerical value (kept here for continuity, also earns marks in (a)):**

$$Q^{*}_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6000 \times 250}{10}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{30{,}000}{24{,}000}} = \sqrt{300{,}000} \cdot \sqrt{1.25} = 547.72 \times 1.1180 = 612.4 \text{ units}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Maximum inventory (2 marks):**

$$I_{\max} = Q^{*}_{EPQ}\left(1 - \frac{D}{p}\right) = 612.4 \times \left(1 - \frac{6000}{30000}\right) = 612.4 \times 0.80 = 489.9 \text{ units}$$

**Production run length (1.5 marks):**

$$t_p = \frac{Q^{*}_{EPQ}}{p} = \frac{612.4}{30{,}000} = 0.02041 \text{ year} \approx 5.21 \text{ days}$$

**Complete cycle length (1.5 marks):** Total cycle = production run + consumption of peak inventory at rate D.

$$T_{cycle} = t_p + \frac{I_{\max}}{D} = \frac{Q^{*}}{p} + \frac{Q^{*}}{D}\left(1 - \frac{D}{p}\right) = \frac{Q^{*}}{D}\cdot\frac{p}{p-D}$$

$$= \frac{612.4}{6000} \times \frac{30{,}000}{24{,}000} = 0.1021 \times 1.25 = 0.1276 \text{ year} \approx 46.8 \text{ days}$$

**Total annual cost (2 marks):**

$$\text{Setup cost} = \frac{6000}{612.4} \times 250 = ₹2{,}449$$

$$\text{Holding cost} = \frac{612.4}{2} \times (0.80) \times 10 = ₹2{,}449$$

$$TC = 2{,}449 + 2{,}449 = ₹4{,}898/\text{year}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part-B style):**
> 1. Students often forget the $(1 - D/p)$ factor and write $\bar I = Q/2$ for EPQ — that is the **EOQ formula**, not the EPQ one. **Loss: 2 marks minimum.**
> 2. In the cycle-length formula, students sometimes write $T = Q/D$ (the EOQ form) instead of the correct EPQ form $T = (Q/D) \cdot p/(p-D)$.
> 3. Computing setup and holding cost components *separately* and *not* verifying they are equal at the optimum is a missed opportunity to score 1–2 easy marks.
> 4. Reporting $Q^*$ as 612 or 613 without a decimal is acceptable; reporting 500 or 700 due to sloppy arithmetic is not.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist (must-memorise for KTU Module 2):**

- **Cycle inventory** is the average of a saw-tooth inventory profile driven by fixed lot size Q; it equals **Q / 2** for instantaneous replenishment and **(Q/2)(1 − D/p)** for finite production.
- **EOQ formula** is $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — the famous **square-root** formula, sometimes called the Wilson lot-size formula.
- **At the optimal EOQ, ordering cost = holding cost**, and the common value is $\sqrt{DSH/2}$.
- **EPQ formula** is the EOQ formula multiplied by the **production factor** $\sqrt{p/(p-D)}$. EPQ → EOQ as **p → ∞**.
- **Reorder point (deterministic)** is **R = d × L** where d = D / working-days and L is in days.
- **Quantity-discount procedure** has 6 steps: list tiers → compute Hk → compute EOQk → adjust upward at break → compute TC at each candidate → pick minimum TC (not minimum price).
- **Average flow time** through cycle stock equals **Q / (2D)** — a useful metric for customer-lead-time analysis.
- **Units discipline is critical**: keep D, S, H all in the same time basis (annual, monthly, weekly) inside a single square-root.
- **EOQ and EPQ assume no stockouts, deterministic demand, fixed lead time, no joint ordering, linear holding cost** — KTU problems will not violate these, but a 1-line comment on relaxing each assumption earns easy marks.
- **Total cost formula** $TC = D \cdot P + (D/Q)S + (Q/2)H$ is universal; always include the $D \cdot P$ term when the question asks for the **total** cost (not just *relevant* cost).
- **Key decision rules to memorise**:
  - If order cost S rises → order **more** per order.
  - If holding cost H rises → order **less** per order.
  - If demand D rises → order **more** per order (by factor $\sqrt{2}$ when D doubles).
  - If supplier offers quantity discount → **always** evaluate every candidate EOQ at every price break; lowest unit price is not the lowest total cost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discounting Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET524 - LOGISTICS & SUPPLY CHAIN ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Discounting Models # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Discounting Models** are quantitative engineering economic techniques used to evaluate the time-dependent worth of cash flows occurring at different points in the project life cycle by mathematically converting all future receipts (inflows) and disbursements (outflows) into a **common equivalent monetary datum** (Present, Future, or Annual equivalent), using a defined **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** or **cost of capital (i)**. In KTU-syllabus language, discounting models form the **analytical backbone of capital investment decision-making** in logistics, warehousing, transportation fleet selection, and supply chain network design. They allow a logistics manager to: 1. **Compare mutually exclusive alternatives** (e.g., Owned truck vs. Leased truck vs. 3PL contract). 2. **Determine economic feasibility** of a single project (e.g., Construction of a new warehouse). 3. **Rank competing capital projects** under capital rationing. The cardinal mathematical principle is the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** — a Rupee (or Dollar) available *today* is worth more than the same amount available *tomorrow* because of its earning potential. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **The Mango Stall Analogy 🍋** > Imagine a street vendor offers you two choices: > - **Option A:** Take ₹100 *right now*. > - **Option B:** Take ₹100 *after one year*. > > Any rational individual picks **Option A**. Why? Because ₹100 today, if parked in a savings account at 8% interest, becomes ₹108 in a year. So "future ₹100" is equivalent to "≈ ₹92.59 today" at i = 8%. > > This process of "shrinking" future money back to today's value is precisely what **discounting** does. In a supply chain context, consider a logistics firm deciding between: - **Buying a delivery truck** for ₹20,00,000 today (single outflow now, savings over 8 years), or - **Leasing the same truck** at ₹3,50,000/year for 8 years (recurring outflows). The amounts occur at *different times*, so direct comparison is meaningless. **Discounting Models** transform both into a common yardstick (e.g., Present Worth), enabling an apples-to-apples comparison. ## 1.3 The Six Standard Cash Flow Conversion Factors Every discounting model is built upon six fundamental engineering economy factors. The KTU examiner expects instant recall of these. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Single P, F, A" Trinity** > - **P** = Present worth (a single sum at time 0) > - **F** = Future worth (a single sum at time n) > - **A** = Uniform Annual series (equal end-of-period amounts over n periods) > - **i** = Interest rate per period (decimal form, e.g., 10% = 0.10) > - **n** = Number of compounding periods (years, in most logistics projects) The six canonical factors derived from these three variables are tabulated in Section 2. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Logical Foundation — Why Discounting is Necessary In logistics and supply chain engineering, capital decisions involve: 1. **Large initial outlays** (warehouse construction, ERP software, fleet procurement). 2. **Operational savings or revenues** spread over 5–20 years. 3. **Salvage values** at project termination. 4. **Recurring costs** (fuel, labor, maintenance, insurance). Because money has *time value*, simply adding these numbers (undiscounted summation) produces a mathematically valid but **economically misleading** result. Discounting rectifies this by weighting future cash flows by $(1+i)^{-n}$. ## 2.2 The Six Master Factors (KTU Formula Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All KTU Numerical Problems in this module are reducible to these six factors.** Memorize the factor NAMES and their FORMULAS, not just symbols. | # | Factor Name | Find → Given | Algebraic Formula | Excel/Standard Notation | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Single Payment Compound Amount | Find F, given P | $F = P(1+i)^n$ | $(F/P, i, n)$ | | 2 | Single Payment Present Worth | Find P, given F | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | $(P/F, i, n)$ | | 3 | Uniform Series Sinking Fund | Find A, given F | $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $(A/F, i, n)$ | | 4 | Uniform Series Compound Amount | Find F, given A | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | $(F/A, i, n)$ | | 5 | Capital Recovery (CRF) | Find A, given P | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $(A/P, i, n)$ | | 6 | Uniform Series Present Worth | Find P, given A | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | $(P/A, i, n)$ | > [!NOTE] > **Reciprocal Property:** The factors come in reciprocal pairs. > - $(F/P) = 1/(P/F)$ > - $(F/A) = 1/(A/F)$ > - $(A/P) = 1/(P/A)$ ## 2.3 Standard Symbols Used in KTU Problems - **i** = Effective annual interest rate (MARR). KTU typically uses 8%, 10%, 12%, 15%. - **n** = Project life in years (integer; KTU rarely tests fractional n). - **P** = Present worth at Year 0. - **F** = Future worth at Year n. - **A** = End-of-year uniform annual amount (occurs at end of each year for n years). - **S** or **SV** = Salvage value at end of project. - **CR** = Capitalized cost (P with infinite life). - **B-C Ratio** = Benefit-Cost ratio = PV(Benefits) / PV(Disbenefits). ## 2.4 The Three Core Decision Models | # | Model | Core Equation | Decision Rule (Single Project) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Net Present Value (NPV / PW)** | $PW = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | Accept if PW ≥ 0 | | 2 | **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC / AW)** | $AW = PW \cdot (A/P, i, n)$ | Accept if AW of cost is minimized (or AW of benefit is maximized) | | 3 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | Solve: $0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t}$ | Accept if IRR ≥ MARR | ## 2.5 Special Engineering Economic Factors (Logistics Applications) In supply chain problems, cash flows are often **NOT uniform**. They may have: - **Arithmetic gradient** (e.g., maintenance cost rising by a constant ₹G each year). - **Geometric gradient** (e.g., fuel cost rising by a constant percentage r each year). - **Discrete sporadic cash flows** (e.g., major overhaul in Year 5). ### 2.5.1 Arithmetic Gradient Series (G) Cash flow pattern: 0, G, 2G, 3G, ..., (n−1)G - **P/G Factor:** $P = G \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - i \cdot n - 1}{i^2(1+i)^n}$ - **A/G Factor:** $A = G \cdot \left[\dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{n}{(1+i)^n - 1}\right]$ ### 2.5.2 Geometric Gradient Series (g) Cash flow pattern: $A_1, A_1(1+g), A_1(1+g)^2, \ldots$ - **P of Geometric Series:** $P = A_1 \cdot \dfrac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1+g}{1+i}\right)^n}{i - g}$ (when $i \neq g$) ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Logistics & Supply Chain | Logistics Application | Discounting Model Used | |---|---| | Fleet replacement analysis (own vs lease) | NPV, EUAC | | Warehouse construction vs. rented godown | B-C Ratio, PW | | Cold-chain investment with rising energy cost | Geometric Gradient PW | | Equipment with major mid-life overhaul | NPV with discrete negative CF | | Perpetual infrastructure (rail siding, port berth) | Capitalized Cost = P × (A/P,i,∞) = P·i | | Justifying a WMS/ERP software rollout | IRR, PW | | Vendor-managed inventory (VMI) partnership | B-C Ratio | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mantra:** Always state the **MARR (i)** explicitly at the start of every solution. If not given in the problem, the KTU paper usually provides it. Never invent an interest rate. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **This section builds every formula from first principles.** KTU examiners love derivations because they test whether the student truly understands discounting or has merely memorized factors. ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Single Payment Compound Amount Factor (F/P) ### Setup You deposit a principal **P** at Year 0 in an account earning interest rate **i** compounded annually. How much is the balance **F** at the end of **n** years? ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Year 0 (Initial Deposit):** $$\text{Balance}_0 = P$$ **Year 1:** Interest earned = $P \cdot i$; New balance = $P + Pi = P(1+i)$ **Year 2:** Interest earned = $P(1+i) \cdot i$; New balance = $P(1+i) + P(1+i)i = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^2$ **Year 3:** By induction, $P(1+i)^3$ **General Year k:** $P(1+i)^k$ **Final Year n:** $$\boxed{F = P(1+i)^n} \quad \text{(Equation 1)}$$ ### Verification using Logs Take natural log of both sides: $$\ln F = \ln P + n \ln(1+i)$$ This confirms the **exponential growth** nature of compound interest. For $i=10\%$, $n=8$ years: $$(F/P, 10\%, 8) = (1.10)^8 = 2.1436$$ So ₹1 today becomes ₹2.14 in 8 years at 10%. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Single Payment Present Worth Factor (P/F) ### Setup What is the present equivalent (P) of a single future sum F occurring at year n? ### Derivation Starting from Equation 1: $F = P(1+i)^n$. Solve for P: $$P = F \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)^n}$$ $$\boxed{P = F(1+i)^{-n}} \quad \text{(Equation 2)}$$ This is simply Equation 1 rearranged. The factor $(1+i)^{-n}$ is the **discount factor** — it "shrinks" future money. ### Numerical Illustration At $i = 12\%$, what is the present worth of ₹5,00,000 received at the end of Year 5? $$P = 5{,}00{,}000 \times (1.12)^{-5}$$ **Step 1:** Compute $(1.12)^5$ $$(1.12)^5 = 1.12 \times 1.12 \times 1.12 \times 1.12 \times 1.12$$ $$= 1.2544 \times 1.12 = 1.404928$$ $$= 1.404928 \times 1.12 = 1.573519$$ $$= 1.573519 \times 1.12 = 1.762342$$ $$= 1.762342 \times 1.12 = 1.973823$$ **Step 2:** Take reciprocal $$(1.12)^{-5} = \frac{1}{1.973823} = 0.50663$$ **Step 3:** Multiply by F $$P = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50663 = ₹2{,}53{,}315$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A lump sum of ₹5,00,000 in 5 years is economically equivalent to having ₹2,53,315 in hand today, assuming a 12% discount rate. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor (F/A) ### Setup You deposit an end-of-year amount **A** for **n** years into an account earning **i** per year. What is the total balance F at the end of year n? ### Derivation **Year 1 deposit A:** Earns interest for (n−1) years → grows to $A(1+i)^{n-1}$ **Year 2 deposit A:** Earns interest for (n−2) years → grows to $A(1+i)^{n-2}$ **Year 3 deposit A:** Grows to $A(1+i)^{n-3}$ ... **Year k deposit A:** Grows to $A(1+i)^{n-k}$ ... **Year n deposit A:** Grows to $A(1+i)^0 = A$ ### Summation $$F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \ldots + A(1+i)^1 + A(1+i)^0$$ $$F = A \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (1+i)^k$$ This is a geometric series with first term = 1, ratio = (1+i), number of terms = n. Using the standard geometric sum formula: $$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} r^k = \frac{r^n - 1}{r - 1}$$ Therefore: $$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i) - 1} = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$ $$\boxed{F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}} \quad \text{(Equation 3)}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Capital Recovery Factor (A/P) ### Setup You borrow principal P at interest rate i. You must repay it in equal end-of-year installments A for n years. Find A. ### Derivation The total of all installments A over n years, when accumulated to year n, must equal the principal P grown to year n. $$P(1+i)^n = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$ Solving for A: $$A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$ $$\boxed{A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}} \quad \text{(Equation 4)}$$ ### Numerical Illustration A logistics firm purchases a forklift for **₹8,00,000** with a bank loan at **10%** interest, to be repaid in **5 equal annual installments**. Find A. **Step 1:** Identify factor = $(A/P, 10\%, 5)$ **Step 2:** Compute $(1.10)^5$ $$(1.10)^5 = 1.61051$$ **Step 3:** Substitute into formula $$A = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.10 \times 1.61051}{1.61051 - 1}$$ $$A = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.161051}{0.61051}$$ $$A = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.26380$$ $$\boxed{A = ₹2{,}11{,}036 \text{ per year}}$$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Net Present Value (NPV) of Mixed Cash Flows ### Setup A supply chain project has: - Initial investment $C_0$ at Year 0 - Operating cash flows $CF_1, CF_2, \ldots, CF_n$ - Salvage value $S$ at Year n Find the Net Present Value at MARR = i. ### Derivation (Step-by-Step) The NPV is the algebraic sum of present worths of every cash flow: $$NPV = -C_0 + \frac{CF_1}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{CF_2}{(1+i)^2} + \ldots + \frac{CF_n}{(1+i)^n} + \frac{S}{(1+i)^n}$$ **Combining the last two terms** (since both are at year n): $$NPV = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} + \frac{S}{(1+i)^n}$$ $$\boxed{NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}} \quad \text{(General Equation)}$$ ### Full Numerical Worked Example > [!NOTE] > **KTU-Style Problem:** A logistics company is evaluating an automated sorting system. Initial cost = ₹30,00,000; it generates net savings of ₹6,00,000/year for 6 years; salvage = ₹4,00,000 at end of Year 6. MARR = 12%. Find NPV. **Cash Flow Table:** | Year (t) | Cash Flow (₹) | Discount Factor (1.12)⁻ᵗ | Present Worth (₹) | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | −30,00,000 | 1.00000 | −30,00,000 | | 1 | +6,00,000 | 0.89286 | +5,35,714 | | 2 | +6,00,000 | 0.79719 | +4,78,316 | | 3 | +6,00,000 | 0.71178 | +4,27,068 | | 4 | +6,00,000 | 0.63552 | +3,81,311 | | 5 | +6,00,000 | 0.56743 | +3,40,457 | | 6 | +10,00,000* | 0.50663 | +5,06,631 | | **Total** | | | **NPV = −3,30,503** | *Year 6 cash flow = 6,00,000 savings + 4,00,000 salvage = 10,00,000 **Detailed Step-by-Step Calculation of Year 6 PW:** $$PW_6 = 10{,}00{,}000 \times (1.12)^{-6}$$ We know $(1.12)^{-5} = 0.50663$. Therefore: $$(1.12)^{-6} = 0.50663 \times (1.12)^{-1} = \frac{0.50663}{1.12} = 0.45235$$ $$PW_6 = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.45235 = ₹4{,}52{,}349$$ Wait — let me recompute the table above using consistent values: $(1.12)^{-1} = 0.892857$ $(1.12)^{-2} = 0.797194$ $(1.12)^{-3} = 0.711780$ $(1.12)^{-4} = 0.635518$ $(1.12)^{-5} = 0.567427$ $(1.12)^{-6} = 0.506631$ | Year | CF (₹) | DF | PW (₹) | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | −30,00,000 | 1.000000 | −30,00,000.00 | | 1 | +6,00,000 | 0.892857 | +5,35,714.29 | | 2 | +6,00,000 | 0.797194 | +4,78,316.33 | | 3 | +6,00,000 | 0.711780 | +4,27,068.15 | | 4 | +6,00,000 | 0.635518 | +3,81,310.85 | | 5 | +6,00,000 | 0.567427 | +3,40,456.12 | | 6 | +10,00,000 | 0.506631 | +5,06,631.00 | | **Σ** | | | **−3,30,503.26** | ### Decision $$\boxed{NPV = -₹3{,}30{,}503}$$ > [!WARNING] > Since NPV is **negative**, the project is **NOT economically justified** at 12% MARR. **Reject.** --- ## 3.6 Derivation 6: Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC) ### Setup A capital project has Present Worth PW. Find the equivalent annual amount A. ### Derivation This is the direct application of the Capital Recovery Factor (Equation 4) rearranged: $$A = PW \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} = PW \cdot (A/P, i, n)$$ ### Numerical Illustration A warehouse has PW = ₹20,00,000 at i = 10%, n = 10 years. Find EUAC. **Step 1:** Compute $(A/P, 10\%, 10)$ From tables: $(1.10)^{10} = 2.59374$ $$(A/P) = \frac{0.10 \times 2.59374}{2.59374 - 1} = \frac{0.259374}{1.59374} = 0.162745$$ **Step 2:** Compute EUAC $$EUAC = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.162745 = ₹3{,}25{,}490 \text{ per year}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Owning this warehouse is equivalent to paying a rent of ₹3,25,490 every year for 10 years. If a comparable rented godown is available for less than this, leasing is preferable. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Visualization Mandate:** The following Mermaid block diagrams map the *information architecture* of discounting models — how the six master factors interlock, and how a typical logistics decision flows from raw cash flow to final acceptance/rejection. ## 4.1 Master Architecture: The Six Factor Inter-Conversion Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A0([P at Year 0]) -- Multiply by F over P
times 1 plus i to the n --> B0([F at Year n]) B0 -- Multiply by P over F
times 1 plus i to the power minus n --> A0 A0 -- Multiply by A over P
CRF --> C0([Uniform A for n years]) C0 -- Multiply by P over A --> A0 C0 -- Multiply by F over A --> B0 B0 -- Multiply by A over F
Sinking Fund --> C0 classDef varNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff class A0,B0,C0 varNode ``` ## 4.2 Cash Flow Pattern Recognition Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD START([Cash Flow Description in Problem]) --> P1{Is it a single
lump sum?} P1 -- Yes, at Year 0 --> L1[Use P only] P1 -- Yes, at Year n --> L2[Use F and F to P factor] P1 -- No --> P2{Is it equal yearly
amounts?} P2 -- Yes --> L3[Use A and P over A or A over P factor] P2 -- No --> P3{Does it grow by a
constant amount G?} P3 -- Yes, arithmetic --> L4[Apply Arithmetic Gradient
P over G or A over G] P3 -- Yes, percentage --> L5[Apply Geometric Gradient
ratio g] P3 -- No --> L6[Sporadic discrete CFs
Sum individual PWs] L1 --> OUT([Compute PW or AW or IRR]) L2 --> OUT L3 --> OUT L4 --> OUT L5 --> OUT L6 --> OUT classDef startEnd fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef decision fill:#f57c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef process fill:#1565c0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff class START,OUT startEnd class P1,P2,P3 decision class L1,L2,L3,L4,L5,L6 process ``` ## 4.3 Logistics Capital Investment Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD S0([Logistics Project Proposal]) --> S1[Tabulate All Cash Flows
Year by Year] S1 --> S2[Identify MARR i from
Company Policy] S2 --> S3{Choose Evaluation
Method} S3 -- Method 1 --> S4[Compute PW at MARR] S3 -- Method 2 --> S5[Compute AW via CRF] S3 -- Method 3 --> S6[Compute IRR by iteration] S4 --> S7{PW greater than 0?} S5 --> S8{AW cost is minimum
among alternatives?} S6 --> S9{IRR greater than MARR?} S7 -- Yes --> S10([ACCEPT Project]) S7 -- No --> S11([REJECT Project]) S8 -- Yes --> S12([SELECT Alternative]) S8 -- No --> S13([Try Next Alternative]) S9 -- Yes --> S10 S9 -- No --> S11 classDef terminal fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef process fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef decision fill:#e65100,stroke:#fff,color:#fff class S0,S10,S11,S12,S13 terminal class S1,S2,S4,S5,S6 process class S3,S7,S8,S9 decision ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Discounting Model Use-Cases in Supply Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph C1[Procurement Decisions] C1A[Vendor Selection over 5 yr] C1B[Equipment Buy vs Lease] end subgraph C2[Warehousing Decisions] C2A[Build vs Rent Godown] C2B[WMS Software Investment] end subgraph C3[Transportation Decisions] C3A[Fleet Renewal Cycle] C3B[3PL Contract Evaluation] end subgraph C4[Network Decisions] C4A[Distribution Center Location] C4B[Cold Chain Infrastructure] end C1A --> DM[Discounting Models] C1B --> DM C2A --> DM C2B --> DM C3A --> DM C3B --> DM C4A --> DM C4B --> DM DM --> OUT1[NPV or PW] DM --> OUT2[EUAC or AW] DM --> OUT3[IRR] DM --> OUT4[B-C Ratio] classDef scenario fill:#37474f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef core fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef output fill:#1a237e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff class C1A,C1B,C2A,C2B,C3A,C3B,C4A,C4B scenario class DM core class OUT1,OUT2,OUT3,OUT4 output ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** 2-mark and 3-mark direct questions. The model answers below are written in 60-90 words each — the precise length KTU examiners expect. ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]** *Define discounting models in the context of logistics engineering economics. State any three discounting factors used in present worth analysis.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Discounting models are engineering economic methods that convert cash flows occurring at different time periods into an equivalent value at a common reference point (usually present time) using a defined Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR). They account for the time value of money. Three commonly used discounting factors are: 1. **Single Payment Present Worth Factor (P/F, i, n)** = $(1+i)^{-n}$ — converts a future sum F to present P. 2. **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (P/A, i, n)** = $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ — converts an annual series A to present P. 3. **Capital Recovery Factor (A/P, i, n)** = $\frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — converts a present sum P to equivalent annual A. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]** *Explain the concept of Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR) and its role in logistics capital investment decisions.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** MARR (i) is the minimum rate of return a company requires from a capital project to justify the investment; it represents the **opportunity cost of capital**. In logistics decisions (e.g., warehouse construction, fleet purchase), MARR serves as the **discount rate** used to evaluate all future cash flows. MARR is influenced by: - Cost of borrowed capital (interest rate on loans) - Expected return from alternative investments of similar risk - Risk premium specific to the logistics project If a project's IRR is **below MARR**, it is rejected; if IRR ≥ MARR, the project is acceptable. MARR thus acts as the **financial benchmark** for all discounting calculations. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** One Module-2 question with internal choice (a) or (b). Each choice has two sub-parts, typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Below are TWO fully worked-out alternative questions. ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2, 14 Marks]** A logistics company is evaluating two alternative forklift procurement options for its new distribution center: | Parameter | Option X (Diesel Forklift) | Option Y (Electric Forklift) | |---|---|---| | Initial Cost (₹) | 12,00,000 | 18,00,000 | | Annual Operating Cost (₹) | 2,00,000 | 1,20,000 | | Salvage Value (₹) | 2,00,000 | 3,00,000 | | Useful Life (years) | 8 | 8 | The MARR for the company is **10% per annum**. Use the **Present Worth method** to determine which alternative is more economical. #### **Part (a) [7 Marks] — Compute the PW of Option X** **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify all cash flows for Option X. - Year 0: −12,00,000 (initial cost) - Years 1–8: −2,00,000 (annual operating cost) - Year 8: +2,00,000 (salvage value, treated as inflow) **Step 2:** Compute the relevant factor values at i = 10%, n = 8. $(1.10)^8 = 2.14359$ (verified by sequential multiplication) $(1.10)^{-8} = \frac{1}{2.14359} = 0.46651$ $(P/A, 10\%, 8) = \frac{1.10^8 - 1}{0.10 \times 1.10^8} = \frac{2.14359 - 1}{0.10 \times 2.14359} = \frac{1.14359}{0.214359} = 5.33493$ **Step 3:** Compute PW of all operating costs (Years 1–8). $$PW_{\text{operating}} = -2{,}00{,}000 \times (P/A, 10\%, 8) = -2{,}00{,}000 \times 5.33493 = -₹10{,}66{,}986$$ **Step 4:** Compute PW of salvage value (Year 8). $$PW_{\text{salvage}} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times (P/F, 10\%, 8) = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.46651 = ₹93{,}302$$ **Step 5:** Compute total PW of Option X. $$PW_X = -12{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}66{,}986 + 93{,}302 = -₹21{,}73{,}684$$ $$\boxed{PW_X = -₹21{,}73{,}684}$$ > [!NOTE] > The negative sign simply indicates the project is a cost-incurring (cost-minimization) problem. Magnitude is what matters for comparison. #### **Part (b) [7 Marks] — Compute PW of Option Y and Recommend** **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify all cash flows for Option Y. - Year 0: −18,00,000 - Years 1–8: −1,20,000 - Year 8: +3,00,000 **Step 2:** Use the same factors (i = 10%, n = 8): (P/A) = 5.33493; (P/F) = 0.46651. **Step 3:** Compute PW of operating costs. $$PW_{\text{operating}} = -1{,}20{,}000 \times 5.33493 = -₹6{,}40{,}192$$ **Step 4:** Compute PW of salvage value. $$PW_{\text{salvage}} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.46651 = ₹1{,}39{,}953$$ **Step 5:** Compute total PW of Option Y. $$PW_Y = -18{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}40{,}192 + 1{,}39{,}953 = -₹23{,}00{,}239$$ $$\boxed{PW_Y = -₹23{,}00{,}239}$$ **Step 6: Decision and Recommendation.** Comparing magnitudes of present worth cost: - $PW_X = -₹21,73,684$ (lower cost magnitude) - $PW_Y = -₹23,00,239$ (higher cost magnitude) $$\boxed{\text{Option X (Diesel Forklift) is more economical by } ₹1{,}26{,}555}$$ > [!NOTE] > Despite the lower operating cost and higher salvage, the high initial cost of Option Y is not recovered over 8 years at 10% MARR. **Select Option X.** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2, 14 Marks]** A logistics firm is considering installation of a new automated conveyor system: - Initial investment: **₹25,00,000** - Estimated annual revenue (savings in labor): **₹5,50,000** for the first 4 years, then **₹4,00,000** for the next 4 years. - A major overhaul costing **₹3,00,000** is required at the **end of Year 4**. - Salvage value at end of Year 8: **₹2,50,000**. - MARR = **12% per annum**. Using the **NPV method**, determine whether the project should be accepted. #### **Part (a) [7 Marks] — Cash Flow Tabulation and PW of All Inflows** **Solution:** **Step 1:** Construct the year-by-year cash flow table. | Year (t) | Revenue | Overhaul | Salvage | Net Cash Flow (₹) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | — | — | — | −25,00,000 | | 1 | +5,50,000 | — | — | +5,50,000 | | 2 | +5,50,000 | — | — | +5,50,000 | | 3 | +5,50,000 | — | — | +5,50,000 | | 4 | +5,50,000 | −3,00,000 | — | +2,50,000 | | 5 | +4,00,000 | — | — | +4,00,000 | | 6 | +4,00,000 | — | — | +4,00,000 | | 7 | +4,00,000 | — | — | +4,00,000 | | 8 | +4,00,000 | — | +2,50,000 | +6,50,000 | **Step 2:** Compute discount factors at i = 12%. $(1.12)^1 = 1.12$ → $(1.12)^{-1} = 0.89286$ $(1.12)^2 = 1.2544$ → $(1.12)^{-2} = 0.79719$ $(1.12)^3 = 1.40493$ → $(1.12)^{-3} = 0.71178$ $(1.12)^4 = 1.57352$ → $(1.12)^{-4} = 0.63552$ $(1.12)^5 = 1.76234$ → $(1.12)^{-5} = 0.56743$ $(1.12)^6 = 1.97382$ → $(1.12)^{-6} = 0.50663$ $(1.12)^7 = 2.21068$ → $(1.12)^{-7} = 0.45235$ $(1.12)^8 = 2.47596$ → $(1.12)^{-8} = 0.40388$ **Step 3:** Multiply cash flows by discount factors to get present worths. | Year (t) | Net CF (₹) | Discount Factor | PW (₹) | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | −25,00,000 | 1.00000 | −25,00,000.00 | | 1 | +5,50,000 | 0.89286 | +4,91,071.43 | | 2 | +5,50,000 | 0.79719 | +4,38,457.53 | | 3 | +5,50,000 | 0.71178 | +3,91,479.94 | | 4 | +2,50,000 | 0.63552 | +1,58,879.16 | | 5 | +4,00,000 | 0.56743 | +2,26,970.85 | | 6 | +4,00,000 | 0.50663 | +2,02,652.54 | | 7 | +4,00,000 | 0.45235 | +1,80,939.66 | | 8 | +6,50,000 | 0.40388 | +2,62,523.65 | | **Σ** | | | **−1,46,025.24** | **Sum of Present Worth of Inflows (Years 1–8):** $$PW_{\text{inflow}} = 4{,}91{,}071 + 4{,}38{,}458 + 3{,}91{,}480 + 1{,}58{,}879 + 2{,}26{,}971 + 2{,}02{,}653 + 1{,}80{,}940 + 2{,}62{,}524$$ $$PW_{\text{inflow}} = ₹23{,}52{,}975$$ **Step 4:** Compute NPV. $$NPV = -25{,}00{,}000 + 23{,}52{,}975 = -₹1{,}47{,}025$$ #### **Part (b) [7 Marks] — Decision and Sensitivity Insight** **Solution:** **Step 1: Decision Rule Application.** $$\boxed{NPV = -₹1{,}47{,}025 < 0}$$ Since NPV is negative at MARR = 12%, the project **fails the present worth test**. $$\boxed{\text{Decision: REJECT the project at 12% MARR.}}$$ **Step 2: Sensitivity / Insight Analysis.** To understand how close the project is to viability, compute the breakeven MARR. The NPV equation is: $$-25{,}00{,}000 + \frac{5{,}50{,}000}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{5{,}50{,}000}{(1+i)^2} + \frac{5{,}50{,}000}{(1+i)^3} + \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{(1+i)^4} + \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{(1+i)^5} + \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{(1+i)^6} + \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{(1+i)^7} + \frac{6{,}50{,}000}{(1+i)^8} = 0$$ **Trial at i = 10%:** Re-evaluating with i = 10%: - PW inflow ≈ ₹25,41,000 (approximate) - NPV ≈ +₹41,000 **Trial at i = 11%:** NPV ≈ −₹53,000 (approximately) By linear interpolation, the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** ≈ **10.4%**. **Step 3: Insight.** Since the firm's MARR is 12% and the project's IRR is only ~10.4%, the project does not clear the hurdle rate. However, the project is *borderline* — a small reduction in initial cost (~₹1.5 lakh) or a marginal increase in revenue would tip the decision toward acceptance. > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion for the Board Examiner:** The project exhibits a marginal economic profile. The recommendation is to **reject at 12% MARR** but flag for re-evaluation if financing cost drops below 10.5%. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes (Where Marks Are Lost)** > > 1. **Forgetting the salvage value.** KTU problems frequently include a salvage value at year n. Students often ignore it, losing 1–2 marks. > > 2. **Sign convention errors.** Cash outflows (costs) MUST be negative; inflows (revenues, salvage) MUST be positive. Mixing signs produces wrong NPV. > > 3. **Using undiscounted payback instead of discounted payback.** If the problem specifies "discounting model", students must use the discounted payback method, not the simple payback. > > 4. **Forgetting to convert percentage to decimal.** Using i = 12 instead of 0.12 in formulas is a frequent blunder. KTU auto-grading is unforgiving. > > 5. **Compounding at the wrong frequency.** KTU problems are usually annual; if the problem says "compounded semi-annually", then i is halved and n is doubled. > > 6. **Choosing the WRONG factor.** Confusing (P/A) with (P/F) or (A/P) with (A/F) is a common 3-mark deduction. Always write the factor name and formula explicitly in the solution. > > 7. **Not stating MARR in the solution.** Examiners award a "presentation mark" for explicitly stating the MARR at the start. > > 8. **Rounding-off errors.** KTU valuation keys retain 4–5 decimal places during intermediate steps. Round only the FINAL answer. > > 9. **Arithmetic gradient direction.** A rising cost (positive G) versus a falling cost (negative G) — sign matters. > > 10. **In Perpetual investments, using a finite n.** Perpetuities (n → ∞) require Capitalized Cost = P / i, not a finite-life formula. --- ## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU PEPET524 — Module 2: Discounting Models** ### 🔹 Core Concept - **Discounting** = converting future cash flows to present value using $(1+i)^{-n}$. - **Time Value of Money (TVM):** ₹1 today > ₹1 tomorrow. - **MARR (i)** = benchmark discount rate (cost of capital + risk premium). ### 🔹 The Six Master Factors (must memorize with names and formulas) 1. **(F/P, i, n)** = $(1+i)^n$ 2. **(P/F, i, n)** = $(1+i)^{-n}$ 3. **(F/A, i, n)** = $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ 4. **(A/F, i, n)** = $\frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ (Sinking Fund) 5. **(P/A, i, n)** = $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ 6. **(A/P, i, n)** = $\frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ (Capital Recovery) ### 🔹 The Three Core Decision Models - **NPV / PW:** Accept if PW ≥ 0. - **AW / EUAC:** Select the alternative with minimum AW cost (or maximum AW benefit). - **IRR:** Accept if IRR ≥ MARR. ### 🔹 Special Models - **Capitalized Cost (perpetuity):** $CC = \frac{P}{i}$ for infinite life. - **B-C Ratio:** $B/C = \frac{PV(\text{Benefits})}{PV(\text{Disbenefits})}$ — accept if B-C ≥ 1. - **Arithmetic Gradient:** P/G factor for linearly increasing/decreasing series. - **Geometric Gradient:** $P = A_1 \cdot \frac{1 - [(1+g)/(1+i)]^n}{i - g}$ (when $i \neq g$). ### 🔹 Standard Procedure for Solving KTU Problems 1. **Read** the problem twice; identify MARR (i) and project life (n). 2. **Tabulate** all cash flows year-by-year (Year 0, 1, 2, ..., n). 3. **Identify** the cash flow pattern (single, uniform, gradient, mixed). 4. **Apply** the appropriate factor(s). 5. **Compute** PW, AW, or IRR. 6. **Compare** against the decision criterion. 7. **Conclude** with a single-sentence recommendation. ### 🔹 Key Reciprocal Properties - $(F/P) = 1 / (P/F)$ - $(F/A) = 1 / (A/F)$ - $(P/A) = 1 / (A/P)$ ### 🔹 Quick Sanity Checks - If **i = 0**: $(P/A, 0, n) = n$; $(F/P, 0, n) = 1$ (no time value). - If **n = 0**: All factors involving n = 1 (no compounding). - If **n → ∞**: $(A/P, i, \infty) = i$ → so $P = A/i$ (perpetuity). - As **i increases**, present worth **decreases** (future money is worth less today). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rule for KTU Exams:** In every long-answer solution, **draw a cash flow diagram (or describe it)** and **label the MARR and n explicitly at the start**. Examiners award 1–2 marks for this "engineering drawing discipline".

topics.title: Multi-Item Inventory Models and Production-Consumption Models Related to Supply Chains Including Multi-Echelon Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET524 - LOGISTICS & SUPPLY CHAIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multi-Item Inventory Models and Production-Consumption Models Related to Supply Chains Including Multi-Echelon Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Multi-Item Inventory Models
A **Multi-Item Inventory Model** is a deterministic inventory control framework in which a firm jointly determines the economic order quantities for **two or more SKUs (Stock Keeping Units)** that share a common, scarce resource. The shared resources are most commonly a **capital budget limit (B)** and a **warehouse floor-space constraint (W)**. The objective is to minimize the aggregate variable cost (setup + holding) subject to the binding constraint(s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** When several items compete for the same scarce resources (money, space, manpower), the simple EOQ formula applied independently to each item gives a *sub-optimal* solution. A coordinated multi-item model is mandatory.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a hostel mess kitchen that has a **fixed budget of ₹10,000 per month** and a **fixed refrigerator space of 50 cubic feet**. The manager must decide how many kilograms of rice, dal, vegetables, and milk to buy. Buying huge rice sacks saves trips to the market (fewer setup costs) but ties up cash and fridge space. The manager cannot run the EOQ for rice alone and for milk alone independently — the *coupling* through budget and space forces a single, coordinated order plan. This is exactly the multi-item inventory problem.

### 1.2 Production-Consumption Model (Economic Production Quantity — EPQ)
The **Economic Production Quantity (EPQ) Model**, also called the **Production Lot Size Model** or **Production-Consumption Model**, extends the classical EOQ by relaxing the assumption of *instantaneous replenishment*. Here, the item is produced internally at a **finite production rate (p)** while being consumed simultaneously at a **demand rate (d)**, with the condition **p > d**. The inventory therefore *builds up* during the production run and *depletes* afterwards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Because production and consumption overlap, the maximum inventory held is **less than the lot size Q**. The holding cost is therefore smaller than in classical EOQ, yielding a *larger* optimal lot size.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a water tank being filled by a tap (production rate p) while a tap at the bottom is open (consumption rate d). The water level rises at the *net rate (p − d)* during filling, and falls at rate d when the inlet is closed. The tank never holds the full quantity of water poured in — only the *net accumulation*.

### 1.3 Multi-Echelon Inventory Systems
A **Multi-Echelon Inventory System** is a supply chain network comprising **two or more sequential tiers (echelons)** of inventory stocking points, where the demand at each upper echelon is generated by the *replenishment orders* of the echelon immediately below it. The three canonical architectures are:

| Architecture | Flow Pattern | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Serial (Chain) System** | Plant → Warehouse → Retailer → Customer | A bakery chain's central bakery supplying city depots |
| **Distribution (Divergent) System** | One Plant → Many Warehouses → Many Retailers | FMCG manufacturer → regional C&FAs → distributors |
| **Assembly (Convergent) System** | Many Components → Sub-assembly → Final Product | Automobile assembly line (engine, chassis, body → car) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The Clark-Scarf (1960) and Roundy (1985) power-of-two results are the cornerstone theorems examined for multi-echelon systems.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Picture a river system. The factory is the *source*, the regional warehouse is the *confluence*, and the customer is the *sea*. Water (inventory) flows downstream. If you build a dam (safety stock) at every point, you waste water; if you coordinate the dams (echelons) through a single policy, you save enormous storage while keeping the sea from running dry. This coordination is the essence of multi-echelon optimization.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Notation
- **D** = annual demand (units/year)
- **d** = daily demand rate (units/day)
- **p** = production rate (units/day)
- **Q** = order/lot quantity
- **S (or K)** = setup (ordering) cost per order cycle (₹/order)
- **H (or h)** = holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
- **C** = unit purchase cost (₹/unit)
- **Im** = maximum inventory level
- **T** = cycle length (time between consecutive order placements)
- **n** = number of items
- **B** = budget limit (₹)
- **aᵢ** = floor space per unit of item *i* (m²/unit)
- **W** = available warehouse space (m²)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Multi-Item Inventory Model — Unconstrained vs. Constrained

**Step 1: Independent (Unconstrained) EOQ per Item**
If the budget and space constraints are *ignored*, the classical EOQ for each item is:
$$Q_i^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D_i S_i}{H_i}}$$

**Step 2: Aggregate Cost Function**
The total variable cost per year (excluding purchase cost) is:
$$TC(Q_1, Q_2, \ldots, Q_n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \frac{D_i S_i}{Q_i} + \frac{H_i Q_i}{2} \right]$$

**Step 3: Lagrangian Formulation (with budget constraint)**
To enforce a budget ceiling $B_c$ (allowing only $B_c$ rupees for inventory investment), the constraint is:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i Q_i \le B_c \quad \text{(or the average inventory cost constraint)}$$

A more common KTU form uses the **storage-space constraint**:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i Q_i \le W$$

**Step 4: Lagrangian Optimality Conditions**
Setting up the Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = TC - \lambda \left( \sum a_i Q_i - W \right)$, the KKT first-order conditions yield:
$$Q_i^{**} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D_i S_i}{H_i + 2\lambda a_i}}$$

If the resulting $Q_i^{**}$ satisfies the constraint exactly, the **constraint is binding** (active). If not, the unconstrained solution is the optimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the multiplier λ?** The Lagrangian multiplier $\lambda$ represents the *shadow price* — the marginal cost of consuming one more unit of the constrained resource (₹ per m² of additional warehouse space).

### 2.2 Production-Consumption (EPQ) Model — Mechanics

**Inventory Trajectory within a Cycle**
- **Phase 1 (Production, duration $t_1$):** Inventory rises at net rate $(p - d)$.
- **Phase 2 (Consumption only, duration $T - t_1$):** Inventory falls at rate $d$ back to zero.
- **Cycle length:** $T = Q/d$
- **Production time:** $t_1 = Q/p$ (since $p > d$, $t_1 < T$)
- **Maximum inventory:** $I_m = (p - d) \cdot t_1 = Q \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$

**Derivation of the Optimal Q**
Total annual cost:
$$TC(Q) = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{H \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{H \cdot Q}{2} \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$

Differentiating $TC(Q)$ with respect to $Q$ and equating to zero:
$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = 0$$

Solving:
$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)}}$$

**Minimum Total Variable Cost:**
$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2 D H S \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)}$$

**Optimal Cycle Time:**
$$T^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2S}{D H \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)}}$$

**Optimal Number of Setups per Year:**
$$N^{*} = \frac{1}{T^{*}} = \sqrt{\frac{D H \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)}{2S}}$$

### 2.3 Multi-Echelon Systems — Clark-Scarf and Roundy Power-of-Two Policies

**Serial Multi-Echelon (Clark-Scarf, 1960)**
For a serial chain of *k* stages with stage demands $d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_k$ and holding costs $h_1 \le h_2 \le \ldots \le h_k$, the optimal echelon-stock policy is **nested** — upper stages hold stock that *covers* the downstream demand plus the lower stage's stock. The cost function is the sum of echelon holding costs.

**Distribution (Divergent) System — Roundy's 98% Approximation**
Roundy (1985) proved that restricting the reorder intervals $T_j$ at each warehouse to be **integer powers of two** times a base period ($T_j = 2^k T_0$) gives a cost that is within **at most 2% (98% optimality)** of the unconstrained optimum. This is the celebrated **"Power-of-Two" (PoT) policy** — practically the most important multi-echelon result in industry.

**Assembly (Convergent) System**
At the final assembly stage, the bill-of-materials (BOM) defines the lead-time assembly structure. Echelon stock at component level is held to *decouple* the assembly process from supplier variability.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Model / Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Unit Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | EOQ (single item) | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ | Classical Wilson Formula |
| 2 | EOQ cycle time | $T^{*} = \sqrt{2S / (DH)}$ | Years |
| 3 | Min total cost | $TC^{*} = \sqrt{2DHS}$ | ₹/year |
| 4 | **EPQ (production)** | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS / [H(1 - d/p)]}$ | Requires $p > d$ |
| 5 | EPQ max inventory | $I_m = Q(1 - d/p)$ | Units |
| 6 | EPQ production time | $t_1 = Q/p$ | Days |
| 7 | EPQ cycle time | $T = Q/d$ | Days |
| 8 | EPQ min cost | $TC^{*} = \sqrt{2DHS(1 - d/p)}$ | ₹/year |
| 9 | Multi-item unconstrained | $Q_i = \sqrt{2 D_i S_i / H_i}$ | Per SKU |
| 10 | Multi-item constrained (Lagrangian) | $Q_i = \sqrt{2 D_i S_i / (H_i + 2\lambda a_i)}$ | Iterate λ to satisfy $\sum a_i Q_i = W$ |
| 11 | **Power-of-Two policy** (Roundy) | $T_j \in \lbrace 2^k T_0 \rbrace$ | Guarantees $\le 2\%$ suboptimality |
| 12 | Echelon stock (serial) | $S_j^{\text{ech}} = S_{j+1} + \text{stock at } j+1$ | Nested convention |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In every EPQ problem, write the constraint $p > d$ *first* — if violated, the model is invalid. The reduction factor $(1 - d/p)$ is the single most important term distinguishing EPQ from EOQ.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Multi-item models** govern procurement planning in any enterprise with a shared warehouse (Amazon FBA, Reliance Retail, industrial buyers of MRO items).
- **EPQ models** dictate batch production in dairy, pharmaceutical, FMCG, and discrete manufacturing where machines produce faster than customers consume.
- **Multi-echelon systems** form the mathematical backbone of every modern ERP/MRP system (SAP, Oracle SCM) and the **DRP (Distribution Requirements Planning)** logic used by global retailers like Walmart and Zara.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the EPQ Optimal Lot Size

**Given:** Annual demand $D$ (units/yr), production rate $p$ (units/day), demand rate $d$ (units/day), setup cost $S$ (₹/setup), holding cost $H$ (₹/unit/yr), and working days $N_d$ per year so that $D = d \cdot N_d$.

**Step 1 — Express inventory on hand as a function of time.**
During the production run of duration $t_1$, inventory builds at the net rate $(p - d)$:
$$I(t) = (p - d) \cdot t, \quad 0 \le t \le t_1$$
At $t = t_1$, inventory reaches its maximum:
$$I_m = (p - d) \cdot t_1$$
The lot size $Q$ equals the total amount produced, $Q = p \cdot t_1$, therefore:
$$t_1 = \frac{Q}{p} \quad \text{and} \quad I_m = (p - d) \cdot \frac{Q}{p} = Q \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average inventory.**
Inventory is a triangular shape: rises linearly from 0 to $I_m$ in time $t_1$, falls linearly from $I_m$ to 0 in time $(T - t_1)$ where $T = Q/d$. The area under the inventory curve is $\frac{1}{2} I_m T$, so the *average* inventory is:
$$\bar{I} = \frac{I_m}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Total cost function.**
Annual setup cost = (number of setups/yr) × (cost/setup) = $(D/Q) \cdot S$
Annual holding cost = average inventory × holding cost per unit = $\bar{I} \cdot H$
$$TC(Q) = \frac{D S}{Q} + \frac{H Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$

**Step 4 — First-order optimality.**
$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D S}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$

Setting the derivative to zero:
$$-\frac{D S}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = 0$$

Rearranging:
$$\frac{D S}{Q^2} = \frac{H}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$

$$Q^2 = \frac{2 D S}{H\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)}$$

$$\boxed{Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)}}}$$

**Step 5 — Minimum cost.**
Substituting $Q^{*}$ back:
$$TC^{*} = \frac{D S}{Q^{*}} + \frac{H Q^{*}}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = 2 \cdot \frac{D S}{Q^{*}} = 2 \cdot \frac{H Q^{*}}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$

Using $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2 D S / [H(1 - d/p)]}$:
$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2 D S \cdot H \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)} = \sqrt{2 D H S \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Sensitivity check.**
The second derivative is positive: $\frac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2 D S}{Q^3} > 0$ for $Q > 0$, confirming a minimum.

### 3.2 Lagrangian Derivation for Multi-Item Constrained Problem

**Problem:**
$$\min_{Q_i > 0} \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\frac{D_i S_i}{Q_i} + \frac{H_i Q_i}{2}\right]$$
subject to
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i Q_i \le W$$

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian.**
$$\mathcal{L}(Q_1, \ldots, Q_n, \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\frac{D_i S_i}{Q_i} + \frac{H_i Q_i}{2}\right] - \lambda\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i Q_i - W\right]$$

**Step 2 — First-order conditions (∂ℒ/∂Qᵢ = 0).**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Q_i} = -\frac{D_i S_i}{Q_i^2} + \frac{H_i}{2} - \lambda a_i = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Q_i$.**
$$\frac{D_i S_i}{Q_i^2} = \frac{H_i}{2} - \lambda a_i$$
$$Q_i^2 = \frac{2 D_i S_i}{H_i - 2\lambda a_i}$$
$$Q_i^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D_i S_i}{H_i - 2\lambda a_i}}$$

**Step 4 — Determine $\lambda$ from the binding constraint.**
Iterate (or solve numerically) for $\lambda$ such that:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \sqrt{\frac{2 D_i S_i}{H_i - 2\lambda a_i}} = W$$

If the resulting $H_i - 2\lambda a_i \le 0$ for some $i$, then $Q_i = 0$ (drop that item from stock) and recompute $\lambda$ over the remaining items.

**Step 5 — Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) complementary slackness.**
$$\lambda \ge 0, \quad \sum a_i Q_i - W \le 0, \quad \lambda \cdot \left(\sum a_i Q_i - W\right) = 0$$

If $\lambda = 0$, the constraint is *inactive* (unconstrained solution feasible) and the simple EOQ per item is optimal.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — EPQ (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question)

**Problem:** A factory produces a component at a rate of $p = 500$ units/day. The demand is $d = 200$ units/day, with 250 working days/year, so $D = 50{,}000$ units/yr. Setup cost $S = ₹200$ per setup, holding cost $H = ₹4$ per unit per year. Find: (a) the optimal lot size $Q^{*}$, (b) the maximum inventory $I_m$, (c) the production run time $t_1$, (d) the cycle time $T^{*}$, (e) the minimum total variable cost $TC^{*}$, and (f) the optimal number of production runs per year $N^{*}$.

**Solution:**

(a) Optimal lot size:
$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H(1 - d/p)}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 50{,}000 \times 200}{4 \times (1 - 200/500)}}$$

Compute the denominator term: $(1 - 200/500) = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$.
$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{20{,}000{,}000}{4 \times 0.6}} = \sqrt{\frac{20{,}000{,}000}{2.4}} = \sqrt{8{,}333{,}333.33} \approx 2886.75 \text{ units}$$

(b) Maximum inventory:
$$I_m = Q^{*}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = 2886.75 \times 0.6 \approx 1732.05 \text{ units}$$

(c) Production run time:
$$t_1 = \frac{Q^{*}}{p} = \frac{2886.75}{500} \approx 5.77 \text{ days}$$

(d) Cycle time:
$$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{d} = \frac{2886.75}{200} \approx 14.43 \text{ days}$$

(e) Minimum total cost:
$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2 D H S (1 - d/p)} = \sqrt{2 \times 50{,}000 \times 4 \times 200 \times 0.6}$$
$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{48{,}000{,}000} \approx 6928.20 \text{ ₹/year}$$

(f) Number of production runs per year:
$$N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{50{,}000}{2886.75} \approx 17.32 \text{ runs/yr}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $T^{*} \times N^{*} = 14.43 \times 17.32 \approx 250$ working days ✓.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Multi-Item Constrained Model

**Problem:** Two items share warehouse space. Item A: $D_A = 10{,}000$, $S_A = ₹100$, $H_A = ₹2$, $a_A = 1$ m²/unit. Item B: $D_B = 5{,}000$, $S_B = ₹150$, $H_B = ₹3$, $a_B = 2$ m²/unit. Available space $W = 150$ m². Find the constrained optimal $Q_A$ and $Q_B$.

**Step 1 — Unconstrained EOQs.**
$$Q_A^{0} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 100}{2}} = \sqrt{1{,}000{,}000} = 1000 \text{ units}$$
$$Q_B^{0} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 150}{3}} = \sqrt{500{,}000} \approx 707.11 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Check unconstrained feasibility.**
Required space: $a_A Q_A^0 + a_B Q_B^0 = 1(1000) + 2(707.11) = 2414.22$ m². Since $2414.22 \gg 150$, the constraint is *violated* and is **binding**.

**Step 3 — Solve Lagrangian.** With $Q_i^{*} = \sqrt{2 D_i S_i / (H_i + 2\lambda a_i)}$ (sign-flip: the multiplier enters with sign chosen for KKT), iterate $\lambda$ such that $a_A Q_A + a_B Q_B = 150$.

**First trial, $\lambda = 100$:**
$$Q_A = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 100}{2 + 200}} = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}000{,}000}{202}} \approx 99.50$$
$$Q_B = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 150}{3 + 400}} = \sqrt{\frac{1{,}500{,}000}{403}} \approx 61.00$$
Total space: $99.50 \times 1 + 61.00 \times 2 = 221.5$ m² — still too high.

**Second trial, $\lambda = 1000$:**
$$Q_A = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}000{,}000}{2002}} \approx 31.62, \quad Q_B = \sqrt{\frac{1{,}500{,}000}{2003}} \approx 27.37$$
Total: $31.62 + 2(27.37) = 86.36$ m² — too low.

**Third trial, $\lambda = 500$:**
$$Q_A = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}000{,}000}{1002}} \approx 44.68, \quad Q_B = \sqrt{\frac{1{,}500{,}000}{1003}} \approx 38.67$$
Total: $44.68 + 2(38.67) = 122.02$ m² — still low.

**Fourth trial, $\lambda = 350$:**
$$Q_A = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}000{,}000}{702}} \approx 53.39, \quad Q_B = \sqrt{\frac{1{,}500{,}000}{703}} \approx 46.20$$
Total: $53.39 + 2(46.20) = 145.79$ m² — very close to 150.

Converging: $\lambda^{*} \approx 320$ gives $Q_A \approx 55.1$, $Q_B \approx 48.3$, total $\approx 151.7$ m² (acceptable).

> [!TIP]
> In an exam, a single iteration with a clearly identified binding $\lambda$ receives full marks; numerical perfection is not required.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between the classical EOQ model and the EPQ (Production-Consumption) model. Why is the optimal lot size in EPQ always greater than or equal to the EOQ for the same demand and cost parameters?*

**Model Answer:**
The classical **EOQ model** assumes that an order is received *instantaneously* — the lot arrives in one shot and the maximum inventory equals the order quantity $Q$. The **EPQ model** (production-consumption) allows production and consumption to occur *simultaneously*. Items are added to stock at rate $p$ while being consumed at rate $d$, so the maximum inventory is $I_m = Q(1 - d/p) < Q$.

Since holding cost in EPQ is charged on $I_m/2 = (Q/2)(1 - d/p)$ rather than $Q/2$, the *effective* holding cost per unit is reduced by the factor $(1 - d/p)$. Differentiating the EPQ cost function yields $Q_{EPQ}^{*} = Q_{EOQ}^{*} / \sqrt{1 - d/p}$, which is strictly greater than $Q_{EOQ}^{*}$ because $\sqrt{1 - d/p} < 1$. Hence the EPQ optimal lot size is always greater than or equal to the EOQ lot size for identical $D, S, H$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a multi-echelon inventory system? Name the three principal topologies and state Roundy's power-of-two policy result.*

**Model Answer:**
A **multi-echelon inventory system** is a network of inventory-holding stages arranged in *tiers* such that the demand at each upper echelon equals the replenishment orders of the echelon immediately below it. The three principal topologies are:

1. **Serial (chain) system** — single path: plant → warehouse → retailer → customer.
2. **Distribution (divergent) system** — one source feeding many downstream stocking points.
3. **Assembly (convergent) system** — many components combining into subassemblies and then a final product.

**Roundy's Power-of-Two Result (1985):** If each stocking echelon is restricted to a reorder interval that is an *integer power of two* times a base period $T_0$ (i.e., $T_j \in \lbrace T_0, 2T_0, 4T_0, 8T_0, \ldots \rbrace$), then the resulting cost is guaranteed to be within at most **2%** of the unconstrained, jointly-optimal policy. This makes multi-echelon coordination *practically implementable* with simple periodic-review rules.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A machine shop produces a component used in its own assembly line. The production rate is $p = 800$ units/day, the demand rate is $d = 300$ units/day, and 300 working days occur in a year ($D = 90{,}000$ units/yr). The setup cost per production run is $S = ₹500$, and the holding cost is $H = ₹6$ per unit per year. Determine: (i) the optimal production lot size $Q^{*}$, (ii) the maximum inventory $I_m$, (iii) the length of the production run $t_1$, (iv) the cycle time $T^{*}$, (v) the minimum total variable cost $TC^{*}$, and (vi) the optimal number of production runs per year.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A distributor stocks two items that share a common warehouse with capacity $W = 400$ m². The data are:
| Item | $D_i$ (units/yr) | $S_i$ (₹) | $H_i$ (₹/unit/yr) | $a_i$ (m²/unit) |
|------|------------------|------------|--------------------|------------------|
| 1    | 6,000            | 200        | 3                  | 1                |
| 2    | 4,000            | 250        | 4                  | 2                |
Using the Lagrangian method, compute the unconstrained EOQs, identify whether the storage constraint is binding, and (if binding) state the structural form of the constrained optimal $Q_1^{*}$ and $Q_2^{*}$.

**Model Solution (a):**

Compute the reduction factor:
$$1 - \frac{d}{p} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625$$

(i) Optimal lot size:
$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 90{,}000 \times 500}{6 \times 0.625}} = \sqrt{\frac{90{,}000{,}000}{3.75}} = \sqrt{24{,}000{,}000} \approx 4898.98 \text{ units}$$

(ii) Maximum inventory:
$$I_m = Q^{*} \times 0.625 = 4898.98 \times 0.625 \approx 3061.86 \text{ units}$$

(iii) Production run time:
$$t_1 = \frac{Q^{*}}{p} = \frac{4898.98}{800} \approx 6.12 \text{ days}$$

(iv) Cycle time:
$$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{d} = \frac{4898.98}{300} \approx 16.33 \text{ days}$$

(v) Minimum total cost:
$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2 \times 90{,}000 \times 6 \times 500 \times 0.625} = \sqrt{337{,}500{,}000} \approx 18{,}371.17 \text{ ₹/yr}$$

(vi) Optimal number of production runs:
$$N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{90{,}000}{4898.98} \approx 18.37 \text{ runs/yr}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

Unconstrained EOQs:
$$Q_1^0 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6{,}000 \times 200}{3}} = \sqrt{800{,}000} \approx 894.43 \text{ units}$$
$$Q_2^0 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4{,}000 \times 250}{4}} = \sqrt{500{,}000} \approx 707.11 \text{ units}$$

Check storage feasibility:
$$a_1 Q_1^0 + a_2 Q_2^0 = 1(894.43) + 2(707.11) = 894.43 + 1414.22 = 2308.65 \text{ m}^2$$

Since $2308.65 \gg 400$ m², the storage constraint is **strictly binding**. The Lagrangian first-order conditions give:
$$Q_1^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6{,}000 \times 200}{3 + 2\lambda \cdot 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}400{,}000}{3 + 2\lambda}}$$
$$Q_2^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4{,}000 \times 250}{4 + 2\lambda \cdot 2}} = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}000{,}000}{4 + 4\lambda}}$$

The constraint $1 \cdot Q_1^{*} + 2 \cdot Q_2^{*} = 400$ must hold with equality. Iterating $\lambda$ (e.g., $\lambda = 250$ gives $Q_1 \approx 68.6$, $Q_2 \approx 43.3$, total $\approx 155.2$ — too low; $\lambda = 60$ gives $Q_1 \approx 130.9$, $Q_2 \approx 79.1$, total $\approx 289.1$ — too low; $\lambda = 20$ gives $Q_1 \approx 169.0$, $Q_2 \approx 98.1$, total $\approx 365.2$ — closer; $\lambda \approx 10$ gives $Q_1 \approx 186.0$, $Q_2 \approx 104.8$, total $\approx 395.6$ — nearly binding).

Thus $\lambda^{*} \approx 9$ to $10$, yielding $Q_1^{*} \approx 188$, $Q_2^{*} \approx 106$ (satisfactory exam approximation).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A two-echelon serial supply chain consists of a **central warehouse (CW)** supplying a **retailer (R)**. Demand at the retailer is deterministic at $D = 12{,}000$ units/yr. The unit holding cost at the retailer is $h_R = ₹5$/unit/yr and at the central warehouse is $h_{CW} = ₹2$/unit/yr. The setup cost at the retailer is $S_R = ₹300$ per order. Lead time from CW to R is $L = 0.1$ year. Using an echelon-stock perspective, find: (i) the retailer's optimal order quantity $Q_R^{*}$, (ii) the retailer's optimal reorder point assuming demand is uniform, and (iii) the central warehouse's optimal replenishment batch $Q_{CW}$ assuming it replenishes in *integer multiples* of $Q_R$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply Roundy's Power-of-Two policy to a distribution network with one plant and three warehouses. The base review period is $T_0 = 1$ week. The optimal continuous-time intervals are $T_1^{*} = 3.4$ weeks, $T_2^{*} = 6.9$ weeks, $T_3^{*} = 1.7$ weeks. Round each to the nearest *power-of-two* value of $T_0$ and compute the percentage cost penalty relative to the optimum, given the cost function $C(T) = 500/T + 100T$ for each warehouse.

**Model Solution (a):**

(i) Retailer's optimal order quantity (single-stage EOQ at echelon R):
$$Q_R^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S_R}{h_R}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 300}{5}} = \sqrt{1{,}440{,}000} \approx 1200 \text{ units}$$

(ii) Reorder point with constant demand and lead time $L$:
$$R_{CW} = D \cdot L = 12{,}000 \times 0.1 = 1200 \text{ units}$$

(iii) Central warehouse optimal batch (integer-multiple policy):
The CW replenishes the retailer in batches of $Q_R$, so the number of replenishments per year is $D/Q_R = 12{,}000/1200 = 10$. The CW's setup cost $S_{CW}$ is typically given; assuming a standard lead-time replenishment:
$$Q_{CW} = m \cdot Q_R$$
with $m$ chosen to balance $S_{CW}$ against $h_{CW}$:
$$m = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S_{CW}}{h_{CW}}} \bigg/ Q_R$$
If $S_{CW} = ₹1000$:
$$Q_{CW}^{\text{ideal}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 1000}{2}} = \sqrt{12{,}000{,}000} \approx 3464.1$$
$$m = \left\lfloor 3464.1 / 1200 \right\rfloor = 2$$
Hence $Q_{CW} = 2 \times 1200 = 2400$ units.

**Model Solution (b):**

Round each $T_i^{*}$ to the nearest power of two ($1, 2, 4, 8$ weeks):

| Warehouse | $T_i^{*}$ (weeks) | Power-of-Two $T_i$ | $C(T_i)$ | $C(T_i^{*})$ |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|----------|---------------|
| 1         | 3.4                | 4                  | $500/4 + 100(4) = 125 + 400 = 525$ | $500/3.4 + 100(3.4) \approx 147.06 + 340 = 487.06$ |
| 2         | 6.9                | 8                  | $500/8 + 100(8) = 62.5 + 800 = 862.5$ | $500/6.9 + 100(6.9) \approx 72.46 + 690 = 762.46$ |
| 3         | 1.7                | 2                  | $500/2 + 100(2) = 250 + 200 = 450$ | $500/1.7 + 100(1.7) \approx 294.12 + 170 = 464.12$ |

Total PoT cost: $525 + 862.5 + 450 = 1837.5$
Total optimal cost: $487.06 + 762.46 + 464.12 = 1713.64$
Penalty: $(1837.5 - 1713.64)/1713.64 \times 100\% \approx 7.21\%$

> [!NOTE]
> Warehouse 3's PoT choice of $T = 2$ weeks is *suboptimal* because the true optimum $T_3^* = 1.7$ is closer to 1 week; rounding to 2 actually *increases* cost slightly. This illustrates that the 2% guarantee holds in aggregate, not necessarily per echelon.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting $(1 - d/p)$ in EPQ:** The single most common mark-losing error. Students often write $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ (EOQ formula) in an EPQ problem and lose 3–4 marks immediately.
> 2. **Mixing time units:** $D$ must be in the same time unit as $H$. If $H = ₹$/unit/yr, then $D$ = units/yr. If $d$ and $p$ are daily, the production-rate ratio $(1 - d/p)$ is *dimensionless* — a frequent confusion point.
> 3. **Lagrangian sign error:** Writing $H_i + 2\lambda a_i$ in the denominator instead of $H_i - 2\lambda a_i$ (depending on the formulation convention used). Pick *one* convention and stick to it.
> 4. **Roundy policy mistakes:** Confusing *power-of-two multiples* with continuous intervals. The whole point of Roundy's theorem is to *restrict* choices to a discrete ladder.
> 5. **Multi-echelon echelon-stock confusion:** The echelon stock at stage $j$ *includes* the stock at stage $j+1$. This *nesting* is what distinguishes echelon-stock models from installation-stock models.
> 6. **Inadequate iteration transparency:** When solving constrained multi-item problems, KTU examiners expect *at least two iterations* of $\lambda$ with numerical values shown — not just a final answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Multi-Item Models:** When multiple SKUs share scarce resources (budget, warehouse space), independent EOQ is suboptimal. Use the **Lagrangian multiplier** $\lambda$ (shadow price) to coordinate.
- **Unconstrained optimum:** $Q_i = \sqrt{2 D_i S_i / H_i}$ per item. Check $\sum a_i Q_i \le W$. If satisfied, you are done.
- **Constrained optimum:** $Q_i = \sqrt{2 D_i S_i / (H_i + 2\lambda a_i)}$ (sign convention) and iterate $\lambda$ until the constraint binds.
- **EPQ essentials:** Requires **finite production rate $p$ with $p > d$**. Max inventory $I_m = Q(1 - d/p)$. Optimal $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/[H(1-d/p)]}$.
- **EPQ is a generalization:** As $p \to \infty$, $(1 - d/p) \to 1$ and EPQ reduces to EOQ.
- **Multi-Echelon Topologies:** Serial (chain), Distribution (divergent/fan-out), Assembly (convergent/fan-in). Each has dedicated solution approaches.
- **Clark-Scarf Serial Result:** Echelon-stock policy is optimal for serial systems with linear holding costs.
- **Roundy's 98% Power-of-Two Result:** Restricting reorder intervals to powers of two ($T_0, 2T_0, 4T_0, \ldots$) achieves within **2%** of the true optimum — the practical workhorse of industry.
- **Holding cost hierarchy:** In serial systems, $h_1 \le h_2 \le \ldots \le h_k$ is the standard convention.
- **Average inventory in EPQ:** $Q(1 - d/p)/2$, not $Q/2$ — a critical distinction from EOQ.
- **Time-unit discipline:** Always keep $D$ and $H$ in the same time unit (usually years); $d$ and $p$ in the same time unit (usually days).
- **Reorder point (ROP):** $R = d \times L$ (deterministic, constant-demand case).
- **Integer-multiple policy:** When a higher echelon must replenish in batches that are integer multiples of the lower echelon's order, take the floor to maintain feasibility.
- **Industrial relevance:** EPQ governs batch production in dairy, pharma, FMCG, steel, paper. Multi-echelon systems underpin SAP/Oracle DRP. Power-of-Two policies are used by Walmart, Amazon, and Zara for replenishment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Safety Stock – Formal KTU Definition

**Safety Stock (SS)** is the extra inventory carried over and above the expected (mean) demand during lead time, to protect against **demand variability** and **lead-time variability**. It acts as a **buffer** that absorbs fluctuations so that the desired customer service level is maintained when actual demand exceeds the forecast.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the probabilistic demand environment is treated under two classical inventory review policies:

| Review Policy | Other Name | Trigger | Order Size |
|---------------|------------|---------|------------|
| **Continuous Review (Q-System)** | R-Model, Reorder Point System | When inventory drops to ROP | Fixed quantity **Q** (EOQ-based) |
| **Periodic Review (P-System)** | Periodic Replenishment, Min-Max System | Every fixed interval **T** | Variable (Order-up-to Level **S**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is the cornerstone of Module 2. The university examiner typically tests the student's ability to **derive the safety stock formula**, **apply z-values from the standard normal table**, and **extend the logic to multi-item and multi-echelon scenarios**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are hosting a **college fest** and 5,000 students are *expected* to attend. You therefore order 5,000 lunch packets. But:

- What if 6,000 students show up? → **Stockout!**
- What if only 4,000 come? → **Wasted money!**

The uncertainty of actual attendance is similar to **probabilistic demand** in supply chains. To avoid the embarrassment of running out, you order an **extra 500 packets** as a "safety reserve". That extra quantity is exactly **Safety Stock** in the inventory world.

- **Continuous Review** is like a vigilant shopkeeper who keeps checking the shelves daily; whenever bottles drop below a fixed line (ROP), he orders a fixed carton.
- **Periodic Review** is like a supermarket manager who only checks the store every Friday and then decides how much more stock to bring in to fill the shelves to a maximum level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Probabilistic Demand Assumption (Standard KTU Treatment):**
> Demand during lead time, $X$, is assumed to follow a **Normal Distribution** with mean $\bar{X}$ (or $\bar{d} \cdot L$) and standard deviation $\sigma_L$. This assumption holds well for high-volume, low-variance items like fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG).

## 1.3 Service Level – The Driver of Safety Stock

Before computing SS, we must decide *how safe is safe?* This is the **Service Level**.

1. **Type-1 Service Level (Cycle Service Level, $\alpha$):** Probability of *no stockout* in a replenishment cycle.
   $$\alpha = P(\text{Demand during Lead Time} \le \text{ROP})$$

2. **Type-2 Service Level (Fill Rate, $\beta$):** Proportion of demand *met* from stock (not lost sales).
   $$\beta = 1 - \frac{E[\text{units short per cycle}]}{Q}$$

3. **Ready Rate (Product Availability):** Fraction of time the system is *not* out of stock.

> [!TIP]
> For a KTU 14-mark problem, the examiner usually specifies the **Type of Service Level** explicitly. Misreading it is the single most common cause of full-mark loss. Always check whether the problem says "*probability of not stocking out*" (Type 1 → use $z$ directly) or "*fraction of demand met*" (Type 2 → use the lost-sales formula with $L(z)$).
