# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"List any four design options available in a transportation network."**

**Model Answer:**

The four design options are:

1. **Direct Shipping** from supplier to retailer (or directly to consumer)
2. **Direct Shipping with Reordering Decision Deferred** at supplier (Vendor-Managed Inventory variant)
3. **All Inventory Held at Supplier**, with consumer or retailer picking up on demand
4. **All Inventory Held at Retailer**, with bulk replenishment from supplier

> [!NOTE]
> For full 3 marks, students should also briefly state **one distinguishing feature** of each option (e.g., inventory location or transportation mode).

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Distinguish between a distribution centre and a cross-dock."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Distribution Centre | Cross-Dock |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage** | Stores inventory for days/weeks | Holds goods for less than 24 hours |
| **Function** | Break-bulk, hold, ship | Consolidate and transfer only |
| **Inventory holding cost** | Significant | Near zero |
| **Value-add activities** | Picking, packing, kitting, labelling | Sorting and routing only |
| **Typical dwell time** | 7–30 days | Less than 24 hours |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**A logistics manager of an e-commerce firm is comparing two design options:**
* **Option P:** Direct ship from supplier to consumer (no warehouse)
* **Option Q:** Single central DC; supplier ships bulk to DC, DC ships to consumer

**(a)** Develop the total annual cost expression for each option, indicating all terms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given: $D = 24{,}000$ units/yr, $H = ₹120$/unit/yr, $S_P = S_Q = ₹3{,}000$, freight under P = ₹6{,}000/truck, freight under Q = ₹30{,}000 per consolidated truck with $k=5$. Compute the EOQ and total cost for each option and recommend. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

Under **Option P**, each consumer order triggers one shipment. Let $Q_P$ be the order size.

$$ TCP = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q_P} S_P}_{\text{Order cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q_P} r_P}_{\text{Transport cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q_P}{2} H}_{\text{Holding cost}} $$

$$ TCP = \frac{D}{Q_P}(S_P + r_P) + \frac{Q_P}{2} H $$

Under **Option Q**, the DC consolidates $k$ consumer orders into one inbound truck. The retailer order size is $Q_Q$ and the supplier ships $k Q_Q$ per truck.

$$ TCQ = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q_Q} S_Q}_{\text{Retailer order}} + \underbrace{\frac{D}{k Q_Q} r_Q}_{\text{Inbound transport to DC}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q_Q}{2} H}_{\text{Holding at DC}} $$

**Term-by-term meaning:**

* $D/Q$ terms = number of orders/trucks per year
* $S$ = fixed cost per order (paperwork, picking, setup)
* $r$ = freight rate per truck
* $Q/2 \cdot H$ = average inventory times holding cost per unit per year

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Option P — EOQ:**

$$ Q_P^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2D(S_P + r_P)}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24000 \times (3000 + 6000)}{120}} $$

$$ Q_P^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24000 \times 9000}{120}} = \sqrt{3{,}600{,}000} \approx 1897 \text{ units} $$

Number of orders per year:

$$ N_P = \frac{24000}{1897} \approx 12.65 $$

Optimal total cost:

$$ TCP^{*} = \sqrt{2 \times 24000 \times 120 \times 9000} = \sqrt{5.184 \times 10^{10}} \approx ₹227{,}683 $$

**Option Q — EOQ:**

$$ Q_Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2D(S_Q + r_Q/k)}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24000 \times (3000 + 30000/5)}{120}} $$

$$ Q_Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24000 \times (3000 + 6000)}{120}} = \sqrt{3{,}600{,}000} \approx 1897 \text{ units} $$

Wait — this gives the same EOQ because the effective cost per order at the supplier level is $S_Q + r_Q/k = 3000 + 6000 = 9000$, identical to $S_P + r_P$. This is the cross-over point. Let's recompute the total cost of Q carefully.

Number of retailer orders per year: $N_Q = 24000/1897 \approx 12.65$

Retailer order cost: $12.65 \times 3000 = ₹37{,}950$

Number of inbound trucks to DC: $N_Q/k = 12.65/5 = 2.53$ trucks/yr

Inbound transport cost: $2.53 \times 30000 = ₹75{,}900$

Holding cost at DC: $(1897/2) \times 120 = ₹113{,}820$

Total cost of Option Q:

$$ TCQ = 37{,}950 + 75{,}900 + 113{,}820 = ₹227{,}670 $$

**Comparison:** $TCP^{*} ≈ ₹227{,}683$ vs $TCQ ≈ ₹227{,}670$. The two options are **essentially equivalent** because the data is symmetric. In practice, Option Q would be chosen for its **better customer service** (single-warehouse dispatch is more trackable than hundreds of supplier-direct shipments) and for **risk pooling** of safety stock.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to **divide $r_Q$ by $k$** when writing the effective transport cost per order. The full truck delivers $k$ retailer orders, so the per-order share of freight is $r_Q/k$. This single error loses 2–3 marks.

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A manufacturer in Ernakulam supplies 5 retail outlets across Kerala. Annual demand at each outlet is 5,000 units. Compare the following two designs:**
* **Design X:** Direct shipment from manufacturer to each outlet (5 separate lanes)
* **Design Y:** Manufacturer → Central DC (Kochi) → 5 outlets

**(a)** Sketch the network and list the inventory location for each design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For Design X, given $H = ₹80$/unit/yr, $S = ₹1{,}500$/order, freight = ₹5{,}000/truck, compute the EOQ and total annual transport + inventory cost per outlet. Comment on whether Design Y is preferable if a DC adds ₹5 lakh/yr in fixed cost. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Design X — Network Sketch (text representation since figure is schematic):**

Manufacturer (Ernakulam) → 5 lanes → Outlet 1 (Trivandrum), Outlet 2 (Kollam), Outlet 3 (Kottayam), Outlet 4 (Thrissur), Outlet 5 (Kozhikode)

Inventory locations: at **manufacturer (raw/SFG buffer)** and at **each retail outlet (finished-goods shelf)**.

**Design Y — Network Sketch:**

Manufacturer → Central DC (Kochi) → 5 retail outlets

Inventory locations: at **manufacturer** and at the **Central DC**. Retail outlets become *trans-shipment points* with minimal inventory (cross-dock like behaviour).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Per-outlet EOQ for Design X:**

$$ Q_X^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times (1500 + 5000)}{80}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 6500}{80}} $$

$$ Q_X^{*} = \sqrt{812{,}500} \approx 901 \text{ units} $$

Number of orders per outlet per year:

$$ N_X = \frac{5000}{901} \approx 5.55 $$

Per-outlet annual cost:

$$ TC_{\text{outlet}} = \sqrt{2 \times 5000 \times 80 \times 6500} = \sqrt{5.2 \times 10^{9}} \approx ₹72{,}111 $$

Total for 5 outlets:

$$ TC_X^{total} = 5 \times 72{,}111 = ₹360{,}555 \text{ per year} $$

**Design Y — Total annual logistics cost:**

Assuming the DC consolidates shipments and uses the same EOQ framework with a smaller effective freight per outlet:

$$ Q_Y^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times (1500 + 5000/5)}{80}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 2500}{80}} = \sqrt{312{,}500} \approx 559 \text{ units} $$

Variable logistics cost per outlet:

$$ TC_{\text{outlet,Y}} = 5.55 \times 1500 + \frac{5.55}{5} \times 5000 + \frac{559}{2} \times 80 $$

$$ TC_{\text{outlet,Y}} = 8325 + 5550 + 22{,}360 = ₹36{,}235 $$

Total for 5 outlets (variable only):

$$ TC_Y^{var} = 5 \times 36{,}235 = ₹181{,}175 $$

Add DC fixed cost:

$$ TC_Y^{total} = 181{,}175 + 5{,}00{,}000 = ₹681{,}175 $$

**Comparison:** $TC_X^{total} = ₹360{,}555$ vs $TC_Y^{total} = ₹681{,}175$.

**Decision:** **Design X (Direct Ship)** is cheaper by about ₹3.2 lakh/year. The DC's fixed cost of ₹5 lakh cannot be offset by the freight savings from consolidation. Design Y would be preferred only if:
* Customer-service levels demanded **faster delivery** than Design X can offer
* Outlets were **geographically far apart** (making direct freight expensive)
* **Risk pooling of safety stock** at the DC reduced total safety stock from $5 \times SS_{\text{outlet}}$ to a smaller pooled value

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to add the **DC fixed cost** in the total cost of Design Y. The variable cost of Y is lower, but the fixed cost flips the decision. This is the *exact* trade-off the examiner is testing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Design Options**

* **Eight design options** exist, ranging from single-tier direct ship to multi-tier cross-dock.
* The choice is governed by **Total Landed Cost** = Inventory + Transport + Facility + Information cost.
* **EOQ formula** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ and optimal cost $TC^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH}$ are essential.
* **Direct Ship** (Option 1) is fast and inventory-light at the retailer but freight-heavy.
* **Central DC** (Options 5, 6) pools inventory and consolidates freight; trades higher facility cost for lower transport cost.
* **Multiple DCs** (Option 7) give better responsiveness at higher fixed cost.
* **Cross-Dock** (Option 8) eliminates storage cost but requires high inbound/outbound volume balance.
* **Vendor-Managed Inventory** (Option 2) shifts inventory to supplier; works when supplier has stable production and good information systems.
* **Risk Pooling** reduces total safety stock when demand is aggregated at fewer locations: $SS_{\text{pooled}} = z \sigma \sqrt{L} \lt n \cdot z \sigma \sqrt{L}$.
* **The optimal number of facilities** is where marginal transport savings equal marginal facility cost increment.
* Always state **assumptions** (deterministic vs. stochastic demand, single vs. multiple SKU, single vs. multiple supplier) before comparing options in the exam.
* The **cross-over analysis** (when does Option A become cheaper than Option B?) is a favourite 14-mark question.
* Remember the **distance-cost trade-off**: number of facilities $\uparrow$ → transport cost $\downarrow$ (initially) but facility cost $\uparrow$.
* **Mnemonic:** *Direct-Distributed-Different.* Direct = 1 tier; Distributed = many DCs; Different = hybrid/cross-dock.
* KTU expects a **sketch of the network** in every long answer — even a hand-drawn box-and-arrow diagram earns full design marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Advice:** When a 14-mark question asks you to "compare" two design options, structure your answer as **(i) network diagram → (ii) cost formula for each → (iii) numerical evaluation → (iv) decision with a clear recommendation**. Examiners reward *structure* as much as *correct arithmetic*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trade-offs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET524 - LOGISTICS & SUPPLY CHAIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Trade-offs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Logistics and Supply Chain Engineering**, a **Trade-off** is defined as the *deliberate, managerial balancing act* between two or more competing supply chain performance objectives, where improving one objective necessarily results in the deterioration of another. Trade-offs arise because supply chain resources (capital, time, capacity, and labor) are **finite and constrained**, forcing decision-makers to operate along a boundary of optimality rather than at a theoretical point of maximum performance for all metrics simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Trade-offs represent the conflicting relationships between logistical activities (transportation, inventory, warehousing, information, sourcing, and pricing) such that decisions made to optimize one element impact the cost or performance of another. The objective of the supply chain manager is to find the optimal *trade-off point* on the **Efficient Frontier** (also called the Production Possibility Frontier of logistics).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a road trip from Kerala to Delhi.
- You can choose the **fastest highway route** (e.g., via Mumbai) — this saves *time* but costs more in tolls and fuel consumption.
- Or, you can take the **scenic, state-road route** — this is *cheaper* but takes 3 extra days.
- You **cannot** simultaneously have the cheapest fuel cost AND the shortest travel time. The two objectives conflict.

In this analogy:
- **Time** = Service Level (responsiveness)
- **Money** = Cost
- The choice you make between them is the **Trade-off**.

Similarly, in a supply chain, a company cannot simultaneously achieve:
- **Zero inventory cost** AND **100% product availability**.
- **Cheapest transportation** AND **fastest delivery**.

Every decision in a supply chain is a **trade-off** between these competing goals.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Frameworks

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Trade-off Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Cost (C):** Total landed cost in INR/USD per unit.
> - **Service Level (SL):** Probability of meeting demand from available stock, expressed as a **percentage (0–100%)** or **fill rate**.
> - **Lead Time (LT):** Time between order placement and receipt, in **days (d)**.
> - **Inventory Turnover (IT):** Ratio of Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) to Average Inventory.
> - **Efficient Frontier:** The set of *Pareto-optimal* trade-off combinations.

The key conceptual metric in trade-off analysis is the **Efficient Frontier Function**:

$$f(\text{Cost}, \text{Service}) = \text{Constant Pareto-Optimal Set}$$

This means a point on the frontier is *efficient* because no other point can reduce cost without also reducing service (and vice versa).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Trade-offs Exist — The Underlying Logic

Trade-offs exist due to **three fundamental drivers**:

1. **Scarcity of Capital:** Money spent on faster transportation cannot be spent on additional inventory.
2. **Information Asymmetry:** Bullwhip effect creates variability that forces trade-offs between *information systems investment* and *safety stock holding*.
3. **Conflicting Customer Demands:** Customers want both *low price* and *instant delivery* — the firm must compromise.

### 2.2 The Major Supply Chain Trade-offs (KTU High-Yield List)

| **#** | **Trade-off Pair** | **What you sacrifice** | **What you gain** | **Typical Application** |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inventory vs. Transportation | Holding cost of large inventory | Lower freight cost via bulk/less-than-truckload consolidation | Long-haul distribution |
| 2 | Cost vs. Service Level | Profit margin | Higher fill rate, faster response | E-commerce vs. Discount retail |
| 3 | Lead Time vs. Cost | Money (air freight, expedite) | Time | Spare parts, perishables |
| 4 | Centralization vs. Decentralization | Economies of scale (central) | Customer proximity & responsiveness (local) | Warehouse location strategy |
| 5 | Make vs. Buy | Capital tied in production | Flexibility & focus on core competency | Outsourcing decisions |
| 6 | Push vs. Pull | Forecast accuracy risk | Lower inventory, mass customization | Production planning |
| 7 | Lot Size vs. Inventory | Setup cost / ordering cost | Lower average inventory (EOQ balance) | Manufacturing & procurement |
| 8 | Product Variety vs. Inventory | Complexity cost | Higher market share | SKU rationalization |
| 9 | Information vs. Inventory | IT infrastructure cost | Reduced safety stock via VMI/CPFR | Collaborative forecasting |
| 10 | Procurement Cost vs. Quality | Cheaper inputs | Defect reduction, warranty savings | Supplier selection |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All mathematical models in trade-off analysis use the following foundational equations:**

| **#** | **Formula Name** | **Equation** | **Variables & Units** | **Purpose** |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Logistics Cost (TLC) | $TLC = TC + IC + WC + OC$ | TC=Transport, IC=Inventory, WC=Warehousing, OC=Order cost (INR) | Aggregate trade-off objective |
| 2 | Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) | $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$ | D=Annual demand, S=Order cost, H=Holding cost/unit/yr | Lot-size vs. inventory trade-off |
| 3 | Safety Stock (SS) | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | z=Service factor, σ_LT=Std dev of lead time, L=Lead time | Inventory vs. service level |
| 4 | Customer Service Level (CSL) | $CSL = 1 - \frac{SS}{Q}$ (for normal dist.) | SS=Safety stock, Q=Reorder qty | Cost vs. service trade-off |
| 5 | Trade-off Index (TOI) | $TOI = \frac{\Delta \text{Cost}}{\Delta \text{Service Level}}$ | Marginal cost of one unit of service improvement | Marginal analysis |
| 6 | Inventory Carrying Cost | $ICC = I \cdot C_c \cdot \frac{i}{100}$ | I=Avg inventory, C_c=Unit cost, i=Carrying rate (%) | Inventory vs. cash trade-off |
| 7 | Freight Cost per Unit | $F = \frac{F_{total}}{Q}$ | F_total=Total freight, Q=Quantity shipped | Bulk vs. LTL trade-off |
| 8 | Total Cost Trade-off (TC) | $TC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q}S + \frac{Q}{2}H$ | D=Demand, S=Setup, H=Holding, Q=Order qty | Classical lot-size model |
| 9 | Service-Cost Trade-off Function | $C(SL) = C_0 + k \cdot e^{m \cdot SL}$ | C_0=Base cost, k,m=Empirical constants, SL=Service % | Convex trade-off curve |
| 10 | Efficient Frontier Slope | $\frac{dC}{dSL} = k \cdot m \cdot e^{m \cdot SL}$ | Derivative of trade-off function | Marginal cost of service |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU RULE:** Never use the vertical pipe symbol `\|` inside a table. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute values or conditional expressions, e.g., $C(Q) = \vert C_{\text{marginal}} \vert \cdot Q$. The trade-off curve $C(SL)$ is **strictly convex** (curving upward) because of *diminishing returns* — improving service from 95% to 99% costs *much* more than improving from 80% to 84%.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Airline Industry:** Airlines trade-off ticket price vs. seat availability (yield management).
- **Automotive:** Toyota's JIT system trades higher supplier coordination cost for near-zero inventory.
- **E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Trades warehousing cost for next-day delivery.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Trades inventory cost for guaranteed cold-chain integrity and zero stockouts of life-saving drugs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Classical Lot-Size vs. Inventory Trade-off (EOQ)

The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) is the foundational mathematical trade-off in supply chain engineering. It balances **ordering cost** (favors larger, fewer orders) against **holding cost** (favors smaller, more frequent orders).

**Given:**
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $S$ = Ordering cost per order (INR/order)
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (INR/unit/year)
- $Q$ = Order quantity (units)

**Total Annual Cost Function:**

$$TC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Annual ordering cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Annual holding cost}}$$

**Step 1 — Set up the optimization problem:**
We want to find the value of $Q$ that minimizes $TC(Q)$.

**Step 2 — Take the first derivative with respect to $Q$:**

$$\frac{d\,TC}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Set the derivative equal to zero (first-order optimality condition):**

$$-\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $Q$ algebraically:**

$$\frac{DS}{Q^2} = \frac{H}{2}$$

$$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

**Step 5 — Take the positive square root (since $Q > 0$):**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

**Step 6 — Verify second-order condition (minimization):**

$$\frac{d^2\,TC}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0$$

Since the second derivative is positive, $TC(Q)$ is convex, and $Q^*$ is indeed a **minimum**, not a maximum. This proves the **trade-off equilibrium**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** At the EOQ point, the marginal ordering cost exactly equals the marginal holding cost. Any deviation increases total cost. This is the **mathematical proof of trade-off necessity**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Service Level vs. Safety Stock Trade-off

**Given:**
- $L$ = Lead time (days)
- $\sigma_{LT}$ = Standard deviation of lead-time demand
- $z$ = Standard normal variate for desired service level
- $SS$ = Safety Stock (units)

**Step 1 — Define safety stock equation:**

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$$

**Step 2 — Reorder Point (ROP) is:**

$$ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$$

where $\bar{d}$ is average daily demand.

**Step 3 — Express Service Level (Type 1, Cycle Service Level) as a function of $z$:**

$$CSL(z) = P(Z \leq z) = \Phi(z)$$

where $\Phi$ is the cumulative standard normal distribution.

**Step 4 — Invert the relationship (so that $z$ becomes a function of $CSL$):**

$$z = \Phi^{-1}(CSL)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the safety stock equation:**

$$SS(CSL) = \Phi^{-1}(CSL) \cdot \sigma_{LT}$$

**Step 6 — Derive the marginal trade-off (cost of one more % of service):**

Differentiating safety stock with respect to service level:

$$\frac{d\,SS}{d\,CSL} = \frac{\sigma_{LT}}{\phi(z)}$$

where $\phi(z)$ is the standard normal probability density function.

**Step 7 — Convert safety stock to monetary trade-off:**

$$\frac{d\,\text{Cost}}{d\,CSL} = H \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \frac{1}{\phi(z)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** As $z$ increases (higher service), the density $\phi(z)$ decreases rapidly, making $\frac{d\,\text{Cost}}{d\,CSL}$ explode. This is the **mathematical reason for the convex trade-off curve** — going from 99% to 99.9% service is *exponentially* more expensive than 90% to 91%.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: The Efficient Frontier (Trade-off Curve)

The **Efficient Frontier** is the locus of all Pareto-optimal (Cost, Service) combinations.

**Step 1 — Define the trade-off constraint function:**

A point $(C, S)$ is feasible if there exists a supply chain design achieving it. The **frontier** is the lower boundary of all feasible points.

**Step 2 — Parametric form of the convex trade-off curve:**

$$C(S) = C_{\min} + \alpha \cdot e^{\beta \cdot S}$$

where $C_{\min}$ is the theoretical minimum cost at $S = 0$, and $\alpha, \beta > 0$.

**Step 3 — Differentiate to find marginal cost of service:**

$$\frac{dC}{dS} = \alpha \cdot \beta \cdot e^{\beta \cdot S}$$

**Step 4 — At any chosen point, the manager decides:**
- If $\frac{dC}{dS} <$ value customer places on service $\Rightarrow$ *Increase service*.
- If $\frac{dC}{dS} >$ value customer places on service $\Rightarrow$ *Reduce service* (lower price).

**Step 5 — The optimal point satisfies:**

$$\frac{dC}{dS} = V_{\text{customer}}$$

where $V_{\text{customer}}$ is the marginal revenue (or perceived value) gained per unit of service improvement.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Trade-off Matrix (KTU Board Exam Ready)

| **Decision Variable** | **Cost Variable** | **Service Variable** | **Mathematical Relationship** | **Marginal Slope** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Order Quantity $Q$ | $S$ (order) | Inventory availability $SS$ | $SS = \Phi^{-1}(1 - S/Q) \cdot \sigma$ | Convex in $S$ |
| Reorder Point $R$ | Carrying cost $H$ | Stockout probability $P_{so}$ | $P_{so} = 1 - \Phi\left(\frac{R - \bar{d}L}{\sigma}\right)$ | Convex in $R$ |
| Transport Mode | Freight rate $F$ | Transit time $T$ | $F = F_0 - \gamma \ln T$ | Logarithmic |
| Warehouse Count | Facility cost $W$ | Delivery lead time $L$ | $L = L_0 / \sqrt{n}$ (approx.) | Diminishing return |
| Supplier Lead Time | Premium price $P$ | Variability $\sigma_{LT}$ | $\sigma_{LT} = a + b \cdot L$ | Linear |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Efficient Frontier — Trade-off Curve Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** A Mermaid block diagram is provided below to model the **functional architecture** of trade-off analysis. It maps how inputs, decision variables, constraints, and the trade-off curve interact — a more reliable representation than attempting a physical curve drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Supply Chain Inputs] --> B[Cost Component TC]
    A --> C[Service Component SL]
    B --> D[Trade-off Function F]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Efficient Frontier?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Pareto Optimal Point]
    E -- No --> G[Infeasible Region]
    F --> H[Marginal Analysis]
    H --> I{Customer Value V > Marginal Cost}
    I -- Yes --> J[Increase Service Level]
    I -- No --> K[Reduce Cost / Service]
    D --> L[Convex Trade-off Curve]
    L --> M[Decision Maker Optimization]
    M --> N[Final Supply Chain Design]
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- **A** is the input (Demand $D$, Cost $C$, Service targets).
- **B & C** decompose inputs into the two competing objectives.
- **D** is the trade-off function $F(C, S)$.
- **E** filters feasible vs. infeasible points.
- **F** identifies Pareto-optimal solutions.
- **H–K** perform marginal cost-value analysis for the final decision.
- **L** is the convex efficient frontier curve.
- **M & N** close the loop with the chosen supply chain design.

---

### 4.2 Inventory vs. Transportation Trade-off Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Decision: How much to ship?] --> Q1[Small Frequent Shipments]
    P1 --> Q2[Large Bulk Shipments]
    Q1 --> R1[High Transportation Cost]
    Q1 --> R2[Low Inventory Carrying Cost]
    Q2 --> R3[Low Transportation Cost]
    Q2 --> R4[High Inventory Carrying Cost]
    R1 --> S1[Total Logistics Cost = High]
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S2[Total Logistics Cost = High]
    R4 --> S2
    S1 --> X1[Sub-optimal Region]
    S2 --> X2[Sub-optimal Region]
    X1 --> Opt[Optimal Trade-off Point Q-star]
    X2 --> Opt
```

---

### 4.3 Push vs. Pull Trade-off Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FC[Forecast Demand] --> Push{Push System}
    Push --> P_Inv[Build Inventory in Advance]
    P_Inv --> P_Out[Ship to Warehouse]
    P_Out --> P_Cust[Customer]
    
    AC[Actual Customer Order] --> Pull{Pull System}
    Pull --> PL_Trig[Order Triggers Production]
    PL_Trig --> PL_Prod[Produce on Demand]
    PL_Prod --> PL_Cust[Direct Ship to Customer]
    
    Push --> Trade[Push vs Pull Trade-off]
    Pull --> Trade
    Trade --> T1[Push: High inventory, Low coordination]
    Trade --> T2[Pull: Low inventory, High coordination cost]
```

---

### 4.4 Centralization vs. Decentralization Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Warehouse Location Decision] --> Central{Centralized Hub?}
    Central -- Yes --> Ch1[Low facility cost]
    Central -- Yes --> Ch2[Longer customer lead time]
    Central -- Yes --> Ch3[High transportation cost]
    Central -- No --> De[Decentralized Network]
    De --> Dh1[High facility cost]
    De --> Dh2[Short lead time]
    De --> Dh3[Lower transportation cost]
    Ch1 --> Tot[Total Cost Trade-off]
    Ch2 --> Tot
    Ch3 --> Tot
    Dh1 --> Tot
    Dh2 --> Tot
    Dh3 --> Tot
    Tot --> OptSel[Optimal Number of Warehouses]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define a *trade-off* in the context of supply chain management. Give one example of a trade-off between inventory and transportation cost.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A trade-off in supply chain management is a deliberate managerial decision in which improving one performance objective (e.g., service level) necessitates sacrificing another (e.g., cost). It arises because supply chain resources are finite.

**Example (1 Mark):** If a firm chooses to ship in **full truckload (FTL)** to reduce transportation cost per unit, it must hold **larger inventory** at the destination, increasing inventory carrying cost. Conversely, shipping in **less-than-truckload (LTL)** smaller, more frequent batches reduces inventory at destination but raises per-unit transportation cost. This is the classic **inventory vs. transportation trade-off**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
What is meant by the *Efficient Frontier* in supply chain trade-off analysis?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Efficient Frontier** (also called the Pareto-optimal frontier) is the locus of all feasible (Cost, Service Level) combinations at which it is impossible to improve one dimension without worsening the other. Mathematically, a point $(C^*, S^*)$ lies on the frontier if no other feasible point has $C \leq C^*$ and $S \geq S^*$ with at least one strict inequality. The frontier is typically **convex** because of the *diminishing marginal returns* of service improvement — going from 95% to 99% service is disproportionately expensive. Firms should always operate on (not inside) this frontier.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 3 Model]*

**Q. (a)** Explain the major types of trade-offs encountered in supply chain management. Discuss the *Inventory vs. Transportation* and *Cost vs. Service Level* trade-offs in detail with suitable diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction to Supply Chain Trade-offs (1 Mark):**
A trade-off is a decision-making situation where improving one supply chain objective requires sacrificing another due to resource constraints. Chopra and Meindl classify six major trade-offs in their framework: (i) inventory–transportation, (ii) inventory–facility, (iii) inventory–information, (iv) transportation–information, (v) facility–information, and (vi) facility–transportation.

**2. Inventory vs. Transportation Trade-off (3 Marks):**

This is the most classical trade-off in distribution network design.

- **Mechanism:** Transportation cost per unit *decreases* with shipment size (economies of scale in freight), but larger shipments force the receiver to hold more inventory, increasing carrying cost.
- **Mathematical Expression:** The total relevant cost is:
$$TC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q}F + \frac{Q}{2}H$$
where $Q$ is shipment size, $D$ is annual demand, $F$ is freight cost per shipment, and $H$ is annual holding cost per unit.
- **Optimization:** The optimal shipment size that balances freight and inventory is $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DF}{H}}$.
- **Real-world example:** A company shipping 12 times a year pays more freight but carries half the safety stock compared to shipping once a year.

**3. Cost vs. Service Level Trade-off (3 Marks):**

- **Mechanism:** Service level (measured as fill rate or cycle service level) can be increased by holding more safety stock, using faster (and costlier) transportation modes, or investing in redundant capacity. All these raise cost.
- **Convex Function:** $C(S) = C_0 + \alpha e^{\beta S}$, where $S$ is service level. The function is convex because improving service from 98% to 99% requires disproportionately more safety stock (since $\phi(z)$ in the normal distribution tail becomes very small).
- **Customer Value Trade-off:** A firm should improve service only as long as the marginal revenue from increased service exceeds the marginal cost.

**4. Diagrammatic Representation (refer to Section 4.2 of these notes) (Bonus understanding):**
- The efficient frontier curves upward from low-cost/low-service (bottom-left) to high-cost/high-service (top-right). The firm chooses a point where customer willingness to pay equals marginal cost of service.

---

**Q. (b)** A retailer sells an item with annual demand $D = 12{,}000$ units. The ordering cost is $S = \text{INR } 500$ per order, and the holding cost is $H = \text{INR } 20$ per unit per year. The current order quantity is $Q = 800$ units. The retailer is considering switching to a new supplier who offers a lower ordering cost of $S_{\text{new}} = \text{INR } 300$ per order, but the lead time variability increases, raising the safety stock by 200 units (each unit held costs INR 20/year).

**Compute:**
(i) The current total cost (ordering + holding) and the proposed total cost. **(3 Marks)**
(ii) The change in cost from the trade-off, and recommend whether the switch should be made. **(4 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Current Total Cost (TC_old):**

**Step 1 — Compute annual ordering cost:**

$$\text{Ordering Cost}_{\text{old}} = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S = \frac{12000}{800} \cdot 500 = 15 \cdot 500 = \text{INR } 7500$$

**Step 2 — Compute annual holding cost** (using average inventory $Q/2$):

$$\text{Holding Cost}_{\text{old}} = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H = \frac{800}{2} \cdot 20 = 400 \cdot 20 = \text{INR } 8000$$

**Step 3 — Total Current Cost:**

$$TC_{\text{old}} = 7500 + 8000 = \text{INR } 15{,}500$$

---

**Proposed Total Cost (TC_new):**

**Step 4 — New EOQ with reduced ordering cost:**

$$Q_{\text{new}}^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS_{\text{new}}}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 12000 \cdot 300}{20}} = \sqrt{360000} = 600 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — New annual ordering cost:**

$$\text{Ordering Cost}_{\text{new}} = \frac{D}{Q_{\text{new}}^*} \cdot S_{\text{new}} = \frac{12000}{600} \cdot 300 = 20 \cdot 300 = \text{INR } 6000$$

**Step 6 — New annual holding cost (cycle stock + safety stock):**

$$\text{Holding Cost}_{\text{new}} = \left(\frac{Q_{\text{new}}^*}{2} + SS_{\text{new}}\right) \cdot H = \left(\frac{600}{2} + 200\right) \cdot 20 = (300 + 200) \cdot 20 = 500 \cdot 20 = \text{INR } 10{,}000$$

**Step 7 — Total Proposed Cost:**

$$TC_{\text{new}} = 6000 + 10000 = \text{INR } 16{,}000$$

---

**(ii) Cost Change & Recommendation (4 Marks):**

**Step 8 — Net change in cost:**

$$\Delta TC = TC_{\text{new}} - TC_{\text{old}} = 16000 - 15500 = +\text{INR } 500$$

**Step 9 — Interpretation:**
- Ordering cost **saved**: $7500 - 6000 = \text{INR } 1500$.
- Holding cost **increased**: $10000 - 8000 = \text{INR } 2000$.
- **Net loss** = INR 500 per year.

**Step 10 — Recommendation:**
> **Do NOT switch to the new supplier.** Although the ordering cost decreases by INR 1500, the increased lead-time variability forces an additional safety stock of 200 units, raising annual holding cost by INR 2000. The trade-off results in a net cost increase of INR 500/year. The retailer should either negotiate lower lead-time variability from the new supplier or remain with the current one. If the new supplier can guarantee a safety-stock increase of less than 75 units (i.e., $\Delta \text{Holding Cost} < \text{INR } 1500$), the switch becomes beneficial — this is the **break-even trade-off point**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 3 Model — Internal Choice]*

**Q. (a)** With the help of a clear diagram, explain the concept of the *Efficient Frontier* (Trade-off Curve) in supply chain decision-making. How does a manager decide the optimal point on this frontier? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of Efficient Frontier (2 Marks):**

The Efficient Frontier is the set of all (Cost, Service) combinations at which the supply chain is *Pareto-optimal* — meaning no other feasible design can lower cost without also lowering service (and vice versa). It is the upper-right boundary of the feasible region in a two-dimensional cost-vs-service plot.

**2. Diagram Description (3 Marks):**

Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. The frontier is a smooth, **convex curve** rising from bottom-left (low cost, low service) to top-right (high cost, high service). Points *inside* the curve are feasible but inefficient (wasteful). Points *outside* the curve are infeasible. The curve is convex (curving upward) because of *diminishing returns* in service improvement — each additional unit of service becomes more expensive.

**3. Managerial Decision Rule (2 Marks):**

The manager identifies the optimal point by equating **marginal cost of service** with **marginal revenue (or customer value) of service**:

$$\frac{dC}{dS}\bigg|_{S=S^*} = V_{\text{customer}}$$

- If marginal cost of service > customer value $\Rightarrow$ move *down-left* (cut cost, accept lower service).
- If marginal cost of service < customer value $\Rightarrow$ move *up-right* (raise service, accept higher cost).
- The **optimum** is the unique point where these two are equal.

**4. KTU Real-world Examples (Bonus):**
- **Amazon Prime:** Operates on the *high-service, high-cost* end of the frontier, justified by customer willingness to pay subscription fees.
- **Walmart:** Operates on the *low-cost, lower-service* end, capturing price-sensitive customers.
- **Apple:** Sits high on the frontier with premium pricing that justifies high service levels.

---

**Q. (b)** A firm has a current service level of 90% with safety stock of 100 units. The standard deviation of lead-time demand is 40 units. The firm wants to evaluate two service-level improvement options:

- **Option 1:** Increase service level to 95% by adding safety stock.
- **Option 2:** Reduce lead-time variability by investing INR 50,000 in supplier collaboration, which reduces $\sigma_{LT}$ to 25 units, and target a service level of 95%.

**Given:**
- Holding cost $H = \text{INR } 50$ per unit per year.
- $z_{0.90} = 1.28$, $z_{0.95} = 1.65$.

**Compute the trade-off and recommend the optimal option. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify the current safety stock (sanity check):**

$$SS_{\text{current}} = z \cdot \sigma = 1.28 \cdot 40 = 51.2 \text{ units}$$

Wait — the problem states the current safety stock is 100 units (possibly including other factors); we will use the given values directly.

**Step 2 — Option 1 Cost: Increase service level to 95% with current $\sigma$:**

New safety stock:

$$SS_1 = z_{0.95} \cdot \sigma = 1.65 \cdot 40 = 66 \text{ units}$$

Increase in safety stock:

$$\Delta SS_1 = 66 - 51.2 = 14.8 \text{ units (or use given 100 as baseline)}$$

*Using given baseline of 100 units:*

$$\Delta SS_1 = 100 \cdot \frac{1.65}{1.28} - 100 = 128.9 - 100 = 28.9 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Annual holding cost increase (Option 1):**

$$\Delta C_1 = 28.9 \cdot 50 = \text{INR } 1445 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 4 — Option 2 Cost: Invest in collaboration, $\sigma = 25$, target 95%:**

New safety stock:

$$SS_2 = 1.65 \cdot 25 = 41.25 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — Change in safety stock (Option 2):**

$$\Delta SS_2 = 41.25 - 51.2 = -9.95 \text{ units (i.e., safety stock decreases)}$$

**Step 6 — Annual holding cost change (Option 2):**

$$\Delta C_2 = (-9.95) \cdot 50 = -\text{INR } 497.5 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 7 — Net cost of Option 2 (including investment amortized over 1 year):**

$$C_2^{\text{net}} = 50000 - 497.5 = \text{INR } 49{,}502.5 \text{ (Year 1)}$$

But in subsequent years, only the holding cost saving of INR 497.5/year applies.

**Step 8 — Trade-off Analysis & Recommendation (4 Marks):**

| **Criterion** | **Option 1** | **Option 2 (Year 1)** | **Option 2 (Year 2+)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Investment | 0 | INR 50,000 | 0 |
| Annual Holding Cost Change | +INR 1,445 | −INR 497.5 | −INR 497.5 |
| Net Cost Impact | +INR 1,445 | +INR 49,502.5 | −INR 497.5 |
| Payback Period | N/A | ≈ 100 years (at current rate) | — |

**Recommendation:**
> **Choose Option 1 (increase safety stock)** for short-term and likely long-term planning. Option 2's INR 50,000 investment yields only INR 497.5/year in holding cost savings, an absurdly long payback period. The collaboration investment is justified **only if** the firm can leverage the lower variability to *also* reduce safety stock by much more (e.g., by 1,000+ units) or improve multiple product lines simultaneously. Otherwise, the trade-off clearly favors adding safety stock directly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to include the **safety stock holding cost** when comparing EOQ scenarios. Many students compute only cycle stock cost and miss the variable cost change.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using $z = 1.96$ (for 97.5%) when 95% is asked. *Always re-read the problem statement carefully.*
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **Type 1 service level** (cycle service level, no stockout probability) with **Type 2** (fill rate, fraction of demand met from stock). KTU questions may specify which type; default assumption in module-3 trade-off analysis is **Type 1**.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting that the **Efficient Frontier is convex**, not linear. Drawing a straight line will lose 1–2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Not stating the **managerial decision rule** (marginal cost = marginal customer value) in the final answer. KTU examiners specifically look for the *optimization principle*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Trade-offs**

- [x] **Trade-off Definition:** Balancing competing objectives when resources are limited.
- [x] **Efficient Frontier:** Convex Pareto-optimal curve in (Cost, Service) space; always operate ON the frontier, never INSIDE.
- [x] **Six Major Chopra-Meindl Trade-offs:** Inventory–Transportation, Inventory–Facility, Inventory–Information, Transportation–Information, Facility–Information, Facility–Transportation.
- [x] **EOQ Formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — classical lot-size vs. inventory trade-off optimum.
- [x] **Safety Stock:** $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ — direct cost of higher service level.
- [x] **Total Logistics Cost:** $TLC = TC + IC + WC + OC$ — aggregate trade-off metric.
- [x] **Convexity Principle:** Trade-off curves are convex due to *diminishing marginal returns*.
- [x] **Optimality Rule:** Operate at the point where $\frac{dC}{dS} = V_{\text{customer}}$.
- [x] **Push vs. Pull:** Push reduces coordination cost but increases inventory risk; Pull does the opposite.
- [x] **Centralization vs. Decentralization:** Centralize for scale economies, decentralize for responsiveness.
- [x] **Make vs. Buy:** Trade capital tie-up (make) for flexibility (buy).
- [x] **KTU Formula Golden List:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$; $SS = z\sigma_{LT}$; $CSL = 1 - SS/Q$; $TC = (D/Q)S + (Q/2)H$.
- [x] **Key Insight:** Service improvement from 99% → 99.9% is **exponentially** more expensive than 90% → 91% (because $\phi(z) \to 0$ in the tail).
- [x] **Units Rule:** $D$ in units/year, $S$ in INR/order, $H$ in INR/unit/year, $Q$ in units.
- [x] **Examination Tip:** Always draw a labeled Efficient Frontier diagram in long-answer questions — it earns 1–2 free marks and demonstrates conceptual clarity.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Supply Chain Risk Management (SCRM)** is the systematic and coordinated application of policies, procedures, operational techniques, and managerial strategies to identify, assess, mitigate, and monitor risks along the entire supply chain, with the objective of reducing vulnerability, ensuring continuity, and protecting value-creation activities from internal and external disruptions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Risk in a supply chain is any uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has a positive or negative effect on one or more supply chain objectives (cost, quality, time, sustainability). *Negative* risks are *threats*; *positive* risks are *opportunities*.

**Risk** itself is formally defined as the product of three dimensions:

$$R = f(P, I, C)$$

Where:
- **P** = Probability of occurrence of the event
- **I** = Impact (severity of consequence)
- **C** = Capability to detect/prevent the event before damage occurs

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Kerala Monsoon Shipment Analogy

Imagine you are a cardamom trader in **Idukki, Kerala** who must ship 5 tonnes of premium cardamom to a buyer in **Hamburg, Germany** every quarter.

- The **probability (P)** of a monsoon delay hitting the Cochin Port route is high (say, 60% during June–August).
- The **impact (I)** is huge — a 2-week delay ruins the Christmas spice premium and triggers penalty clauses worth ₹15 lakhs.
- Your **detection capability (C)** is low — you cannot easily predict a cyclone 30 days out, so you learn about disruptions *after* they occur.

A *risk-aware* trader does **not** simply pray for good weather. She:
1. **Spreads shipments** across two ports (Cochin + Tuticorin).
2. **Buffers inventory** in a bonded warehouse in Dubai.
3. **Buys marine cargo insurance** to transfer the financial impact.
4. **Monitors** a real-time dashboard of weather + port congestion.

That is Supply Chain Risk Management in action — a discipline, not a feeling.

## 1.3 Two Fundamental Categories of Supply Chain Risk

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 must-know dichotomy — every question is anchored here:**

| Category | Description | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Disruption Risks** | Catastrophic, low-probability, high-impact events that interrupt the normal flow | Earthquakes, tsunamis, pandemics, port strikes, supplier bankruptcy, cyber-attacks, geopolitical conflict |
| **Operational Risks** | Day-to-day uncertainties in routine processes | Demand variability, yield problems, lead-time variance, quality defects, forecast error, minor machine downtime |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Trick:** If a question says *"Sudden tsunami hitting the Chennai automotive cluster"* → Disruption Risk. If it says *"A 4% demand forecast error"* → Operational Risk.

## 1.4 The Four Strategic Risk Management Responses

KTU expects you to memorise these four strategy labels verbatim:

1. **Avoid** — Exit the risky activity entirely (e.g., stop sourcing from a politically unstable region).
2. **Mitigate** — Reduce probability or impact (e.g., add a backup supplier).
3. **Transfer** — Shift the financial burden to a third party (e.g., cargo insurance, hedging).
4. **Accept** — Consciously retain the risk when its cost of mitigation exceeds its expected loss (e.g., absorb minor forecast errors from a strategic low-cost supplier).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Risk Management Process (SCRM Cycle)

A KTU-favoured framework. The process is **cyclic, not linear** — outcomes feed back into identification.

### Stage 1 — Risk Identification
- Brainstorm *what could go wrong* across the SCOR model (Plan → Source → Make → Deliver → Return).
- Tools: **FMEA** (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis), **Fault Tree Analysis**, **SWOT**, **Scenario Planning**, **Delphi Technique**.
- Map risks to a **Risk Register** (a structured database listing each risk, its category, owner, and status).

### Stage 2 — Risk Assessment (the heart of the topic)
Two sub-tasks:
- **Qualitative Assessment** — Score risks on a High/Medium/Low matrix.
- **Quantitative Assessment** — Compute the **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** or use **Monte Carlo Simulation**.

### Stage 3 — Risk Treatment / Response Selection
Choose from the 4 As above (Avoid, Mitigate, Transfer, Accept). Always pick the lowest *residual risk* option that meets cost-benefit criteria.

### Stage 4 — Risk Monitoring & Control
- Set up **KPIs** (Key Risk Indicators — KRIs) such as supplier on-time-in-full (OTIF), inventory days-of-cover, port dwell time.
- Trigger **contingency plans** when KRIs breach thresholds.

### Stage 5 — Communication & Documentation
- Report to stakeholders via **risk dashboards**.
- Update the risk register after every disruption (lessons-learned loop).

## 2.2 The Risk Assessment Matrix (Probability × Impact)

This is the **#1 high-yield visual** for KTU 2024 ESE. The matrix below uses a 5×5 grid, the most commonly asked in exams.

| Probability ↓ \ Impact → | 1 (Negligible) | 2 (Minor) | 3 (Moderate) | 4 (Major) | 5 (Catastrophic) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **5 (Almost Certain)** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | **25** |
| **4 (Likely)** | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
| **3 (Possible)** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 |
| **2 (Unlikely)** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| **1 (Rare)** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

> **Interpretation bands:** 1–4 = **Low (Green)**, 5–12 = **Medium (Yellow)**, 13–25 = **High (Red)**.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise these 6 formulas — they appear in nearly every ESE Part B question on this module.**

### 2.3.1 Expected Monetary Value (EMV)

$$EMV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times L_i$$

Where:
- $P_i$ = Probability of outcome $i$
- $L_i$ = Monetary loss (or gain) of outcome $i$
- $n$ = number of possible outcomes
- **Units:** Currency (₹, $, €, etc.)

**Decision rule:** Choose the alternative with the **lowest negative EMV** (i.e., the least expected loss) or the **highest positive EMV** (i.e., the highest expected gain).

### 2.3.2 Risk Score (from FMEA)

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity of the failure (1–10 scale)
- $O$ = Occurrence probability (1–10 scale)
- $D$ = Detection difficulty (1–10 scale)
- **Unitless, dimensionless**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** RPN is not absolute — companies set their own threshold (e.g., RPN > 100 must be mitigated). Be ready to discuss *prioritisation by RPN ranking*.

### 2.3.3 Expected Loss (Risk Exposure)

$$EL = P \times I$$

Where:
- $P$ = Annual probability of the risk event
- $I$ = Financial impact in ₹ per occurrence
- **Units:** ₹ per year

### 2.3.4 Value-at-Risk (VaR) for Supply Chain Disruptions

$$VaR_{\alpha} = \mu + z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = Mean supply chain cost
- $\sigma$ = Standard deviation of supply chain cost
- $z_{\alpha}$ = Standard normal deviate at confidence level $\alpha$ (e.g., $z_{0.95} = 1.645$)
- **Units:** Currency

### 2.3.5 Safety Stock for Risk Mitigation (the SCOR way)

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LTD}$$

Where:
- $z$ = Service-level z-score
- $\sigma_{LTD}$ = Standard deviation of lead-time demand
- **Units:** Units of inventory

### 2.3.6 Risk-Adjusted Total Cost

$$TC_{risk} = TC_{direct} + EMV_{disruption} + C_{mitigation}$$

Where:
- $TC_{direct}$ = Standard logistics + procurement cost
- $EMV_{disruption}$ = Expected cost of unmanaged risks
- $C_{mitigation}$ = Cost of mitigation actions (insurance, dual sourcing, safety stock)

## 2.4 Mitigation Levers in Real Engineering Practice

| Lever | What It Does | KTU Recognised Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Multiple Sourcing** | Reduces dependency on one supplier | Mitigate |
| **Dual Sourcing** | Two qualified suppliers for the same part | Mitigate |
| **Safety Stock** | Buffer against demand & lead-time variability | Mitigate |
| **Postponement** | Delay final customisation until demand is known | Mitigate (operational) |
| **Cargo Insurance** | Shifts financial loss to insurer | Transfer |
| **Hedging (currency, fuel)** | Locks in price against market volatility | Transfer |
| **Reshoring/Nearshoring** | Brings supply closer to demand | Mitigate (disruption) |
| **Digital Twins** | Simulate disruptions in a virtual replica | Mitigate (proactive) |
| **Supplier Audits** | Early detection of supplier distress | Mitigate (via better C) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — EMV Decision Between Two Sourcing Strategies

This is the most frequently-asked **14-mark** problem in KTU exams. I will solve it from absolute first principles.

### Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Model Question)

> A Kerala-based spice exporter can either:
> - **Strategy A:** Source cardamom from a single supplier in Wayanad (Single Sourcing).
> - **Strategy B:** Source from two suppliers — one in Wayanad and one in Coorg, Karnataka (Dual Sourcing).
>
> Annual revenue depends on whether a *supply disruption* occurs:
> - Strategy A: P(disruption) = 0.20 → Loss = ₹50,00,000; No disruption → Profit = ₹1,00,00,000
> - Strategy B: P(disruption) = 0.05 → Loss = ₹30,00,000; No disruption → Profit = ₹90,00,000
>
> Strategy B has an extra annual cost of ₹8,00,000 for managing the second supplier.
>
> **Determine which strategy is preferred on an EMV basis.**

### Step 1 — State the EMV Formula

$$EMV = \sum_{i} P_i \times L_i$$

### Step 2 — Compute EMV for Strategy A (Single Sourcing)

Outcomes:
- Disruption: $P_1 = 0.20$, $L_1 = -₹50,00,000$
- No Disruption: $P_2 = 0.80$, $L_2 = +₹1,00,00,000$

Calculation:

$$EMV_A = (0.20 \times -50,00,000) + (0.80 \times 1,00,00,000)$$

$$EMV_A = -10,00,000 + 80,00,000$$

$$\boxed{EMV_A = ₹70,00,000}$$

### Step 3 — Compute EMV for Strategy B (Dual Sourcing)

Outcomes (before adding extra cost):
- Disruption: $P_1 = 0.05$, $L_1 = -₹30,00,000$
- No Disruption: $P_2 = 0.95$, $L_2 = +₹90,00,000$

Calculation:

$$EMV_B^{gross} = (0.05 \times -30,00,000) + (0.95 \times 90,00,000)$$

$$EMV_B^{gross} = -1,50,000 + 85,50,000$$

$$EMV_B^{gross} = ₹84,00,000$$

### Step 4 — Subtract the Extra Annual Cost of Dual Sourcing

$$EMV_B^{net} = 84,00,000 - 8,00,000$$

$$\boxed{EMV_B = ₹76,00,000}$$

### Step 5 — Compare and Recommend

$$EMV_B (₹76,00,000) > EMV_A (₹70,00,000)$$

**Recommendation:** Adopt **Strategy B (Dual Sourcing)** because its expected value is ₹6,00,000 higher.

### Step 6 — Compute the Variance of Each Strategy (Risk Magnitude)

A complete KTU answer also shows that EMV alone is insufficient — you must check **risk spread** (variance/standard deviation).

For Strategy A:

$$\sigma_A^2 = (0.20) \times (-50,00,000 - 70,00,000)^2 + (0.80) \times (1,00,00,000 - 70,00,000)^2$$

$$\sigma_A^2 = (0.20) \times (-1,20,00,000)^2 + (0.80) \times (30,00,000)^2$$

$$\sigma_A^2 = (0.20) \times 1,44,00,00,00,00,000 + (0.80) \times 9,00,00,00,00,000$$

$$\sigma_A^2 = 28,80,00,00,00,000 + 7,20,00,00,00,000 = 36,00,00,00,00,000$$

$$\sigma_A = ₹60,00,000$$

For Strategy B (using the net EMV of ₹76,00,000):

$$\sigma_B^2 = (0.05) \times (-30,00,000 - 76,00,000)^2 + (0.95) \times (90,00,000 - 76,00,000)^2$$

$$\sigma_B^2 = (0.05) \times (-1,06,00,000)^2 + (0.95) \times (14,00,000)^2$$

$$\sigma_B^2 = (0.05) \times 1,12,36,00,00,00,000 + (0.95) \times 1,96,00,00,00,000$$

$$\sigma_B^2 = 5,61,80,00,00,000 + 1,86,20,00,00,000 = 7,48,00,00,00,000$$

$$\sigma_B = ₹27,34,96,000 \approx ₹27,35,000$$

### Step 7 — Final Interpretation

| Metric | Strategy A | Strategy B |
|---|---|---|
| Expected Value (EMV) | ₹70,00,000 | **₹76,00,000** |
| Standard Deviation (σ) | ₹60,00,000 | **₹27,35,000** |

**Conclusion:** Strategy B is superior on **both** criteria — higher expected profit **and** lower risk. This is the textbook "dominant alternative."

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Apply Each Mitigation Lever

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix has come up in KTU exams as a 7-mark part (a) question. Memorise the *trigger condition* for each lever.

| Trigger Condition Observed | Recommended Lever | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Single-source critical component from politically unstable region | Reshoring or **multi-sourcing** | Eliminates dependency on a high-risk geography |
| Demand variability coefficient of variation (CV) > 0.30 | **Safety stock** build-up | Buffers against forecast error |
| Lead-time standard deviation > 30% of mean lead time | **Dual sourcing** + expedited options | Reduces dependency on a single slow lane |
| Currency depreciation risk on imported raw material | **Forward contract / Hedging** | Locks in FX rate (Transfer strategy) |
| Port congestion > 48 hours average dwell | **Multi-port routing** | Avoids single-port failure |
| Sensitive product (e.g., pharma, seafood) with high spoilage | **Cold chain insurance** + shorter routes | Transfer + Mitigate |
| High-impact, low-probability catastrophic event | **Business continuity plan + insurance** | Risk transfer for tail risks |
| Supplier financial distress signs (low D/E ratio, late filings) | **Pre-qualify a backup supplier** | Ensures continuity of supply |
| Quality defects exceeding 2% AQL | **Supplier development + Incoming inspection** | Lowers Occurrence (O) in FMEA |

> A risk register is a living document. The KTU board often asks: *"Prepare a risk register for an electronics supply chain."*

| Risk ID | Risk Description | Category | P (1-5) | I (1-5) | Score | Strategy | Owner | KRI |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R-01 | Sole-source chip from Taiwan | Disruption | 3 | 5 | 15 | Mitigate (dual-source) | Procurement Head | Alt-supplier audit score |
| R-02 | FX volatility on USD imports | Operational | 4 | 3 | 12 | Transfer (hedge) | Treasury | 90-day FX rate |
| R-03 | Container ship delay at Kochi | Operational | 4 | 2 | 8 | Accept | Logistics Mgr | Port dwell time |
| R-04 | Factory fire at Tier-2 supplier | Disruption | 2 | 5 | 10 | Transfer (insurance) | Risk Officer | Fire audit status |
| R-05 | Cyber-attack on WMS | Disruption | 2 | 5 | 10 | Mitigate (redundancy) | IT Head | Penetration-test score |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five-Stage SCRM Cycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Risk Identification] --> B[Stage 2: Risk Assessment Qualitative and Quantitative]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Risk Treatment Select Avoid Mitigate Transfer Accept]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Risk Monitoring KPIs and KRIs Dashboards]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Communication and Documentation Risk Register Updates]
    E --> A
    F[Risk Register] -.feeds into.-> A
    G[Contingency Plan] -.triggered by.-> D
    H[Lessons Learned] -.feeds back.-> A
```

**How to read it:**
- Solid arrows show the **forward flow** of the SCRM cycle.
- Dotted arrows show the **feedback loops** — the heart of continuous improvement.
- A triggered *Contingency Plan* emerges from the Monitoring stage.
- *Lessons Learned* after a disruption is fed back into Identification.

## 4.2 Risk Treatment Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify Risk Event] --> Q1{Is the activity critical to survival}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Can we eliminate the risk source}
    Q1 -- No --> Avoid[Strategy: ACCEPT]
    Q2 -- Yes --> AvoidIt[Strategy: AVOID Exit activity]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Can we reduce P or I cost effectively}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Mit[Strategy: MITIGATE Dual source safety stock process redesign]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Can a third party absorb the loss}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Trans[Strategy: TRANSFER Insurance hedge contract]
    Q4 -- No --> Absorb[Strategy: ACCEPT with monitoring]
```

**How to read it:** Start at the top. Each diamond is a yes/no decision a risk manager makes. The leaves are the four recognised response strategies.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SCRM System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        E1[Internal Data: ERP WMS TMS]
        E2[External Data: News Feeds Weather Port APIs]
        E3[Historical Disruption Database]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE
        F1[Risk Identification Module]
        F2[Risk Assessment Module FMEA and EMV]
        F3[Treatment Optimiser]
        F4[Monitoring and Alert Engine]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS
        G1[Risk Register]
        G2[Mitigation Plan]
        G3[Real Time KRI Dashboard]
        G4[Executive Risk Report]
    end

    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> G2
    F2 --> G1
    F4 --> G3
    F4 --> G4
    G2 --> F4
```

**How to read it:** This is the **enterprise architecture** of a digital SCRM platform. Inputs feed an engine that produces four outputs. The arrow from `Mitigation Plan` back to `Monitoring and Alert Engine` closes the loop — once you take action, you need to monitor whether the action worked.

## 4.4 Risk Bow-Tie Diagram (Cause → Event → Consequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Threats: Natural Political Cyber Supplier Failure] -->|via| B[Top Event: Supply Disruption]
    B -->|leads to| C[Consequences: Lost Sales Penalties Brand Damage]
    D[Preventive Controls: Dual Sourcing Audits BCP] -.prevent.-> B
    E[Mitigative Controls: Safety Stock Insurance] -.reduce.-> C
```

**How to read it:** The **left side** of the bow-tie is *prevention* (reduce P), the **right side** is *mitigation after the event* (reduce I). The **centre** is the risk event itself. KTU examiners love this diagram because it cleanly separates *before* and *after* controls.
