# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Supply Chain Risk Management. List any four categories of supply chain risks with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Supply Chain Risk Management (SCRM)** is the coordinated set of strategies and processes used to identify, assess, mitigate, and monitor risks across the supply chain, ensuring continuity of material, information, and financial flows.
>
> **Four categories of SC risk:**
> 1. **Disruption Risks** — e.g., earthquake shutting down a Tier-1 supplier.
> 2. **Operational Risks** — e.g., forecast error causing stockouts.
> 3. **Financial Risks** — e.g., currency volatility on imported parts.
> 4. **Geopolitical & Regulatory Risks** — e.g., a sudden export ban on rare earths.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of Expected Monetary Value (EMV). How is it used in supply chain risk decisions?**

**Model Answer:**

> **EMV** is the probability-weighted sum of all possible monetary outcomes of a decision alternative. It is calculated as $EMV = \sum P_i \times L_i$.
>
> **Use in SC risk decisions:** When choosing between sourcing strategies, the manager computes the EMV of each. The alternative with the **least negative** (or most positive) EMV is preferred. EMV is also combined with standard deviation to evaluate *risk-adjusted* return — a strategy with slightly lower EMV but much lower variance may still be chosen by a risk-averse manager.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice Format

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, the student must answer **one full question from a pair (A or B)**, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both questions are below — students choose one.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **five stages of the Supply Chain Risk Management (SCRM) process**. Mention at least two tools used in each stage.

**Model Answer:**

The five stages of the SCRM process form a continuous cycle:

**Stage 1 — Risk Identification:** Tools include *FMEA*, *SWOT analysis*, *Delphi technique*, *fault tree analysis*, and *scenario planning*. Output: a *risk register* with all foreseeable risks.

**Stage 2 — Risk Assessment:** Two sub-steps. *Qualitative*: a 5×5 Probability-Impact matrix. *Quantitative*: EMV calculation, RPN from FMEA, and Monte Carlo simulation. Output: a prioritised list of high-score risks.

**Stage 3 — Risk Treatment / Response Selection:** From the four As — *Avoid, Mitigate, Transfer, Accept*. Cost-benefit check ensures the chosen lever has the highest ROI. Output: a *mitigation plan* with owners and timelines.

**Stage 4 — Risk Monitoring & Control:** Set up *Key Risk Indicators (KRIs)* — supplier OTIF, port dwell time, inventory cover, FX rate. Trigger *contingency plans* when KRIs breach thresholds. Tools: *dashboards*, *control charts*, *early-warning systems*.

**Stage 5 — Communication & Documentation:** Risk reports to senior management, board, and external stakeholders. Update the risk register. Conduct a *lessons-learned* review after every disruption and feed it back into Stage 1.

**(Refer to the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1 of these notes for the diagram.)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturer is evaluating **two supplier strategies**:
- **Strategy X:** Single source from Supplier S1. P(disruption) = 0.15, loss = ₹40,00,000; no-disruption profit = ₹1,20,00,000.
- **Strategy Y:** Dual source (S1 + S2). P(disruption) = 0.05, loss = ₹25,00,000; no-disruption profit = ₹1,10,00,000. Extra annual cost = ₹10,00,000.

**Compute the EMV of each strategy and recommend the better one.**

**Model Solution:**

**Strategy X:**

$$EMV_X = (0.15 \times -40,00,000) + (0.85 \times 1,20,00,000)$$

$$EMV_X = -6,00,000 + 1,02,00,000$$

$$\boxed{EMV_X = ₹96,00,000}$$

**Strategy Y (gross):**

$$EMV_Y^{gross} = (0.05 \times -25,00,000) + (0.95 \times 1,10,00,000)$$

$$EMV_Y^{gross} = -1,25,000 + 1,04,50,000 = ₹1,03,25,000$$

**Strategy Y (net of extra cost):**

$$EMV_Y^{net} = 1,03,25,000 - 10,00,000$$

$$\boxed{EMV_Y = ₹93,25,000}$$

**Comparison:**

$$EMV_X (₹96,00,000) > EMV_Y (₹93,25,000)$$

**Recommendation:** **Strategy X (Single Sourcing)** is preferred on EMV alone, with an advantage of ₹2,75,000.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** Most students stop after computing EMV and writing the recommendation. To secure full marks, you **must** also discuss the *risk profile* — Strategy Y has a *much lower* probability of disruption (5% vs 15%) and a *lower* worst-case loss. A risk-averse manager may still choose Y. Mentioning this nuance **earns you 1–2 extra marks** and demonstrates Board-level thinking.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Disruption Risks** and **Operational Risks** in a supply chain. Provide **three examples** of each and explain the **two-pronged mitigation approach** (before-event and after-event) for disruption risks.

**Model Answer:**

**Disruption Risks:**
- Low probability, **high impact** events that interrupt the normal flow.
- Examples: (1) A tsunami damaging the Cochin port, (2) A Tier-1 chip supplier going bankrupt, (3) A political embargo on rare earth exports.

**Operational Risks:**
- Higher probability, **lower impact** uncertainties of routine processes.
- Examples: (1) Forecast error of ±10%, (2) Quality defect rate of 2%, (3) Lead-time variance of ±3 days.

**Two-Pronged Mitigation Approach for Disruption Risks:**

1. **Before-Event (Preventive) Controls** — Reduce *P*.
   - Multi-sourcing or dual sourcing of critical components.
   - Supplier financial-health audits and resilience scores.
   - Geographic diversification of manufacturing bases.
   - Business Continuity Planning (BCP) and Disaster Recovery Drills.
   - Cybersecurity penetration testing on the WMS/TMS.

2. **After-Event (Mitigative) Controls** — Reduce *I*.
   - Safety stock of critical SKUs.
   - Postponement strategy to delay customisation.
   - Cargo & business-interruption insurance.
   - Pre-arranged emergency logistics contracts with 3PLs.
   - Alternate routing and expedited shipping.

> **Bow-tie diagram** (see Section 4.4) is the ideal visual aid for this answer.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** An electronics OEM uses FMEA to analyse a soldering-defect risk in its PCB line. The failure mode is *"cold joint on BGA package"*. The team has assigned: **Severity (S) = 8**, **Occurrence (O) = 5**, **Detection (D) = 4**. The OEM also has an alternative — using **selective soldering** — which would change the scores to **S = 8, O = 2, D = 2**. The cost of the alternative is ₹20,00,000 per year; the average loss from cold-joint failures is ₹15,00,000 per year. **Should the OEM adopt selective soldering?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute RPN for the current process:**

$$RPN_{current} = S \times O \times D = 8 \times 5 \times 4 = \boxed{160}$$

**Step 2 — Compute RPN for the selective-soldering alternative:**

$$RPN_{new} = 8 \times 2 \times 2 = \boxed{32}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the reduction in expected loss (EL):**

Using $EL = P \times I$ where $P$ is proportional to Occurrence (O) on a 1–10 scale:

$$EL_{current} \propto O_{current} = 5 \Rightarrow EL_{current} = ₹15,00,000$$

$$EL_{new} \propto O_{new} = 2 \Rightarrow EL_{new} = 15,00,000 \times \frac{2}{5} = ₹6,00,000$$

**Step 4 — Compute annual benefit of switching:**

$$\text{Benefit} = EL_{current} - EL_{new} = 15,00,000 - 6,00,000 = ₹9,00,000$$

**Step 5 — Compute net benefit (Benefit – Cost of alternative):**

$$\text{Net Benefit} = 9,00,000 - 20,00,000 = -₹11,00,000$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

The selective-soldering alternative has a **negative net benefit of ₹11,00,000**, meaning the cost of mitigation exceeds the expected loss avoided.

**However**, we must also factor in **intangible benefits**: better brand reputation, lower warranty claims, higher customer satisfaction. If these are valued at, say, ₹12,00,000 per year, the strategy becomes worthwhile.

**Recommendation:** **Do not adopt selective soldering on a purely financial basis**. Re-evaluate the RPN after first attempting **lower-cost mitigations** such as improved solder-paste inspection (which lowers D from 4 to 2) — this would reduce the current RPN to $8 \times 5 \times 2 = 80$, achieving 50% RPN reduction at a fraction of the cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Valuation Pitfall — Top 3 mistakes on FMEA problems:**
> 1. Forgetting to compare **RPN reduction** with **mitigation cost** — the question is always *economic*, not *technical*.
> 2. Confusing **Severity** with **Impact** — severity is technical; impact in EMV is monetary.
> 3. Ignoring the **Detection (D)** factor — lowering D (e.g., via X-ray inspection) is often the cheapest way to reduce RPN. Examiners award marks for this insight.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

### Definitions You Must Know Word-for-Word
- **Risk** = Probability × Impact × (1/Detectability) [in the broader sense].
- **SCRM** = systematic identification, assessment, mitigation, and monitoring of SC risks.
- **EMV** = probability-weighted sum of monetary outcomes.
- **RPN** = Severity × Occurrence × Detection (FMEA metric).
- **VaR** = μ + z·σ (statistical risk exposure).

### The 5-Stage Cycle (in order)
**Identify → Assess → Treat → Monitor → Communicate** — and the loop closes back to Identify via *lessons learned*.

### The 4 As of Risk Response
**Avoid, Mitigate, Transfer, Accept** — in that order of preference per KTU's risk-management hierarchy. *Accept* is the last resort, never the first.

### The 2 Risk Categories
- **Disruption** (low P, high I) — handled via BCP, insurance, multi-sourcing.
- **Operational** (high P, low I) — handled via safety stock, forecasting, Six Sigma.

### Numerical Formulas (carry a formula sheet if permitted)
1. $EMV = \sum P_i L_i$
2. $RPN = S \times O \times D$
3. $EL = P \times I$
4. $VaR = \mu + z_{\alpha} \sigma$
5. $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LTD}$
6. $TC_{risk} = TC_{direct} + EMV_{disruption} + C_{mitigation}$

### Visual Aids to Draw in the Exam
- **5×5 Probability-Impact Matrix** (always expected in part-a).
- **SCRM cycle flowchart** (5 stages in a circle).
- **Bow-tie diagram** (cause → event → consequence with preventive and mitigative controls).
- **Risk register table** (with at least 4–5 rows filled).

### Examiner Pet Phrases to Use
- *"On a risk-adjusted basis…"* — shows you understand variance, not just EMV.
- *"Risk-averse managers may choose a lower-EMV alternative with lower variance."* — distinguishes you from a 70% answer.
- *"Insurance transfers the financial impact, not the probability of occurrence."* — common-sense nuance that examiners reward.
- *"Detection (D) in FMEA is inversely related to risk — the harder to detect, the higher the RPN."* — Board-level concept.

### Common Numerical Pitfalls
- Forgetting to **subtract extra cost** from gross EMV in dual-sourcing problems.
- Confusing **Severity** (1–10 in FMEA) with **monetary Impact** (in ₹).
- Using **wrong z-score** — $z_{0.95} = 1.645$, $z_{0.99} = 2.326$, $z_{0.975} = 1.96$.

### One-Sentence Final Mantra
> *"Identify → Quantify (EMV/RPN) → Mitigate (4 As) → Monitor (KRIs) → Learn (feedback loop)."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 4PL and 5PL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET524 - LOGISTICS & SUPPLY CHAIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** 4PL and 5PL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — 4PL and 5PL: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Evolution of Logistics Service Providers (Context Setting)

Before diving into 4PL and 5PL, it is essential to understand where they sit in the evolutionary chain of logistics service providers. KTU frequently tests this hierarchy in Part A questions.

| Tier | Provider Type | Core Role |
|------|---------------|-----------|
| 1PL | First Party Logistics | Shipper performs own transport (in-house) |
| 2PL | Second Party Logistics | Asset-based carrier (owns trucks/warehouses) |
| 3PL | Third Party Logistics | Outsourced operational logistics (storage, transport, customs) |
| 4PL | Fourth Party Logistics | **Supply Chain Integrator & Strategic Manager** |
| 5PL | Fifth Party Logistics | **Network Orchestrator & Knowledge Aggregator** |

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Fourth Party Logistics (4PL)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **4PL (Fourth Party Logistics Provider):** A 4PL is a supply chain integrator that assembles and manages its own resources, capabilities, and technology, along with those of complementary service providers, to deliver a **comprehensive, end-to-end supply chain solution** to the client. It acts as a **single point of contact** between the client and multiple 3PL providers.

The term was originally coined by **Accenture (formerly Andersen Consulting)** in 1996 to describe a logistics model where a single entity takes complete responsibility for managing and coordinating the entire supply chain on behalf of the client.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "General Contractor" Model

Imagine you are building a house:
- **You (the shipper)** want a finished home, not bricks, cement, or wiring.
- A **2PL** is like a truck that delivers bricks.
- A **3PL** is like a warehouse that stores your bricks and a separate team that does the plumbing.
- A **4PL** is like a **General Contractor** who hires the brick supplier, the plumber, the electrician, the architect, and the painter — and gives **you one final invoice and one single phone number** to call.

The 4PL does **not** own the bricks, nor does it pour the concrete. It simply **orchestrates, plans, integrates, and optimizes** the entire supply chain ecosystem on your behalf.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** 4PL providers do not own physical logistics assets (trucks, warehouses). They are **asset-light** and rely on a network of 3PLs to execute operations.

---

## 1.3 Formal Definition — Fifth Party Logistics (5PL)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **5PL (Fifth Party Logistics Provider):** A 5PL is a logistics aggregator that uses **network optimization, information technology, and electronic marketplaces (e-marketplaces)** to provide **demand aggregation** and **supply aggregation** across multiple clients and service providers. It is the highest level of supply chain orchestration, focused on **knowledge management, innovation, and total network optimization**.

### 1.3.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Air Traffic Controller & Global Auctioneer"

If a 4PL is a General Contractor, a 5PL is the **smart digital platform** that:
1. Looks at **every cargo shipment** needing to move from Kerala to Germany today.
2. Looks at **every empty truck** available in the network.
3. **Matches them in real time** at the best possible price.
4. Tracks **carbon footprint, customs, weather, port congestion** simultaneously.
5. Sells these solutions to **multiple companies at once** in a digital marketplace.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** A 4PL serves **one client** with a fully integrated solution. A 5PL serves **the entire market** as a neutral, technology-driven, multi-client orchestrator.

---

## 1.4 The "PL Hierarchy" Visual Anchor

| Parameter | 3PL | 4PL | 5PL |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| **Ownership of Assets** | Yes (asset-based) | No (asset-light) | No (virtual/network) |
| **Primary Focus** | Operational execution | Strategic management | Network optimization |
| **Number of Clients** | Multiple | **One** (dedicated) | **Many** (aggregated) |
| **Technology Role** | Supportive | Integrated | **Core business** |
| **Relationship** | Transactional | **Partnership** | Marketplace / Platform |
| **Decision Level** | Tactical | **Strategic** | **Network-wide** |
| **Example Activity** | Warehousing, trucking | SCM consulting, integration | E-marketplaces, demand aggregation |

---

## 1.5 Standard Industry Metrics Referenced by KTU

- **Asset Ownership Score:** **0%** for 4PL/5PL (purely orchestrators)
- **Time Horizon:** 4PL works on **3–5 year strategic cycles**; 5PL operates in **real-time digital cycles**
- **Number of 3PLs managed by a 4PL:** Typically **3 to 7** partners per client
- **Cost Savings Target:** A well-implemented 4PL can deliver **10–15%** supply chain cost reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 4PL — Theoretical Decomposition

### 2.1.1 The Four Pillars of 4PL (Accenture Framework)

A 4PL provider delivers value through four strategic pillars. KTU frequently asks students to "explain the role of 4PL" — knowing these pillars is mandatory.

1. **Supply Chain Re-engineering (SCR)**
   - Redesigns the client's entire supply chain structure
   - Eliminates non-value-adding activities
   - Example: A 4PL may recommend relocating a regional warehouse to a central hub after demand-pattern analysis

2. **Resource and Capability Integration**
   - Combines the client's resources with 3PLs, technology vendors, and consultants
   - Creates a **virtual supply chain organization** with no single asset owner

3. **Technology Enablement**
   - Deploys advanced IT systems (WMS, TMS, SCM platforms)
   - Provides **end-to-end visibility** through a single dashboard
   - Uses AI/ML for demand forecasting under the 4PL's guidance

4. **Continuous Improvement and Change Management**
   - Operates on a **gain-sharing model** (4PL is paid based on cost savings)
   - Reviews KPIs quarterly and re-engineers continuously

### 2.1.2 The 4PL Operating Model — Step-by-Step Logic

| Step | Action | KTU-Exam Focus |
|------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | Client outsources entire SCM to 4PL | "What is outsourced?" — *not operations, but management* |
| 2 | 4PL audits existing supply chain | Identification of inefficiencies |
| 3 | 4PL designs an optimized supply chain | Strategic redesign |
| 4 | 4PL selects and manages multiple 3PLs | Vendor selection & governance |
| 5 | 4PL implements technology platform | TMS/WMS/RFID integration |
| 6 | 4PL monitors KPIs and shares gains | Gain-sharing model |
| 7 | Continuous re-engineering | Long-term partnership |

### 2.1.3 Advantages and Disadvantages of 4PL (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often poses Part A questions like *"List four advantages of 4PL"* or *"What are the limitations of 4PL?"*

**Advantages**
- Single point of accountability
- Access to best-in-class 3PLs without managing them
- Strategic focus for the client (client can focus on core business)
- Technology investment is borne by the 4PL
- Risk mitigation through diversified 3PL base

**Disadvantages**
- High dependency on the 4PL (vendor lock-in)
- Loss of direct control over logistics operations
- Confidentially concerns (4PL handles sensitive data)
- Limited number of qualified 4PL providers globally
- Higher cost in the short term

---

## 2.2 5PL — Theoretical Decomposition

### 2.2.1 The Three Core Functions of 5PL

1. **Demand Aggregation**
   - Combines logistics needs of multiple shippers
   - Achieves **economies of scale** that no single client could achieve
   - Example: Pooling shipments of 50 SMEs on one route

2. **Supply Aggregation**
   - Combines capacity of multiple carriers/3PLs
   - Fills empty backhauls and reduces deadhead miles
   - Builds a **virtual asset network**

3. **Network Optimization (Digital Brain)**
   - Uses AI, IoT, Big Data, Blockchain
   - Optimizes routes, modes, and carriers in real time
   - Provides **end-to-end visibility** across the entire ecosystem

### 2.2.2 The 5PL Operating Model

| Step | Action | Why It Matters |
|------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | Shippers post logistics requirements on a **digital e-marketplace** | Transparency |
| 2 | 5PL platform aggregates these demands | Demand aggregation |
| 3 | Carriers/3PLs post available capacity | Supply aggregation |
| 4 | 5PL algorithm matches demand with supply | Network optimization |
| 5 | Best-fit combination is selected based on cost, time, carbon, reliability | Multi-criteria optimization |
| 6 | Real-time tracking and IoT-based monitoring | Visibility |
| 7 | Payment, feedback, and rating cycle closed in the platform | Trust building |

### 2.2.3 The 5PL Value Proposition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **5PL is essentially a Logistics-as-a-Service (LaaS) platform** that monetizes information, network effects, and orchestration capability rather than physical movement of goods.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — 4PL & 5PL Decision Models

Although 4PL/5PL are managerial concepts, KTU expects students to use quantitative decision models when justifying outsourcing decisions. The following formulas are critical.

### 2.3.1 Cost Savings Under 4PL Gain-Sharing Model

$$C_{savings} = C_{baseline} - C_{4PL} - C_{3PL\_combined}$$

Where:
- $C_{baseline}$ = Original in-house supply chain cost
- $C_{4PL}$ = Fee paid to the 4PL integrator
- $C_{3PL\_combined}$ = Combined operational cost of all 3PLs managed by 4PL

### 2.3.2 4PL Gain-Sharing Split

The 4PL's incentive is typically a percentage of the savings achieved:

$$G_{4PL} = \alpha \times C_{savings}$$

$$G_{client} = (1 - \alpha) \times C_{savings}$$

Where:
- $G_{4PL}$ = Gain earned by the 4PL provider
- $G_{client}$ = Gain retained by the client
- $\alpha$ = **Gain-share ratio** (typically 0.30 to 0.50)

### 2.3.3 5PL Network Optimization — Load Consolidation Efficiency

The 5PL aggregates fragmented shipments into full truckloads (FTL) or optimized container loads:

$$E_{consolidation} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i^{utilized}}{V_{vehicle}^{capacity}}$$

Where:
- $E_{consolidation}$ = Vehicle utilization efficiency (target: 0.85 to 0.95)
- $V_i^{utilized}$ = Volume of consolidated shipment $i$
- $V_{vehicle}^{capacity}$ = Total vehicle capacity
- $n$ = Number of aggregated shippers

### 2.3.4 5PL Cost per Unit Shipped (Aggregation Benefit)

$$\bar{C}_{unit} = \frac{C_{total}^{5PL} + \sum_{j=1}^{m} C_{j}^{carrier}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}$$

Where:
- $\bar{C}_{unit}$ = Average cost per unit shipped after aggregation
- $C_{total}^{5PL}$ = 5PL platform fee
- $C_{j}^{carrier}$ = Cost of carrier $j$
- $Q_i$ = Quantity shipped by client $i$
- $m$ = Number of carriers in the aggregated network

### 2.3.5 4PL Service Level Index (Composite KPI)

A KTU-favorite concept is the composite Service Level Indicator used by 4PLs to measure performance:

$$SLI = w_1 \cdot OTD + w_2 \cdot IF + w_3 \cdot DOF - w_4 \cdot LT_{variance}$$

Where:
- $OTD$ = On-Time Delivery percentage
- $IF$ = Inventory Fill rate
- $DOF$ = Damage-free Order percentage
- $LT_{variance}$ = Lead Time variance ratio
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ = Weights assigned by the client (sum to **1.0**)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** Always assume $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ unless otherwise stated in the question.

### 2.3.6 Summary Cheat-Sheet Table

| Formula | Use Case | KTU Importance |
|---------|----------|----------------|
| $C_{savings} = C_{baseline} - C_{4PL} - C_{3PL\_combined}$ | Justifying 4PL outsourcing | ★★★★★ |
| $G_{4PL} = \alpha \times C_{savings}$ | Gain-sharing model | ★★★★ |
| $E_{consolidation} = \frac{\sum V_i^{utilized}}{V_{capacity}}$ | 5PL load aggregation | ★★★★ |
| $\bar{C}_{unit} = \frac{C_{5PL} + \sum C_{j}^{carrier}}{\sum Q_i}$ | 5PL cost reduction proof | ★★★ |
| $SLI = w_1 OTD + w_2 IF + w_3 DOF - w_4 LT_{variance}$ | 4PL KPI measurement | ★★★★★ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Industry Utility

- **Manufacturing Firms:** Use 4PLs to manage multi-tier supplier networks (Tier 1, Tier 2, Tier 3 suppliers)
- **E-Commerce Giants:** Use 5PL platforms (e.g., Flexport, Freightos) to dynamically match cargo with capacity
- **Automotive Industry:** A 4PL may manage inbound logistics of 200+ component suppliers
- **Pharmaceuticals:** 5PLs use IoT sensors for cold chain integrity
- **Sustainability:** 5PLs reduce carbon emissions through route optimization and backhaul matching

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — 4PL vs 5PL Decision Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE (End Semester Exam) questions often present a **scenario** and ask the student to *"Justify whether a 4PL or 5PL is more suitable."* The matrix below is the single most important analytical tool for such questions.

| Decision Criterion | Prefer 4PL When | Prefer 5PL When |
|--------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Client Size** | Large enterprise with dedicated supply chain | SMEs seeking shared logistics infrastructure |
| **Strategic Control** | Client wants a single integrator to own the relationship | Client is comfortable with a digital marketplace model |
| **Service Complexity** | Highly customized, multi-modal, multi-country | Standardized, transactional, freight-only |
| **Technology Maturity** | Client has legacy systems needing integration | Client is digitally native (e-commerce, startups) |
| **Cost Structure** | Client wants fixed + gain-sharing | Client wants variable, transaction-based pricing |
| **Risk Appetite** | Client wants managed risk via single partner | Client wants market-driven price discovery |
| **Asset Preference** | Client wants no asset exposure | Client wants to leverage aggregated assets |
| **Visibility Need** | End-to-end, strategic dashboards | Real-time, granular tracking |
| **Time Horizon** | 3–5 year partnership | On-demand, project-based |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — 4PL Gain-Sharing Calculation

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**
> A manufacturing company spends **₹50,00,000 per year** on its in-house supply chain. After engaging a 4PL, the annual 4PL fee is **₹8,00,000** and the combined cost of the three 3PLs managed by the 4PL is **₹32,00,000**. The agreed gain-share ratio ($\alpha$) is **0.40** in favour of the 4PL. Calculate: (a) Total annual savings, (b) Gain of the 4PL, (c) Gain of the client, (d) Effective percentage savings for the client.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $C_{baseline}$ = ₹50,00,000
- $C_{4PL}$ = ₹8,00,000
- $C_{3PL\_combined}$ = ₹32,00,000
- $\alpha$ = 0.40

**Step (a): Total Annual Cost under 4PL Model**

The total annual cost is the sum of the 4PL fee and the combined 3PL cost:

$$C_{total} = C_{4PL} + C_{3PL\_combined}$$

$$C_{total} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 32{,}00{,}000$$

$$C_{total} = 40{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹ per year)}$$

**Step (b): Total Annual Savings**

The savings are the difference between the baseline and the new total cost:

$$C_{savings} = C_{baseline} - C_{total}$$

$$C_{savings} = 50{,}00{,}000 - 40{,}00{,}000$$

$$C_{savings} = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹ per year)}$$

**Step (c): Gain of the 4PL**

Using the gain-sharing formula:

$$G_{4PL} = \alpha \times C_{savings}$$

$$G_{4PL} = 0.40 \times 10{,}00{,}000$$

$$G_{4PL} = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹ per year)}$$

**Step (d): Gain of the Client**

$$G_{client} = (1 - \alpha) \times C_{savings}$$

$$G_{client} = (1 - 0.40) \times 10{,}00{,}000$$

$$G_{client} = 0.60 \times 10{,}00{,}000$$

$$G_{client} = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹ per year)}$$

**Step (e): Effective Percentage Savings for the Client**

$$\text{Client Savings \%} = \frac{G_{client}}{C_{baseline}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Client Savings \%} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\text{Client Savings \%} = 12\%$$

**Final Answer:**
- Total annual cost after 4PL: **₹40,00,000**
- Total annual savings: **₹10,00,000**
- 4PL's gain: **₹4,00,000**
- Client's gain: **₹6,00,000**
- Effective client savings: **12%**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — 5PL Load Consolidation Efficiency

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**
> A 5PL platform aggregates four shippers' cargo onto a single truck with a total capacity of **30 tonnes**. Shipper A ships 8 tonnes, Shipper B ships 7 tonnes, Shipper C ships 6 tonnes, and Shipper D ships 4 tonnes. Calculate: (a) Total load, (b) Vehicle utilization efficiency, (c) Remaining capacity, and (d) Per-unit cost if the total transport cost is **₹45,000**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $V_A = 8$ tonnes
- $V_B = 7$ tonnes
- $V_C = 6$ tonnes
- $V_D = 4$ tonnes
- $V_{capacity} = 30$ tonnes
- $C_{total} = 45{,}000$

**Step (a): Total Consolidated Load**

$$V_{total} = V_A + V_B + V_C + V_D$$

$$V_{total} = 8 + 7 + 6 + 4$$

$$V_{total} = 25 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step (b): Vehicle Utilization Efficiency**

$$E_{consolidation} = \frac{V_{total}}{V_{capacity}}$$

$$E_{consolidation} = \frac{25}{30}$$

$$E_{consolidation} = 0.8333 \text{ (or } 83.33\% \text{)}$$

**Step (c): Remaining Unused Capacity**

$$V_{remaining} = V_{capacity} - V_{total}$$

$$V_{remaining} = 30 - 25$$

$$V_{remaining} = 5 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step (d): Per-Unit Transportation Cost**

$$\bar{C}_{unit} = \frac{C_{total}}{V_{total}}$$

$$\bar{C}_{unit} = \frac{45{,}000}{25}$$

$$\bar{C}_{unit} = 1{,}800 \text{ per tonne}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Total load: **25 tonnes**
- Utilization: **83.33%**
- Remaining capacity: **5 tonnes**
- Per-tonne cost: **₹1,800**

**Inference:** If each shipper had booked an individual vehicle, only one truck per shipper would have run at 25–27% capacity, leading to far higher per-unit costs. The 5PL aggregation yields a **3× utilization improvement** in this scenario.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — 4PL Service Level Index (Composite KPI)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**
> A 4PL reports the following monthly KPIs: OTD = 96%, IF = 92%, DOF = 99%, $LT_{variance}$ = 0.05. The client assigns weights: $w_1 = 0.35$, $w_2 = 0.30$, $w_3 = 0.25$, $w_4 = 0.10$. Calculate the SLI and comment on performance.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $OTD = 0.96$
- $IF = 0.92$
- $DOF = 0.99$
- $LT_{variance} = 0.05$
- $w_1 = 0.35$, $w_2 = 0.30$, $w_3 = 0.25$, $w_4 = 0.10$

**Step 1: Verify Weight Constraint**

$$w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 0.35 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.10 = 1.00 \checkmark$$

**Step 2: Apply the SLI Formula**

$$SLI = w_1 \cdot OTD + w_2 \cdot IF + w_3 \cdot DOF - w_4 \cdot LT_{variance}$$

$$SLI = (0.35 \times 0.96) + (0.30 \times 0.92) + (0.25 \times 0.99) - (0.10 \times 0.05)$$

**Step 3: Compute Each Term**

- $0.35 \times 0.96 = 0.3360$
- $0.30 \times 0.92 = 0.2760$
- $0.25 \times 0.99 = 0.2475$
- $0.10 \times 0.05 = 0.0050$

**Step 4: Sum the Terms**

$$SLI = 0.3360 + 0.2760 + 0.2475 - 0.0050$$

$$SLI = 0.8595 - 0.0050$$

$$SLI = 0.8545 \text{ (or } 85.45\% \text{)}$$

**Comment:** A SLI of **85.45%** is considered **good but improvable**. The high OTD and DOF scores are strong, but the IF rate of 92% and the small lead-time variance indicate minor inefficiencies that the 4PL must address in the next KPI review cycle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Evolution of Logistics Service Providers (1PL → 5PL)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef tier fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    classDef four fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#bf360c,stroke-width:3px
    classDef five fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#311b92,stroke-width:3px

    P1[1PL - Shipper Owns Transport]:::tier
    P2[2PL - Asset-Based Carrier]:::tier
    P3[3PL - Outsourced Operations]:::tier
    P4[4PL - Strategic Integrator]:::four
    P5[5PL - Network Orchestrator]:::five

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5

    A1[In-House] -.-> P1
    A2[Owns Trucks Warehouses] -.-> P2
    A3[Storage Transport Customs] -.-> P3
    A4[Manages Multiple 3PLs] -.-> P4
    A5[Digital E-Marketplace] -.-> P5
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — 4PL Operating Model (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef client fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef fourpl fill:#ffe082,stroke:#e65100,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef threepl fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef tech fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000

    C[Client Enterprise]:::client
    F4[4PL Integrator]:::fourpl

    C -->|1 Outsource SCM Strategy| F4
    F4 -->|2 Supply Chain Audit and Reengineering| C
    F4 -->|3 KPI Monitoring and Reporting| C

    T1[3PL Provider A Transport]:::threepl
    T2[3PL Provider B Warehousing]:::threepl
    T3[3PL Provider C Customs]:::threepl
    T4[3PL Provider D Value Added]:::threepl

    F4 -->|4 Manage Contract| T1
    F4 -->|5 Manage Contract| T2
    F4 -->|6 Manage Contract| T3
    F4 -->|7 Manage Contract| T4

    TMS[Integrated TMS Platform]:::tech
    WMS[Integrated WMS Platform]:::tech
    VIZ[End-to-End Visibility Dashboard]:::tech

    F4 --> TMS
    F4 --> WMS
    F4 --> VIZ
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — 5PL E-Marketplace Orchestration Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef shipper fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef fivepl fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef carrier fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000

    S1[Shipper 1 Demand]:::shipper
    S2[Shipper 2 Demand]:::shipper
    S3[Shipper N Demand]:::shipper

    PLAT[5PL E-Marketplace Platform]:::fivepl
    PLAT -->|Aggregates Demand| MATCH
    MATCH[AI Based Match Engine]:::fivepl
    PLAT -->|Aggregates Supply| MATCH

    C1[Carrier 1 Capacity]:::carrier
    C2[Carrier 2 Capacity]:::carrier
    C3[Carrier N Capacity]:::carrier

    IOT[IoT Real Time Tracking]:::data
    BC[Blockchain Ledger]:::data
    AI[Big Data Analytics]:::data

    MATCH --> OPT
    OPT[Optimized Route Mode and Cost]:::data
    OPT --> DEL[Final Delivery to End Customer]:::shipper

    DEL --> IOT
    IOT --> PLAT
    PLAT --> BC
    PLAT --> AI
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Decision Flow: Choosing Between 4PL and 5PL

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef start fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef yes fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef no fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000

    A[Company Needs Logistics Outsourcing]:::start
    A --> B{Need Single Dedicated Integrator}:::logic

    B -->|YES| C{Need Customized Multi-Country SCM}:::logic
    B -->|NO| D{Want Shared Digital Marketplace}:::logic

    C -->|YES| E[Choose 4PL Provider]:::yes
    C -->|NO| F{Want Transactional Freight Booking}:::logic
    F -->|YES| E2[Choose 5PL Platform]:::yes

    D -->|YES| E2
    D -->|NO| G[Re-evaluate Outsourcing Strategy]:::no

    E --> H[Strategic Long-Term Partnership]
    E2 --> I[On-Demand Transactional Model]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]
**Define 4PL. List any two characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**
A **4PL (Fourth Party Logistics)** provider is a supply chain integrator that assembles and manages its own resources, capabilities, and technology along with those of complementary service providers (typically 3PLs) to deliver a comprehensive end-to-end supply chain solution to a client.

**Two characteristics:**
1. **Asset-light** — Does not own trucks, warehouses, or other physical logistics assets; relies on partner 3PLs.
2. **Strategic focus** — Operates at the strategic level, managing the client's entire supply chain rather than performing day-to-day operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for 3 marks:** Definition = 1 mark, each characteristic = 1 mark (2 marks). Avoid writing more than 2 characteristics.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]
**What is 5PL? How is it different from 3PL?**

**Model Answer:**
**5PL (Fifth Party Logistics)** is a logistics aggregator that uses network optimization, e-marketplaces, and digital technology to provide demand and supply aggregation across multiple clients and service providers.

**Difference from 3PL:**

| Aspect | 3PL | 5PL |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Scope** | Operational execution | Network-wide orchestration |
| **Clients** | Multiple, transactional | Many, marketplace-based |
| **Technology** | Supportive function | Core business model |
| **Asset Ownership** | Usually asset-based | Asset-less (virtual) |
| **Value Creation** | Physical movement of goods | Information, aggregation, optimization |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> KTU ESE Rule: *Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student may answer either the full question or choose between the two alternatives provided.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Pattern]

**a)** Explain the concept of 4PL with a suitable diagram. Discuss the **four strategic pillars** of 4PL as defined by Accenture. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A manufacturing firm's current annual supply chain cost is **₹80,00,000**. After engaging a 4PL provider, the 4PL charges an annual fee of **₹12,00,000**, and the combined cost of three 3PLs managed by the 4PL is **₹50,00,000**. The gain-sharing ratio ($\alpha$) is **0.35** in favour of the 4PL. Calculate: (i) Total annual savings, (ii) 4PL's gain, (iii) Client's net gain, and (iv) Effective percentage savings for the client. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A**

**(a) Concept of 4PL and the Four Strategic Pillars (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):**
4PL is a supply chain integrator that assumes **strategic responsibility** for managing the client's end-to-end supply chain by orchestrating 3PLs, technology providers, and consultants under a single accountable relationship. Coined by **Accenture in 1996**.

**Diagram (2 marks) — Verbal representation:**
*Client → 4PL Integrator → (3PL Transport + 3PL Warehousing + 3PL Customs + Technology Platform) → End Customer.*

**The Four Strategic Pillars (3 marks):**

1. **Supply Chain Re-engineering:** Redesigns the structural flow of materials, information, and finance.
2. **Resource and Capability Integration:** Combines 3PL operational expertise with the 4PL's strategic and technological capabilities.
3. **Technology Enablement:** Deploys TMS, WMS, IoT, and AI for end-to-end visibility.
4. **Continuous Improvement:** Operates on a gain-sharing model with quarterly KPI reviews.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $C_{baseline}$ = ₹80,00,000
- $C_{4PL}$ = ₹12,00,000
- $C_{3PL\_combined}$ = ₹50,00,000
- $\alpha$ = 0.35

**Step 1: Total Annual Cost under 4PL Model (1 mark)**

$$C_{total} = C_{4PL} + C_{3PL\_combined} = 12{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}00{,}000 = 62{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2: Total Annual Savings (2 marks)**

$$C_{savings} = C_{baseline} - C_{total} = 80{,}00{,}000 - 62{,}00{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3: 4PL's Gain (1 mark)**

$$G_{4PL} = \alpha \times C_{savings} = 0.35 \times 18{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}30{,}000$$

**Step 4: Client's Net Gain (1 mark)**

$$G_{client} = (1 - \alpha) \times C_{savings} = 0.65 \times 18{,}00{,}000 = 11{,}70{,}000$$

**Step 5: Effective Client Savings % (2 marks)**

$$\text{Client Savings \%} = \frac{G_{client}}{C_{baseline}} \times 100 = \frac{11{,}70{,}000}{80{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 14.625\%$$

**Final Tabular Answer (Presentation):**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Total annual cost after 4PL | ₹62,00,000 |
| Total annual savings | ₹18,00,000 |
| 4PL's gain | ₹6,30,000 |
| Client's net gain | ₹11,70,000 |
| Effective client savings | **14.63%** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Pattern]

**a)** Explain the concept of 5PL. Discuss its **three core functions** and **two key advantages** over 4PL. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A 5PL e-marketplace aggregates cargo from 5 shippers onto a single 40-tonne truck. The individual shipments are: 10 t, 8 t, 7 t, 6 t, and 5 t. If the total transportation cost is **₹72,000**, calculate: (i) Total consolidated load, (ii) Vehicle utilization efficiency, (iii) Per-tonne transportation cost, and (iv) Comment on the benefit of 5PL aggregation compared to each shipper using an individual 20-tonne truck at the same per-tonne rate of ₹1,800. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

**(a) Concept of 5PL, Three Core Functions, and Advantages (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):**
5PL is a **network-orchestrating, asset-less logistics aggregator** that uses digital platforms (e-marketplaces), AI, and IoT to provide demand aggregation, supply aggregation, and end-to-end network optimization across multiple shippers and carriers.

**Three Core Functions (3 marks):**

1. **Demand Aggregation:** Combines the logistics needs of multiple shippers to achieve economies of scale unattainable by a single client.
2. **Supply Aggregation:** Combines the available capacity of multiple carriers, reducing empty backhauls and deadhead miles.
3. **Network Optimization:** Uses Big Data, AI, and IoT to dynamically match demand with the best-fit supply option in real time.

**Two Key Advantages over 4PL (2 marks):**

1. **Multi-client scale:** While a 4PL serves one client, a 5PL serves the entire market, generating network effects and far lower per-unit costs.
2. **Marketplace transparency:** Real-time price discovery, carrier ratings, and electronic bidding create a transparent, competitive environment that 4PLs cannot replicate.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $V_A = 10$ t, $V_B = 8$ t, $V_C = 7$ t, $V_D = 6$ t, $V_E = 5$ t
- $V_{capacity} = 40$ t
- $C_{total}^{5PL}$ = ₹72,000

**Step 1: Total Consolidated Load (1 mark)**

$$V_{total} = 10 + 8 + 7 + 6 + 5 = 36 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2: Vehicle Utilization Efficiency (2 marks)**

$$E_{consolidation} = \frac{V_{total}}{V_{capacity}} = \frac{36}{40} = 0.90 \text{ or } 90\%$$

**Step 3: Per-Tonne Cost (1 mark)**

$$\bar{C}_{unit} = \frac{72{,}000}{36} = 2{,}000 \text{ per tonne}$$

**Step 4: Comparative Analysis with Individual Trucks (3 marks)**

If each shipper used a separate 20-tonne truck at the same per-tonne rate of ₹1,800, each shipper's effective utilization would be:

- Shipper A: 10/20 = 50%
- Shipper B: 8/20 = 40%
- Shipper C: 7/20 = 35%
- Shipper D: 6/20 = 30%
- Shipper E: 5/20 = 25%

Each individual truck's actual cost at ₹1,800/tonne on 20-tonne capacity = **₹36,000** per truck. For 5 trucks, total = **₹1,80,000**.

5PL cost = **₹72,000**.

$$\text{Cost Savings} = 1{,}80{,}000 - 72{,}000 = 1{,}08{,}000$$

$$\text{Savings \%} = \frac{1{,}08{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000} \times 100 = 60\%$$

**Final Tabular Answer (Presentation):**

| Parameter | 5PL Model | Individual Truck Model |
|-----------|-----------|------------------------|
| Total load | 36 t | 36 t (across 5 trucks) |
| Total cost | ₹72,000 | ₹1,80,000 |
| Cost per tonne | ₹2,000 | ₹5,000 |
| Average utilization | 90% | 36% |
| **Net Savings** | **₹1,08,000 (60%)** | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the gain-sharing formula** — students often write only $C_{savings}$ without applying $\alpha$. Always state the gain-split explicitly.
> 2. **Misinterpreting "effective percentage savings for the client"** — this is $G_{client}/C_{baseline}$, NOT $C_{savings}/C_{baseline}$. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 3. **Confusing 4PL with 3PL** — 4PL does NOT own assets and does NOT perform operations. It only **manages** 3PLs. Writing operational tasks as 4PL functions is a common error.
> 4. **Forgetting to comment on results** — in 7-mark numerical questions, the final 1–2 marks are reserved for **inference/comment**. Always end with a one-line interpretation.
> 5. **Skipping the weight-constraint check** in SLI problems — failing to verify $\sum w_i = 1$ loses a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Block — Read this 5 times before the exam.**

### **Core Definitions**
- ✅ **4PL** = Supply chain **integrator and strategic manager**; asset-light; manages multiple 3PLs for one client; coined by Accenture in 1996.
- ✅ **5PL** = Network **orchestrator and aggregator**; uses e-marketplaces; multi-client; focuses on demand aggregation, supply aggregation, and digital optimization.

### **Four Strategic Pillars of 4PL (Accenture)**
- ✅ Supply Chain Re-engineering
- ✅ Resource & Capability Integration
- ✅ Technology Enablement
- ✅ Continuous Improvement (gain-sharing)

### **Three Core Functions of 5PL**
- ✅ Demand Aggregation
- ✅ Supply Aggregation
- ✅ Network Optimization (AI/IoT/Big Data)

### **Must-Memorize Formulas**
- ✅ $C_{savings} = C_{baseline} - C_{4PL} - C_{3PL\_combined}$
- ✅ $G_{4PL} = \alpha \times C_{savings}$ (where $\alpha \approx 0.30$ to $0.50$)
- ✅ $G_{client} = (1 - \alpha) \times C_{savings}$
- ✅ $E_{consolidation} = \frac{\sum V_i^{utilized}}{V_{capacity}}$ (target 0.85–0.95)
- ✅ $SLI = w_1 OTD + w_2 IF + w_3 DOF - w_4 LT_{variance}$ with $\sum w_i = 1$

### **Comparative Snapshot**
- ✅ **3PL →** does operations; owns assets; multiple clients; transactional
- ✅ **4PL →** owns the relationship; asset-light; single dedicated client; strategic
- ✅ **5PL →** owns the platform; asset-less; many clients; transactional + network

### **Key Numerical Traps to Avoid**
- ✅ Client's **percentage savings** is based on $G_{client}$, not $C_{savings}$.
- ✅ Always verify $\sum w_i = 1$ before computing SLI.
- ✅ Vehicle utilization is between 0 and 1 (multiply by 100 only for percentage display).
- ✅ 5PL aggregation benefits increase with the **number of shippers** and the **fragmentation** of shipments.

### **Industry Examples to Quote in Long Answers (Bonus Marks)**
- ✅ 4PL examples: **Accenture, IBM, Ryder, Deloitte Supply Chain Practice**
- ✅ 5PL examples: **Flexport, Freightos, Uber Freight, Convoy (now defunct), Maersk Spot**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods and Techniques for Allocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET524 - LOGISTICS & SUPPLY CHAIN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Methods and Techniques for Allocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Allocation in Logistics & Supply Chain

In the context of **Operations Research (OR)** and **Logistics Engineering**, *Allocation* refers to the family of mathematical optimization problems that determine the **most efficient distribution of limited resources** (supply) among competing demands (destinations or activities) to **minimize total cost** or **maximize total profit/performance**, subject to a set of linear constraints.

The three classical allocation problems studied in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU 2024 Definition**
> An **Allocation Problem** is a special class of Linear Programming Problem (LPP) in which the objective is to assign a finite set of resources (men, machines, vehicles, products) to a finite set of tasks (jobs, destinations, routes) on a **one-to-one basis**, such that the total cost of assignment is minimized or total effectiveness is maximized.

The three pillars of allocation in KTU Module 3 are:

| S.No. | Allocation Method | Core Decision |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transportation Problem (TP)** | How much to ship from multiple origins to multiple destinations |
| 2 | **Assignment Problem (AP)** | Which single resource should be assigned to which single task (1:1 mapping) |
| 3 | **Transshipment Problem** | Routing shipments through intermediate nodes (warehouses/hubs) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Courier Company**
> Imagine you run **Delhivery-Kerala** with **4 warehouses** in Kerala (Kochi, Trivandrum, Kozhikode, Thrissur) and **5 major delivery hubs** across the state. Every day, you must decide:
> - **How many parcels** to send from each warehouse to each hub (this is a **Transportation Problem**).
> - **Which single delivery agent** to assign to **which single delivery zone** (this is an **Assignment Problem**).
> - Whether to **route parcels through an intermediate sorting hub** before final delivery (this is a **Transshipment Problem**).
>
> The methods in this module give you the **mathematical shortcut** to make these decisions optimally without trial-and-error.

### 1.3 Mathematical Structure (Standard Form)

The general Linear Programming formulation of an allocation problem is:

$$\text{Decision Variables: } x_{ij} = \text{quantity/units assigned from source } i \text{ to destination } j$$

$$\text{Objective: } \min Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij}x_{ij}$$

Subject to the supply constraints, demand constraints, and non-negativity:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} \leq S_i \quad \text{(Supply constraint at source } i\text{)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} \geq D_j \quad \text{(Demand constraint at destination } j\text{)}$$

$$x_{ij} \geq 0 \quad \text{(Non-negativity)}$$

Here, $c_{ij}$ represents the **per-unit cost** of allocation from source $i$ to destination $j$, $S_i$ is the supply at source $i$, and $D_j$ is the demand at destination $j$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Balanced vs. Unbalanced Problems**
> A transportation/assignment problem is **balanced** when **Total Supply = Total Demand**. If not equal, we introduce a **dummy source/destination** with zero cost to balance the matrix — this is a frequently tested KTU concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Transportation Problem (TP) — Step-by-Step Logic

The Transportation Problem determines **how much** to ship from each origin to each destination. It is a special LP with $m \times n$ variables, but solved using the **MODI method** rather than the Simplex method for speed.

#### Why TP is Special:
- Constraint matrix has a structure where each variable $x_{ij}$ appears in exactly **one supply row** and **one demand column**.
- Hence, the constraint matrix is **totally unimodular**, guaranteeing integer solutions for integer supplies/demands.

#### TP Solution Procedure (Two-Phase Logic):

**Phase 1 — Find Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS):**
1. **North-West Corner Rule (NWCR)** — Simplest; ignores costs.
2. **Least Cost Method (LCM)** — Greedy; picks the lowest cost cell first.
3. **Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM)** — Most accurate IBFS; uses penalty cost.

**Phase 2 — Test for Optimality:**
1. **Stepping Stone Method** — Iterates by tracing closed paths.
2. **MODI Method (u, v method)** — Computes opportunity costs using dual variables.

#### Deep Explanation of VAM (Vogel's Approximation Method):

VAM works on the principle of **minimizing the penalty (regret) cost** of NOT choosing the lowest-cost cell in each row/column.

- For each row, compute: **Penalty = (Second smallest cost) − (Smallest cost)**
- For each column, compute: **Penalty = (Second smallest cost) − (Smallest cost)**
- Select the row/column with the **highest penalty**.
- In that row/column, allocate the **maximum possible quantity** to the **lowest-cost cell**.
- Update supply/demand; cross out satisfied rows/columns; repeat.

### 2.2 The Assignment Problem (AP) — Deep Logic

The Assignment Problem is a **special case of TP** where each $x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$ (binary) and $m = n$. The optimal solution is found using the **Hungarian Method** (developed by Harold W. Kuhn, 1955).

#### Hungarian Method Logic Flow:
1. **Row Reduction:** Subtract the row minimum from every element in each row.
2. **Column Reduction:** Subtract the column minimum from every element in each column.
3. **Cover all zeros** using a minimum number of horizontal and vertical lines.
4. If number of lines = order of matrix ($n$), an **optimal assignment exists** — make assignments in zero cells (one per row, one per column).
5. If lines $< n$, find the **smallest uncovered element** ($\theta$), subtract it from all uncovered elements, add it to doubly-covered elements, and **return to Step 3**.

### 2.3 The Transshipment Problem — Conceptual Logic

A Transshipment Problem is a TP where **intermediate nodes** (transshipment points like warehouses) exist between origins and destinations. The mathematical trick is to convert it into a standard TP by:

- Adding a **dummy origin** with supply = $(D - S)$ if demand $>$ supply, or a **dummy destination** if supply $>$ demand.
- Setting shipping costs to/from dummy = 0.
- Allowing shipments between all pairs of transshipment points.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Reference Table**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Balanced TP Condition | $\sum_{i=1}^{m} S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} D_j$ | Units: items, tonnes, units |
| Number of Basic Variables | $m + n - 1$ | Required for non-degenerate BFS |
| Degeneracy in TP | BFS allocations $< m + n - 1$ | Solved by allocating $\epsilon$ (a small positive number) |
| VAM Penalty (Row) | $P_i = c_{i,2nd} - c_{i,1st}$ | Difference between 2nd smallest and smallest in row $i$ |
| VAM Penalty (Column) | $P_j = c_{2nd,j} - c_{1st,j}$ | Difference between 2nd smallest and smallest in column $j$ |
| MODI Opportunity Cost | $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - (u_i + v_j)$ | For non-basic cells; optimality when all $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$ |
| MODI Initial Value | $u_1 = 0$ (or $v_1 = 0$) | Set arbitrarily; compute the rest from $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ for basic cells |
| Hungarian Reduction | New $c_{ij} = c_{ij} - \min(\text{row}) - \min(\text{col})$ | Element-wise operation on the cost matrix |
| Hungarian Cover Test | $\text{Min lines} = n \rightarrow \text{Optimal}$ | Else modify matrix using $\theta$ |
| Hungarian Modification | $\theta = \min(\text{uncovered elements})$ | Subtract from uncovered, add to doubly-covered |
| Transshipment Conversion | Add dummy with $\vert S - D \vert$ | Cost to/from dummy = 0 |
| Maximization Conversion | $c'_{ij} = M - c_{ij}$ | Where $M = \max(c_{ij})$ |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in Logistics

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility**
> - **E-commerce giants** (Amazon, Flipkart) use TP variants daily to determine **warehouse-to-customer fulfillment routes**.
> - **Courier companies** (BlueDart, DTDC) use the **Assignment Problem** to allocate delivery agents to zones.
> - **Airlines** use Transshipment Models for **hub-and-spoke routing** at airports.
> - **Trucking companies** use TP for **vehicle loading and dispatch optimization**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Worked Example — Transportation Problem (VAM + MODI)

**Problem Statement:**
A logistics company has 3 warehouses (W1, W2, W3) with capacities of **150, 200, and 250 units** respectively. It must supply 4 retail stores (R1, R2, R3, R4) with demands of **100, 175, 125, and 200 units** respectively. The per-unit shipping cost (in ₹) matrix is given below. Find the **minimum cost shipping plan** using VAM and test optimality with MODI.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & R1 & R2 & R3 & R4 & \text{Supply} \\
\hline
W1 & 4 & 8 & 5 & 6 & 150 \\
\hline
W2 & 6 & 5 & 3 & 4 & 200 \\
\hline
W3 & 7 & 4 & 5 & 6 & 250 \\
\hline
\text{Demand} & 100 & 175 & 125 & 200 & 600 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

#### Step A: Check Balance
Total Supply = $150 + 200 + 250 = 600$ units.
Total Demand = $100 + 175 + 125 + 200 = 600$ units.
**The problem is Balanced.** No dummy row/column required.

#### Step B: Apply Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM)

**Iteration 1:**
- **Row Penalties:** W1: $5 - 4 = 1$, W2: $4 - 3 = 1$, W3: $5 - 4 = 1$
- **Column Penalties:** R1: $6 - 4 = 2$, R2: $5 - 4 = 1$, R3: $5 - 3 = 2$, R4: $6 - 4 = 2$
- **Maximum Penalty = 2** (tied between R1, R3, R4). Choose R1 (any tied column).
- Lowest cost in R1: $W1$ at ₹4. Allocate $\min(150, 100) = 100$ units to $(W1, R1)$.
- Update: W1 supply = $150 - 100 = 50$; R1 demand = $100 - 100 = 0$ (R1 satisfied, cross out).

**Iteration 2:** (Remove R1)
- Remaining supplies: W1=50, W2=200, W3=250. Remaining demands: R2=175, R3=125, R4=200.
- **Row Penalties:** W1: $6 - 5 = 1$, W2: $4 - 3 = 1$, W3: $5 - 4 = 1$
- **Column Penalties:** R2: $5 - 4 = 1$, R3: $5 - 3 = 2$, R4: $6 - 4 = 2$
- **Max Penalty = 2** (R3 or R4). Choose R3. Lowest cost in R3: $W2$ at ₹3. Allocate $\min(200, 125) = 125$ to $(W2, R3)$.
- Update: W2 supply = $200 - 125 = 75$; R3 demand = $125 - 125 = 0$ (R3 satisfied).

**Iteration 3:** (Remove R3)
- Remaining: W1=50, W2=75, W3=250. Demands: R2=175, R4=200.
- **Row Penalties:** W1: $8 - 6 = 2$, W2: $5 - 4 = 1$, W3: $4 - 6 = 2$ (Note: 2nd smallest is 4, smallest is 6, so $4 - 6$ would be negative; penalty is taken as the difference regardless of sign, or zero if row is single cell. With 2 cells [R2=4, R4=6], penalty = $\vert 6 - 4 \vert = 2$).
- **Column Penalties:** R2: $5 - 4 = 1$, R4: $6 - 4 = 2$
- **Max Penalty = 2** (W1, W3, or R4). Choose R4. Lowest cost in R4: $W2$ at ₹4 (tie with W3 at ₹4, but penalty max is at W2 row also = 1, not max). Choose R4 directly. Min cost in R4 = 4 (both W2 and W3). Pick W2. Allocate $\min(75, 200) = 75$ to $(W2, R4)$.
- Update: W2 supply = $75 - 75 = 0$ (W2 satisfied, cross out).

**Iteration 4:** (Remove W2)
- Remaining: W1=50, W3=250. Demands: R2=175, R4=200.
- Allocate to lowest cost: W1→R2 (₹8) vs W3→R2 (₹4). Pick W3→R2. Allocate $\min(250, 175) = 175$.
- Update: W3 supply = $250 - 175 = 75$; R2 demand = $0$ (satisfied).
- Allocate remaining: W3→R4 = 75.

#### VAM Final Allocation Table:
$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & R1 & R2 & R3 & R4 & \text{Supply} \\
\hline
W1 & 100 \text{ (4)} & \text{—} & \text{—} & \text{—} & 150 \\
\hline
W2 & \text{—} & \text{—} & 125 \text{ (3)} & 75 \text{ (4)} & 200 \\
\hline
W3 & \text{—} & 175 \text{ (4)} & \text{—} & 75 \text{ (6)} & 250 \\
\hline
\text{Demand} & 100 & 175 & 125 & 200 & 600 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

#### VAM Initial Cost:
$$Z_{\text{VAM}} = (100 \times 4) + (125 \times 3) + (75 \times 4) + (175 \times 4) + (75 \times 6)$$

Breaking down the calculation:
$$Z = 400 + 375 + 300 + 700 + 450 = 2225$$

$$\boxed{Z_{\text{VAM}} = \text{₹}2225}$$

#### Step C: Test Optimality using MODI Method

**Set up $u_i$ and $v_j$ equations** for basic cells ($u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$), with $u_1 = 0$ (arbitrary starting value).

Basic cells: $(W1,R1), (W2,R3), (W2,R4), (W3,R2), (W3,R4)$.

| Cell | Cost | Equation | Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(W1, R1)$ | 4 | $u_1 + v_1 = 4$ | $0 + v_1 = 4 \Rightarrow v_1 = 4$ |
| $(W2, R3)$ | 3 | $u_2 + v_3 = 3$ | $u_2 + 0 = 3 \Rightarrow u_2 = 3$ |
| $(W2, R4)$ | 4 | $u_2 + v_4 = 4$ | $3 + v_4 = 4 \Rightarrow v_4 = 1$ |
| $(W3, R2)$ | 4 | $u_3 + v_2 = 4$ | $u_3 + 0 = 4 \Rightarrow u_3 = 4$ |
| $(W3, R4)$ | 6 | $u_3 + v_4 = 6$ | $4 + 1 = 5$ ⚠️ Inconsistency! |

> [!NOTE]
> **Degeneracy Resolution:** The system is inconsistent because we have **5 basic cells** but the matrix has 3 rows + 4 columns = 7 equations worth of constraints. The IBFS is **degenerate** (less than $m+n-1 = 6$ allocations). We must introduce a $\epsilon$ (epsilon) allocation in a non-basic cell to break the degeneracy. Add $\epsilon$ to $(W1, R2)$ or any cell that does not form a closed loop.

**Revised basic cells** (add $\epsilon$ at $(W1, R2)$): $(W1,R1), (W1,R2), (W2,R3), (W2,R4), (W3,R2), (W3,R4)$ — now we have 6 cells.

| Cell | Equation |
|---|---|
| $(W1, R1)$ | $u_1 + v_1 = 4 \Rightarrow v_1 = 4$ |
| $(W1, R2)$ | $u_1 + v_2 = 8 \Rightarrow v_2 = 8$ |
| $(W2, R3)$ | $u_2 + v_3 = 3 \Rightarrow u_2 = 3$ (with $v_3 = 0$) |
| $(W2, R4)$ | $u_2 + v_4 = 4 \Rightarrow v_4 = 1$ |
| $(W3, R2)$ | $u_3 + v_2 = 4 \Rightarrow u_3 = -4$ |
| $(W3, R4)$ | $u_3 + v_4 = 6 \Rightarrow -4 + 1 = -3 \neq 6$ |

Add another $\epsilon$ at $(W1, R3)$: $u_1 + v_3 = 5 \Rightarrow 0 + 0 = 0 \neq 5$. 
Try $\epsilon$ at $(W3, R3)$: $u_3 + v_3 = 5 \Rightarrow -4 + 0 = -4 \neq 5$. 

For consistency, place $\epsilon$ at $(W1, R4)$: $u_1 + v_4 = 6 \Rightarrow 0 + 1 = 1 \neq 6$. 

After systematic trial, place $\epsilon$ at $(W3, R1)$: $u_3 + v_1 = 7 \Rightarrow -4 + 4 = 0 \neq 7$. 

**Optimal placement** to satisfy all six equations: use $\epsilon$ at $(W1, R4)$ and $(W2, R1)$ — but we need only one $\epsilon$ in our 6-cell allocation. Final consistent set: $(W1,R1), (W1,R3), (W2,R3), (W2,R4), (W3,R2), (W3,R4)$.

Let us reset cleanly with allocation cells: $(W1,R1), (W2,R3), (W2,R4), (W3,R2), (W3,R4), (W1,R3_\epsilon)$.

| Equation | Result |
|---|---|
| $u_1 + v_1 = 4$ | $v_1 = 4$ |
| $u_1 + v_3 = 5$ | $v_3 = 5$ |
| $u_2 + v_3 = 3$ | $u_2 = -2$ |
| $u_2 + v_4 = 4$ | $v_4 = 6$ |
| $u_3 + v_2 = 4$ | with $v_2 = 0$ assumed, $u_3 = 4$ |
| $u_3 + v_4 = 6$ | $4 + 6 = 10 \neq 6$ ❌ |

This indicates a fundamental recomputation is needed. For KTU exam purposes, the standard procedure is to **set $u_1 = 0$, $v_j = 0$ for the column with the most basic cells, then solve**. The fully correct computation is shown below assuming the correct degenerate resolution.

**Corrected consistent $u, v$ values** (using $(W1,R1), (W2,R3), (W2,R4), (W3,R2), (W3,R4), (W1,R3)$ as basis):

Set $v_1 = 0$. Then $u_1 = 4$ (from $W1,R1$). From $(W1,R3)$: $u_1 + v_3 = 5 \Rightarrow v_3 = 1$. From $(W2,R3)$: $u_2 + v_3 = 3 \Rightarrow u_2 = 2$. From $(W2,R4)$: $u_2 + v_4 = 4 \Rightarrow v_4 = 2$. From $(W3,R4)$: $u_3 + v_4 = 6 \Rightarrow u_3 = 4$. From $(W3,R2)$: $u_3 + v_2 = 4 \Rightarrow v_2 = 0$. ✓ Consistent.

**Compute Opportunity Costs** $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - (u_i + v_j)$ for **non-basic cells**:

| Cell | $c_{ij}$ | $u_i + v_j$ | $\Delta_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(W1, R2)$ | 8 | $4 + 0 = 4$ | $+4$ |
| $(W1, R4)$ | 6 | $4 + 2 = 6$ | $0$ |
| $(W2, R1)$ | 6 | $2 + 0 = 2$ | $+4$ |
| $(W2, R2)$ | 5 | $2 + 0 = 2$ | $+3$ |
| $(W3, R1)$ | 7 | $4 + 0 = 4$ | $+3$ |
| $(W3, R3)$ | 5 | $4 + 1 = 5$ | $0$ |

All $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$. **The VAM solution is OPTIMAL.**

$$\boxed{Z_{\min} = \text{₹}2225}$$

### 3.2 Complete Worked Example — Assignment Problem (Hungarian Method)

**Problem Statement:**
A logistics firm needs to assign **4 delivery agents** (A1, A2, A3, A4) to **4 delivery zones** (Z1, Z2, Z3, Z4). The effectiveness (in deliveries/day) matrix is given. Find the **maximum effectiveness assignment**.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & Z1 & Z2 & Z3 & Z4 \\
\hline
A1 & 45 & 40 & 50 & 55 \\
\hline
A2 & 50 & 45 & 40 & 45 \\
\hline
A3 & 40 & 55 & 45 & 50 \\
\hline
A4 & 45 & 50 & 55 & 40 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

#### Step 1: Convert Maximization to Minimization
$$c'_{ij} = M - c_{ij}, \quad M = 55$$

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & Z1 & Z2 & Z3 & Z4 \\
\hline
A1 & 10 & 15 & 5 & 0 \\
\hline
A2 & 5 & 10 & 15 & 10 \\
\hline
A3 & 15 & 0 & 10 & 5 \\
\hline
A4 & 10 & 5 & 0 & 15 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

#### Step 2: Row Reduction
Subtract row minimum from each row. Row mins: 0, 5, 0, 0.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & Z1 & Z2 & Z3 & Z4 \\
\hline
A1 & 10 & 15 & 5 & 0 \\
\hline
A2 & 0 & 5 & 10 & 5 \\
\hline
A3 & 15 & 0 & 10 & 5 \\
\hline
A4 & 10 & 5 & 0 & 15 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

#### Step 3: Column Reduction
Column mins: 0, 0, 0, 0. Matrix unchanged.

#### Step 4: Cover Zeros with Minimum Lines
- Draw a line through each row/column containing a zero.
- Rows with zeros: A1 (Z4), A2 (Z1), A3 (Z2), A4 (Z3). All 4 rows covered.
- Number of lines = 4 = order of matrix. **Optimal assignment exists.**

#### Step 5: Make Assignments
- **A1** has only one zero (Z4). Assign $A1 \rightarrow Z4$.
- **A2** has only one remaining zero (Z1). Assign $A2 \rightarrow Z1$.
- **A3** has only one remaining zero (Z2). Assign $A3 \rightarrow Z2$.
- **A4** has only one remaining zero (Z3). Assign $A4 \rightarrow Z3$.

**Optimal Assignment:**
- $A1 \rightarrow Z4$ (effectiveness = 55)
- $A2 \rightarrow Z1$ (effectiveness = 50)
- $A3 \rightarrow Z2$ (effectiveness = 55)
- $A4 \rightarrow Z3$ (effectiveness = 55)

$$\boxed{\text{Total Maximum Effectiveness} = 55 + 50 + 55 + 55 = 215 \text{ deliveries/day}}$$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start with IBFS from VAM or LCM"] --> B["Count basic cells"]
    B --> C{"Basic cells = m + n - 1?"}
    C -- No --> D["Introduce epsilon to fix degeneracy"]

    B -- No --> D["Row Reduction subtract row min"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Column Reduction subtract col min"]
    E --> F["Draw minimum lines to cover all zeros"]
    F --> G{"Number of lines = n?"}
    G -- No --> H["Find theta = min uncovered element"]
    H --> I["Subtract theta from uncovered add to intersections"]
    I --> F
    G -- Yes --> J["Make assignments: one zero per row and column"]
    J --> K["OPTIMAL ASSIGNMENT"]
```
