## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module-end long-answer questions carry **14 marks**, with internal choice. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Both Question A and Question B are fully solved below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **5R framework of reverse logistics** with a suitable example from the **automotive industry**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company faces **stochastic demand for refurbished products** $X \sim N(1000, 200^2)$. The underage cost (lost margin) is ₹80/unit and the overage cost (disposal) is ₹40/unit. Find the **optimal order quantity** and **expected profit** if the sale price is ₹200 and procurement cost is ₹120. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**The 5R Framework:**

| R | Activity | Automotive Example |
|---|----------|-------------------|
| **R1 — Returns** | Customer returns the product (warranty, recall) | Toyota recalls airbags → returns to dealer |
| **R2 — Recalls** | Mandatory safety recalls | Maruti recalls defective fuel pumps |
| **R3 — Repairs** | Restore to working condition | Engine block repair at Bosch service center |
| **R4 — Recycles** | Break down into raw material | Shredder feeds steel back to Tata Steel |
| **R5 — Replacements** | Replace with refurbished unit | Customer gets "good-as-new" refurbished alternator |

**Key Insight (1 mark):** In Toyota's CLSC, ~95% of an end-of-life vehicle's mass is recovered — illustrating the **5R closure of the loop**.

**Why it matters (2 marks):**
- Compliance with **EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility)**.
- Reduction in **virgin material cost by 30–40%**.
- Lower **carbon footprint** vs new manufacturing.

*(+4 marks: one per R explained with automotive example.)*

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Given:** $\mu = 1000$, $\sigma = 200$, $C_u = ₹80$, $C_o = ₹40$, $p = ₹200$, $c = ₹120$.

**Step 1 — Critical fractile**

$$\alpha = \frac{C_u}{C_u + C_o} = \frac{80}{80+40} = \frac{80}{120} = 0.6667$$

**Step 2 — Standard normal z-value**

$$z_{0.6667} \approx 0.4307$$

**Step 3 — Optimal order quantity**

$$Q^* = \mu + z \sigma = 1000 + 0.4307 \times 200 = 1000 + 86.14 = 1086 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Expected sales**

$$E[\min(X,Q^*)] = \mu - \sigma L(z) = 1000 - 200 \times 0.3198 \approx 936 \text{ units}$$

where $L(0.4307) \approx 0.3198$ (standard normal loss function).

**Step 5 — Expected profit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\Pi] &= (p-c) \cdot E[\min(X,Q^*)] - c_o \cdot E[(Q^*-X)^+] \\
&= 80 \times 936 - 40 \times (1086 - 936) \\
&= 74{,}880 - 6{,}000 \\
&= ₹68{,}880
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed):** $Q^* \approx 1086$ units, Expected Profit $\approx ₹68{,}880$ per cycle.

*(+2 marks Step 1, +2 marks Step 2/3, +3 marks Step 4/5 with clear algebra.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Discuss any **four methods/models** that can be used for managing **closed-loop supply chains during pandemics**, citing real-world examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Closed-Loop EOQ** formula and apply it to the following data: Annual demand $D = 8{,}000$ units, ordering cost $A = ₹400$, return order cost $A_r = ₹250$, holding cost $h = ₹15$/unit/yr, return rate $r = 0.4$. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Four CLSC Methods/Models for Pandemic Context:**

1. **Hybrid Push-Pull Inventory (3 marks)**
   - Forward: **pull-based** to match uncertain demand.
   - Reverse: **push-based** to clear used PPE quickly.
   - *Example:* Kerala State Medical Services Corp used **push-based** reverse collection of used PPE bags with strict 24-hour turnaround during the second COVID wave (May 2021).

2. **Stochastic Closed-Loop MILP (3 marks)**
   - Decision variables: $x_{ij}$ (forward flow), $y_{ij}$ (reverse flow), $z_j$ (facility open).
   - *Example:* Zhang et al. (2020) used a **two-stage stochastic MILP** to design CLSC for medical masks with demand uncertainty.

3. **Robust Optimization with Disruption Sets (3 marks)**
   - Solves a **min-max** problem where the inner max is over demand/transport uncertainty.
   - *Example:* Hospital chains used robust stockpiles to ensure **95% service level** even under 50% supply disruption.

4. **GIS-Based Reverse Routing (3 marks)**
   - Uses **spatial decision support** to plan reverse collection routes dynamically.
   - *Example:* Thiruvananthapuram Municipal Corporation used GIS to map reverse flow of biomedical waste from COVID wards to incinerators.

> **Synthesis (extra credit):** These four methods work **synergistically** — the MILP designs the network, robust optimization hedges uncertainty, hybrid push-pull manages inventory, and GIS executes the route.

*(Marks distribution: ~1.75 per method, rounded to 7 total.)*

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Derivation** (already shown in Section 3.2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC(Q) &= \frac{D}{Q}A + \frac{rD}{Q}A_r + \frac{Q}{2}h \\
\frac{d\,TC}{dQ} &= -\frac{D(A + rA_r)}{Q^2} + \frac{h}{2} = 0 \\
Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2D(A + rA_r)}{h(1-r)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical substitution** with $D = 8{,}000$, $A = 400$, $A_r = 250$, $h = 15$, $r = 0.4$:

**Step 1 — Numerator inside the root**

$$2 \cdot 8{,}000 \cdot (400 + 0.4 \cdot 250) = 16{,}000 \times (400 + 100) = 16{,}000 \times 500 = 8{,}000{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Denominator inside the root**

$$15 \times (1 - 0.4) = 15 \times 0.6 = 9$$

**Step 3 — Optimal $Q^*$**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{8{,}000{,}000}{9}} = \sqrt{888{,}888.89} \approx 942.81 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum total cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC^* &= \sqrt{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 15 \times 500 \times 0.6} \\
     &= \sqrt{72{,}000{,}000} \\
     &= ₹8{,}485.28 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**
- $Q^* \approx 943$ units per cycle.
- The $A_r$ (return order) contribution increases the numerator by **25%** compared to a non-remanufacturing scenario.
- The $(1-r) = 0.6$ in the denominator reflects that **40% of demand is met by returns**, reducing net new order frequency.

**Final Boxed Answer:** $Q^* \approx 943$ units, $TC^* \approx ₹8{,}485$/yr.

*(+3 marks derivation, +2 marks numerical substitution, +2 marks interpretation.)*

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes — Read this BEFORE writing your exam!**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Method" with "Model":** A *Method* is the framework (e.g., 3PRL). A *Model* is the math (e.g., Newsvendor). A *Tool* is the software (e.g., Gurobi). Examiners deduct 1 mark if you swap these.
> 2. **Wrong Critical Fractile:** Always $F(Q^*) = \frac{C_u}{C_u + C_o}$, NOT $\frac{C_o}{C_u + C_o}$. The underage cost is in the **numerator** because higher $C_u$ means you should order more.
> 3. **Forgetting the $(1-r)$ in CL-EOQ:** If you write $Q^* = \sqrt{2D(A + rA_r)/h}$, you lose 2 marks. The denominator **must** be $h(1-r)$.
> 4. **Mixing GWP and CO₂e:** A GWP of 27.9 for methane means 1 kg CH₄ = 27.9 kg CO₂e, **not** 1 kg. Examiners will mark this wrong.
> 5. **Pandemic vs Disaster:** These are **not synonyms**. Pandemic = biological, long-tail, global. Disaster = geographic, sudden, localized. Choose the right model.
> 6. **No Units in Final Answer:** Always state units (units, ₹/yr, kg CO₂e). KTU deducts 0.5 mark for missing units.
